    Wherever his eagles appeared, there blew a potent spirit of modernity; wherever he passed with his victorious armies, he spread, of necessity, the ferment of the new ideas that smoothed the way to the future. And in every other respect, his entire life was a wholesome lesson: "one must become strong and be bold in order to overcome the strong."

    Thus in the very yoke he imposed lay the germ of liberty and redemption; the very good that accompanied his evil works was destined — strange to say! — to undermine his power and precipitate him into the abyss of Leipzig.

    Metternich was too keen an observer not to perceive that point of weakness. In his titanic enterprise of driving the human flock back toward the past, he took every care to be a tyrant — a dark tyrant and nothing else.

    In 1814, when the power of Napoleon, though declining rapidly, still appeared menacing and the aid of the peoples seemed indispensable to forestall surprises, Metternich and the other European diplomatists who took their cue from him made the most lavish promises of liberty to the Italians. It sufficed, to obtain it, that they should show themselves worthy of it by helping the Holy Alliance to break the bloody yoke of the French adventurer; by rallying round a banner that signified the happiness, the regeneration, of their Country.

    Once the Napoleonic peril had vanished, those deluded souls, divided in the choice of a prince, yet showed themselves agreed in openly demanding their independence.

    The replies that came, at long intervals, from the mouth of Metternich mark the various phases through which the nefarious work of subjugation passed, and would suffice, of themselves, to arouse hatred in the heart of any people that has feeling.

    — The affairs of the Italians will be settled in Vienna.

    — Austria will never consent to relinquish its rights of conquest.

    — Italy is a geographical expression.

    Unfortunately, Metternich was able to give force to his own pronouncements not only through diplomatic intrigues and through the arts of the Church, but also — and above all — through the armies of the Holy Alliance.

    The debt of gratitude toward the peoples being treated as non-existent, there remained to Austria that toward the princes. And perhaps, striking a line through even this last, she would have appropriated half the Peninsula for herself, had not the jealousies of Prussia and of Czar Alexander I arisen, most opportunely, to play, to however limited a degree, the game of the petty tyrants.

    Compelled to divide Italy into morsels to amortize that debt, Metternich took care to create about himself a cluster of satellites that would gravitate of necessity toward the great center of Vienna. A partial exception he proposed to make for Murat, for whom he reserved the kingdom of Naples. The wife of Murat, Napoleon's sister, had delighted him with kisses worth a kingdom... but the impetuous French general's untimely adoption of the role of champion of the Italians deprived Metternich of the opportunity to accomplish the only phosphorescent design of his entire political life.

    The kingdom of Naples fell to Ferdinando I, who had, moreover, prepared his way to it by lavishing nine million francs in the antechambers of the Congress of Vienna. It was an Austrian army that conducted him triumphantly to his capital.

    Rome and the Legations fell to Pius VII, the restorer of the power of the Jesuits; the future compiler of the Bull "Ecclesiam Jesu Christo" which excommunicated all those faithful who, knowing an affiliate of the Carboneria, did not degrade themselves by denouncing him — by playing the spy.

    The Grand Duchy of Tuscany and the principality of Piombino fell to Ferdinando III, an Austrian archduke; the Duchy of Modena to Francesco IV, surnamed by his subjects the "executioner." Maria Luisa, daughter of the Emperor of Austria and wife of Napoleon I, had to content herself with ruling at Parma; while the other Maria Luisa, Infanta of Spain, was placed over the principality of Lucca.

    Genoa, despite its protests and the eloquence of the Marchese Brignole, who sought to invoke its long centuries of liberty and its past greatness, was incorporated into Piedmont; upon whose throne, returning from his refuge in Sardinia, Vittorio Emanuele I took his seat.

    Austria retained under its direct dominion Lombardy, Venezia, the Tyrol, Istria, and Dalmatia.

    Having provided for securing Austrian preponderance by surrounding the empire with weak States, Metternich set himself to augment its power ever further by spreading a close-woven network of secret treaties. And by imposing, along with the obligation to adopt reactionary measures, the further obligation of reciprocal aid in the event of unrest, he reserved to himself, in substance, the right to intervene with arms in the affairs of each separate State.

    The sovereigns of Italy, so haughty and cruel toward their subjects, had, in order to reign, accepted the part of Austria's hired assassins. Austria, having had its direct dominion over the entire Peninsula taken from it by the jealousy of its faithful allies, had turned the difficulty and had succeeded in securing for itself an indirect dominion no less substantial. And the chains that weighed upon the wrists of the august Latin Mother were none the lighter nor the looser for it.

    By virtue of those Treaties, every Italian movement, even peaceful, aimed at provoking liberal reforms, was smothered in blood by the nefarious work of the army in white uniforms with pomaded mustaches; and not even Piedmont, whose Kings, not lacking in courage, had already given some fine proof of manly independence, was spared by the barbaric deluge.

    In the great historical periods, glad or sorrowful, there unfold incidents that epitomize them and may serve art in figuring them forth. The symbol of Italy in those times was as though embodied and enacted in the torture practiced under the diabolical inspiration of the Bourbon of Naples. An imprudent word, a suspicion, the hatred of a delator — the more ready for calumny in that the accused and the accuser were never confronted with one another — and an innocent man was torn from his family, stripped to the waist, bound astride a donkey, driven through the streets of the city.

    A company of Austrian soldiers preceded him to the sound of a drum, another followed behind; at his side was the gaoler wielding a scourge armed with nails, and at every step came blows that penetrated into the flesh and tore it away in shreds.

    The torture was suspended when the wretch, scarlet with blood, bowed his pale face and seemed about to breathe his last... Suspended, but to greater torment. It recommenced as soon as a groan, a convulsive movement of the muscles, revealed the revival of sensibility.

    The passers-by did not react; they covered their faces and fled in horror... At the end of the long Calvary, the gibbet! It was the agony of Christ renewed daily by the work of most Christian Kings allied together — under the auspices of the Pontiff — in holy alliance!

    More refined, but no less inexorable, was the oppression weighing upon those Italians subject to the direct dominion of the Empire. The air, impregnated with falsehoods, had begun to envelop tender childhood, which was taught, as its highest duty, as a religious obligation, servile submission to the tyrant; it penetrated the lungs of the university student, upon whom the institution was compelled to furnish the State not so much with learned men as with faithful subjects; it hung, subtle and dissolving, over every phase of social life. Free, the cherished subjects of His Majesty were, to move from house to house, from place to place; to attend to their affairs upon whose profits the Exchequer extended its rapacious claws... serfs in all besides; men resigned or swollen with rage... citizens never!

    Not for this did the repression of refractory spirits proceed without due respect for the forms prescribed by strict legality — an easy task where the Laws are promulgated by despotism, without the collaboration and assent of the governed. And with generous Italians, lawfully struck down, the fortress of the Spielberg in Moravia was peopled — that fortress which, for the groans suppressed by its walls, for the noble intellects that were withered and extinguished there, for the crimes against humanity that it concealed in the darkness of its underground chambers, shall rise horrible in the memory of men even when the last of the Habsburgs has been confounded, for centuries, with the dust.

    The flood of informers, high and low, who inundated the Kingdom provided admirably for the supply of innocent flesh to that Austrian Bastille, to the pillory, and to the flogging reenacted in its old forms. Bearing no distinguishing marks of recognition; concealed beneath plausible occupations, they insinuated themselves into every family, drew up reports on the tendencies, the habits, the conversations of every individual — even on the style of hat habitually preferred; they violated the postal secret; they spun plots; they besmirched or sought to besmirch the reputation of the most prominent and authoritative men, whose word might have incited men's souls to manly vindication of their Rights.

    The population of the Lombardo-Venetian Kingdom, emaciated and corroded by a monstrous fiscal exaction, monstrously applied, had to provide not only for the expenses of the local administration but to replenish the imperial coffers handsomely as well. So true is it that when you scrape the tyrant, the freebooter is laid bare!

    And the freebooter cast aside the refined and reptilian means to lay hold, like the other oppressors of Italy, of those barbarously ferocious, the moment the Milanese, refusing to smoke and to wear cloth manufactured by the foreigner, attempted to injure him in his purse. The prison gates were thrown wide, and the dregs of society, mingled with the police, roamed the streets of the metropolis, smoking and compelling others to smoke. The bold men who refused to press between their lips the leaf impregnated with German spittle were mutilated or run through with steel; and neither sex nor age offered any defense.

    Martial law became ordinary law; treason, punishable by harsh torments, was the wearing of cloth of certain colors, the singing of certain songs, the applauding of certain passages in this or that drama; the adorning of oneself with beard or hair cut in a given fashion; allowing oneself to be carried away by any act which the Authorities, entirely at their own discretion, deemed a demonstration of a subversive nature. "Three days of slaughter ensure thirty years of peace," said old Marshal Radetzky. And the pretext to perpetrate the slaughter was sought with the pertinacity of one who pushes the craving for dominion to the pitch of lust: of one who knows no other inspiration than the dark interests of the dynasty.

    Such were the delights that the well-groomed and smiling Metternich dispensed, with a hand more or less open, to a large part of Europe. And the Peoples escaped him by virtue of a quality inherent in the very arts that held them in bondage. For those arts of necessity aroused the hatred which is a regenerating energy, which drives men toward the luminous affirmations of Right as powerfully as, or more powerfully than, love.

    And so intensely did this energy stir the hearts of the Milanese that on the 18th of March, 1848, they found themselves united by a supreme intent; without distinction of class, without distinction of sex, without distinction of age... to kill or be killed; to conquer or to lie buried beneath the ruins of their own metropolis.

    No exhortations were required, no watchword, no leaders. The hundred and sixty thousand inhabitants of the city became one terrible unity... the PEOPLE; even as the thousands of millions of grains of powder constitute, upon exploding, the terrible unity of the powder magazine.

    Arms?

    They were drawn from beneath the earth, from within the thickness of walls, from the hiding-places where bold men, yearning for the day of vengeance, had smuggled them away... old implements of every description, rusted; to which the armorers, emptying their shops, soon added a supply of hunting-pieces. Then came the bludgeons, the rapiers, the halberds once gripped by mercenary soldiers; the great swords that perhaps broke the impetus of Barbarossa in the fine days of the Lombard League... the past issuing from the half-light of silent Museums, hurling itself into the vortex of the uprising, bringing its own contribution to the redemption of a People.

    That formidable tumult of bursting passions did not shatter itself against the walls of the Military Academy of San Luca; but, penetrating within, it aroused in our youthful souls a fever which, though suppressed, lost nothing of its intensity.

    On the first day of the insurrection, the Officers spoke to us with contempt of a handful of brigands whom the Austrian force, and the good sense of the "respectable" population itself, had soon enough brought back to their duty. Then... a hard face and silence. But that silence was worth, concentrated, the speech of several volumes.

    The energy that agitated the living and made them greater than themselves had passed all at once into things. In little more than a night, more than a thousand barricades sprang up and blocked the narrow streets of the city. The pavement had yielded its stones; from the palaces artistic furnishings came crashing down; the workman's house furnished its household goods, its mattresses, its pillows. Sanctified by the feverish hand of a people that willed it, even the humblest utensils had been transformed into a bulwark of liberty... shapeless bulwarks; their disorder proclaimed the fury of men's souls.

    The combatant who, pale, tormenting his carbine, waits at his post in ambush, rediscovers emotions refined almost to their purest essence. It was among that furniture that fathers, chastising him, had taught him to mouth secretly the name of Italy. And now that name he would cry aloud at the appearing of the enemy as a supreme provocation, as a challenge to the death; now he would prove that the ancient valor may languish in Italian fiber, but perish never. Conqueror or conquered, he would have accomplished a work not without greatness.

    The assault came, and at a hundred disparate points, simultaneously. But the Austrian found everywhere men whom desperation had taught the art of defense. The barricades creak and shift form under the fury of assailants and assailed, are drenched with the blood of one side and the other... blood... but they send back the imprecation of the barbarian who falls, the battle-cry of the citizen who triumphs.

    While the patriots fight in the foremost ranks, the mothers, the sisters, the children share the care of the wounded and prepare fresh means of attack. The cobblestones and tiles heaped upon the rooftops are transformed into deadly projectiles; the web of barricades extends and draws close about the barracks, the isolated garrisons. Besieged by the oppressor, the Milanese thus become, in part, the besiegers. And under the leaden sky, more insistent, more resonant than the battle itself, spread the peals of bells rung in alarm. Across the broad plain they spread the certainty that Milan is not beaten, that she still hopes and fights.

    On the morning of the fourth day, we knew that the garrisons within the ring of the Naviglio had surrendered; that Radetzky was compelled to confine himself to the outskirts of the city; that a Provisional Government had been constituted; that the insurgents, certain of victory and impatient to achieve it, had rejected the offer of an armistice; that the wind and the balloons — one of which had flown above our very Academy — had scattered through the countryside incitements to the uprising; that the cities of Lombardy and of the Veneto were in full revolt; that the Piedmontese army was already moving to lend its strength to the Italian cause; that armed bands were ranging through the countryside and making even the problem of retreat arduous for the old Marshal.

    In the afternoon of that same day, a committee of citizens, preceded by a white flag, came to the College and demanded the return to their families of the Milanese cadets. Then discipline availed no longer to set a barrier to our enthusiasm. Those who depart and those who remain embrace each other as brothers, and not without tears. Brief and solemn promises were murmured which not a few of those smooth-cheeked youths were later to seal with their blood.

    Not many hours passed before we were concentrated at the Monte Tabor, near Porta Romana. The order was to fall in behind the imperial army, which was filing along the bastions. Radetzky — the iron Radetzky — despite the resources of his own genius, found himself compelled to avert utter ruin by a retreat, dissembled but precipitous.

    Five days had the clash of virtue against the barbaric oppressor lasted. The latter, maddened by his own unexpected impotence, massacred old men and children, buried prisoners alive; bound body to body and burned alive lovers and husbands; bloodied the Sforza castle by severing, for sport, the limbs and the breasts of the gentle and majestic Lombard women.

    The Milanese people had, in conquering, inaugurated the period of history exuberant in enthusiasms and disillusionments, in victories and defeats, in tumults and silences, from which — as from a laboring womb — our Nation was pouring forth to new life, to the tremors of Liberty and of Glory!

    Parte Prima

    Capitolo Terzo

    Part One

    Chapter Three

    Il giorno stesso della nostra fuga, ebbi il primo tuffo nella vita intensa... e doveva essere decisivo.

    Sfilavano i tedeschi sui bastioni che corrono da Porta San Celso a Porta Romana, stanchi, avviliti e torvi ad un tempo. E poiché noi dovevamo metterci alle loro spalle, assistevamo, a piè fermo, a quel loro sgombro forzato. Dai bastioni si dominava la città. Qua e là si scorgevano case in fiamme; frammezzo ai tetti, la bianca cattedrale giganteggiava e i tedeschi mostravano il pugno al tricolore che sventolava sul suo pinnacolo maggiore. Le campane continuavano a suonare a stormo.

    Tra i bastioni e il folto delle case, verdeggiava, a quell'epoca, una distesa di orti... vi dominava una quiete strana in quella metropoli corsa e ricorsa dall'onda impetuosa delle passioni. Solitario presso i bastioni, sorgeva il casolare degli agricoltori. Un bimbo, sereno come il lembo di terra che, probabilmente, l'aveva visto nascere, ne uscì correndo e, arrampicandosi sulla verde rampa, si fermò in muta contemplazione, sotto gli ippocastani che sovrastano le mura. Poi, come attratto dal meraviglioso mondo delle cose minime, si chinò e le tenui dita scherzarono tra i sassolini che luccicavano ai suoi piedi.

    Le truppe continuavano a sfilare, ogni Compagnia preceduta da tre Croati che facevano da battistrada. Uno di questi sbirciò il bimbo, abbassò il fucile e, ritrattolo a maggior slancio, vibrò una puntata, come se si trattasse d'infilare un selvatico allo spiedo. La baionetta penetrò nel fianco e uscì dalla schiena di quell'innocente che, alzato di peso dall'impeto feroce, spalancò la bocca. Ma l'urlo, intercettato dalla morte, non venne.

    La mamma del piccino era ad una finestra del casolare solitario e lo vide e urlò per lui. Ella accorse, arrampicandosi a stento sul verde declivio... greve le riesciva il ventre nuovamente turgido e fecondo. La derelitta non giunse a stringere tra le braccia la tenera spoglia. Non appena fu sopra, gli aguzzini d'Italia vibrarono un'altra baionettata ed ella precipitava riversa, cadavere con un cadavere in seno.

    N'ebbi la febbre, n'ebbi il delirio. In procinto di spianare il fucile, alla cieca, contro gli assassini, sentii la mano ferrea di mio fratello Achille che mi tratteneva. Egli mi sussurrò nell'orecchio una frase che, pur non distinguendola, compresi. Allora alzai istintivamente la faccia, quasi aspettassi dall'alto la vendetta. Il cielo era plumbeo, immoto... Dio, se esiste, doveva essere assorto nelle faccende di un mondo migliore.

    Fu allora che nel mio cuore incominciò a farsi strada la persuasione che nessuna salvezza hanno i derelitti fuorché nel risveglio delle proprie energie ribelli. "Agli sciocchi — ne conclusi — l'imbelle preghiera; ai forti che fortemente vogliono, l'azione."

    La nostra ritirata da Milano a Verona non fu di tal natura da affievolire quella terribile impressione. Radetzky aveva dato ordine alle guarnigioni stanziate qua e là nelle varie parti del Regno, di contrarsi sul grosso dell'esercito. In pari tempo, a scongiurare il pericolo d'essere tormentato da un nemico che gli decimasse le forze e gli sfuggisse di mano, inafferrabile come uno sciame di zanzare, ingiunse lo sgombro d'ogni terra, grossa o piccina, situata sul suo passaggio. Al minimo accenno alla resistenza, le cannonate e gli orrori del saccheggio.

    Le varie guarnigioni scesero pressochè indisturbate, a tempo per rifornire le forze del Radetzky affievolite dalle continue diserzioni. La confusione, il panico degli Imperiali erano tali che, giunti a mala pena a Lodi, ogni loro disciplina appariva effimera. Se Carlo Alberto, invece di perdere un tempo prezioso brigando per soddisfare le esigenze della Diplomazia avesse, come folgore, passato il Ticino, la ritirata degenerava in rotta e forse l'Austria, perduto l'esercito d'Italia, più non si rialzava.

    Quanto alla popolazione, priva d'armi, traviata da notizie incerte, contradditorie, né sapeva, né poteva risolversi a tentare cose grandi. Tuttavia l'odio inveterato e lo spirito virile, qua e là, proruppero. Melegnano, che si era lusingata d'intercettare la strada al nemico, fu tosto ridotta in soggezione da cinquanta pezzi d'artiglieria. Io vidi i Croati che la saccheggiavano, ubbriachi e selvaggi, gettare dalle finestre donne scapigliate e fanciulli senza difesa.

    Giorno non passava, che non dovessi assistere a scene feroci e codarde, spesso affatto improvocate e l'ira repressa, tanto più rapidamente temperava il mio patriottismo.

    Esso ebbe un primo scoppio a Desenzano. La guarnigione di Brescia, capitanata da un de Domini, mio zio materno, aveva operato la congiunzione con Radetzky in quella località. Saputo della presenza dei due nipoti, volle vederli. Egli ci interrogò sulle nostre opinioni politiche. Era lo zio un austriacante arrabbiato ed io non lo ignoravo. Mi espressi con linguaggio vibrato e libero.

    Il vecchio militare impallidì e mi interruppe. "Questi — disse — sono i maledetti sentimenti di tuo padre. Egli te li ha infiltrati nel sangue. Povero stolto! In che ti affidi? in che speri? Credi tu che quattro villani armati di tridente e di bastone basteranno a far paura all'esercito più agguerrito del mondo; a mettere in forse la stabilità dell'impero?" Mi accontentai di accennare freddamente alla ritirata del Radetzky; alla rivoluzione prorompente da un capo all'altro dell'usurpato dominio.

    "Ah se non fossi tuo zio... — mormorò tra i denti. Ringrazia tua madre; essa ti salva! Ma tant'è!... o tosto o tardi lascerai la testa tra le mani del carnefice!"

    Ed io sorridendo: "Preferisco l'affrontare cento volte il carnefice, all'essere un turpe rinnegato, come sei tu!"

    Partì sbuffando e più non lo rividi.

    Ma quelle erano semplici parole. Un altro terribile incidente venne presto a colmare la misura, a dare la piega fatale alla mia esistenza.

    L'esercito aveva lasciato alle spalle Peschiera, quando informatori giunsero ad annunciare che la piccola Castelnuovo osava barricarsi. Gli animosi argomentando, a ragione, che Radetzky non potesse giungere al forte riparo di Verona senza passare per la loro località, accolsero il monito di Carlo Alberto, che incitava ad incagliare la ritirata, e decisero di opporre il petto alle armi, di offrire sé stessi in olocausto alla Patria.

    In quei tempi d'eroico entusiasmo, non si discuteva troppo sulla probabilità del successo. Più grande, più certo il sacrificio e più forte il pungolo che sospingeva a sobbarcarvisi.

    L'artiglieria imperiale ebbe presto ragione del patriottismo italiano. Castelnuovo crivellata, in fiamme, venne abbandonata alla libidine della soldatesca. Noi, cadetti dell'Accademia, si assisteva a quello scempio schierati presso la porta delle vecchie mura, all'estremità di una piazza ellittica. E come i Croati, nel loro cieco furore, non miravano precisamente il segno, avevamo cercato un riparo dietro parecchie vetture abbandonate.

    D'improvviso, vidi sbucare da una casa in fiamme una giovinetta coi capelli sciolti, scalza, mal coperta da una semplice camicia... evidentemente un'ammalata sorpresa dalla furia dell'incendio e degli uomini. Ed ecco, dalla casa stessa, prorompere, con l'arme in pugno, il sozzo croato che l'inseguiva.

    Pensai: "Se quel cane l'arriva, me la sgozza!" E seguendo un impulso irresistibile, già mi lanciavo per soccorrerla, quando un altro soldataccio, immemore che anche nel suo paese erano madri e sorelle trepidanti, spianò la carabina e fece fuoco. La giovinetta cadde e una striscia scarlatta rigò la candida tela che l'avvolgeva.

    Un'imprecazione, un balzo e le ero d'accanto, tremante di pietà e di rabbia. Al tocco delle mie mani, che le sollevavano la testa, ella aperse gli occhi; ma vista appena l'odiata uniforme, ebbe un brivido e torse la faccia altrove... la faccia pallida ove, più risaltante dei segni profondi della fine che sovrastava, dominò un'espressione di estremo ribrezzo.

    "Non temere. Sono dei tuoi; sono italiano anch'io!" e le toccavo le carni per cercare la ferita.

    L'infelice accennò al cuore, sospirò e le giovani membra furono prese dall'ineffabile rilassatezza della morte. Mi fermai a contemplarla. Era di una bellezza resa ai miei occhi sublime dall'aureola del martirio. Allora fu come se quelle labbra mi parlassero un linguaggio appassionato e vibrante. La mia emozione ne divenne prepotente. Io ne soffersi come se accogliessi nell'animo tutti quanti i dolori della Patria e un impeto di propositi nuovi si sprigionò improvvisamente dal profondo. Né mai, dopo quel giorno, invocai, nei momenti di pericolo, il santo ideale della Libertà che, a rinnovare quell'impeto, non mi ricomparissero, nette nella mente, le delicate sembianze di quella giovinetta sospinta nell'eterno silenzio dal piombo dell'oppressore, inconsciamente grande, tragica e bella.

    Scese la notte e fu l'ultima per qualche aguzzino che si aspettava d'assaporare ancora a lungo il succo dei nostri grappoli. Le livide labbra non palesarono segreti. Né si indagò tanto pel sottile. A che prò in tempi di confusione inestricabile? Ma io posso parlare adesso senza timori e senza spavalderia. Fu quella notte che le mie mani e quelle d'Achille conobbero il primo sangue. Atto in difesa della mia razza risorgente, atto di Giustizia!.. L'incinta madre lombarda e la giovinetta di Castelnuovo non rimasero a lungo invendicate!

    Source pp. 17–19Scan to view on another deviceOn the very day of our flight, I had my first plunge into the life of intensity... and it was to prove decisive.

    The Germans were filing along the bastions that run from Porta San Celso to Porta Romana, weary, dispirited, and sullen all at once. And since we were obliged to take our place at their rear, we stood our ground and watched that forced withdrawal of theirs. From the bastions, one commanded the whole city. Here and there, houses could be discerned in flames; amid the rooftops, the white cathedral towered aloft, and the Germans shook their fists at the tricolor that flew from its highest pinnacle. The bells went on ringing the tocsin.

    Edgren 1901orto — ortense: of a kitchen-garden (Eng. hortensial)Edgren confirms the period-specific compound 'kitchen-garden' for 'orto', which was preferred over the modern 'vegetable garden' or Gemini's plain 'gardens'. This preserves the 19th-century English horticultural register.Edgren 1901tenui — ténue: THIN (slender); slight (poor, insignificant)Edgren lists 'slight' as a sense of 'tenue'. This was preferred over Gemini's 'slender' because 'slight' better conveys the child's smallness and fragility rather than mere thinness, matching the pathos of the scene.Between the bastions and the dense mass of houses, there stretched at that time, still green, an expanse of kitchen-gardens... a strange quietude held sway there, amidst that great city swept and re-swept by the impetuous tide of passions. Solitary near the bastions stood the farmsteaders' cottage. A child, as serene as the strip of earth that had in all likelihood seen him born, came running out and, climbing the green slope, stopped in silent contemplation beneath the horse-chestnuts that overhang the walls. Then, as though drawn by the marvelous world of small things, he bent down, and his slight fingers played among the little stones that glittered at his feet.

    Gemini Prothat innocent soul whoThe Italian 'quell'innocente' is a substantivised adjective, not a noun phrase; dropping 'soul' is more faithful and avoids sentimentality the original does not have.Gemini Prothe cry, cut off by death'Intercettato' literally means 'intercepted'; 'cut off' is a looser paraphrase. 'Intercepted' preserves the striking personification of death catching the cry mid-flight.The troops continued to file past, each Company preceded by three Croatians serving as outriders. One of these cast a sidelong glance at the child, lowered his rifle, and drawing it back for greater force, drove a thrust forward, as though it were a matter of running a wild creature onto a spit. The bayonet entered the flank and came out through the back of that innocent who, lifted bodily by the ferocious impetus, opened wide his mouth. But the cry, intercepted by death, never came.

    Gemini Proher belly, heavy and swollen with new life, made it hard goingGemini supplied 'fecund' for 'fecondo', which is more exact than Claude's explanatory 'with new life'. The final reworks the syntax into an inverted construction closer to the Italian word order 'greve le riesciva il ventre'.The little one's mother was at a window of the solitary cottage and she saw it and screamed for him. She came running, clambering with effort up the green slope... heavy upon her was the belly, swollen anew and fecund. The wretched woman did not reach him in time to gather the tender remains in her arms. No sooner had she arrived than the tormentors of Italy drove in another bayonet thrust, and she fell backwards, a corpse with a corpse in her womb.

    I was seized with fever, with delirium. On the point of leveling my rifle blindly at the murderers, I felt the iron hand of my brother Achille restraining me. He whispered a phrase in my ear which, though I could not make it out, I understood. Then I raised my face instinctively, as though awaiting vengeance from on high. The sky was leaden, motionless... God, if He exists, must have been absorbed in the affairs of some better world.

    Gemini Prothe impotent prayer'Imbelle' means 'unwarlike, weak' — 'feeble' is closer to this passive weakness than 'impotent', which carries connotations of frustrated desire not present in the Italian.Gemini Proto the strong who will with strengthMirrors the Italian adverb-verb construction 'fortemente vogliono' more closely and has better rhetorical rhythm.It was then that in my heart there began to take hold the conviction that the wretched have no salvation save in the awakening of their own rebellious energies. "To fools," I concluded, "the feeble prayer; to the strong who strongly will, action."

    Our retreat from Milan to Verona was not of a nature to weaken that terrible impression. Radetzky had given orders to the garrisons posted here and there throughout the various parts of the Kingdom to contract upon the main body of the army. At the same time, in order to forestall the danger of being harassed by an enemy that might decimate his forces and slip through his fingers, elusive as a swarm of gnats, he commanded the evacuation of every town, large or small, lying in his line of march. At the slightest sign of resistance, cannonades and the horrors of sacking.

    Gemini Proa fragile thingDirect cognate of the Italian 'effimera'. 'Fragile' shifts the meaning from transience to breakability.Gemini Prowould never have recovered'Più non si rialzava' literally means 'would no longer rise again'; 'recovered' is too clinical and loses the physical metaphor of standing back up.The various garrisons descended almost unmolested, arriving in time to replenish Radetzky's forces, which had been weakened by continual desertions. The confusion and panic among the Imperialists were such that, having barely reached Lodi, all discipline among them appeared ephemeral. Had Carlo Alberto, instead of wasting precious time in scheming to satisfy the demands of Diplomacy, crossed the Ticino like a thunderbolt, the retreat would have degenerated into a rout, and perhaps Austria, having lost the army of Italy, would never have risen again.

    As for the population, unarmed, misled by uncertain and contradictory reports, it neither knew how nor could bring itself to attempt great deeds. Yet inveterate hatred and a virile spirit broke forth here and there. Melegnano, which had flattered itself that it might cut off the enemy's road, was swiftly reduced to submission by fifty pieces of artillery. I saw the Croatians sacking it, drunk and savage, flinging dishevelled women and defenseless children from the windows.

    Not a day passed but I was obliged to witness scenes of ferocity and cowardice, often entirely unprovoked, and the suppressed rage tempered my patriotism all the more swiftly.

    rabid *austriacante* and I was well awareRestores a gloss for the untranslated term; Claude's draft glosses austriacante as a zealous partisan of Austria so the meaning is not lost to the English reader.It found a first outburst at Desenzano. The garrison of Brescia, commanded by one De Domini, my maternal uncle, had effected its junction with Radetzky at that place. Learning of the presence of his two nephews, he wished to see them. He questioned us as to our political opinions. The uncle was a rabid austriacante — a zealous partisan of Austria — and I was well aware of it. I expressed myself in bold and unrestrained language.

    Gemini ProThe old military man'Il vecchio militare' is more naturally rendered as 'the old soldier' in English; 'military man' is a stiff calque.The old soldier went pale and interrupted me. "These," he said, "are the accursed sentiments of your father. He has instilled them into your blood. Poor fool! In what do you trust? In what do you hope? Do you believe that four peasants armed with pitchfork and cudgel will suffice to frighten the most battle-hardened army in the world; to call into question the stability of the empire?" I contented myself with coolly alluding to Radetzky's retreat; to the revolution bursting forth from one end to the other of the usurped dominion.

    "Ah, were you not my nephew..." he muttered between his teeth. "Thank your mother; she is what saves you! But so be it!... sooner or later you will leave your head in the hands of the headsman!"

    And I, smiling: "I would sooner face the headsman a hundred times over than be a vile renegade, as you are!"

    Gemini ProHe left in a fury'Partì sbuffando' — 'sbuffando' means puffing or snorting with irritation. 'Departed' from Gemini is closer to 'partì' than 'left'; 'fuming' (novel synthesis) captures the huffing physicality of 'sbuffando' better than either draft's attempt.He departed fuming, and I never saw him again.

    But those were mere words. Another terrible incident soon came to fill the measure to overflowing, to give the fateful turn to my existence.

    Gemini Prothe shelter of VeronaThe Italian has 'il forte riparo di Verona'; 'forte' (strong) was omitted by Claude. Gemini correctly preserved it.Gemini Protheir own bodies to armed force'Il petto' means 'the breast/chest', not 'bodies'. 'Oppose their breasts' is the correct idiom and preserves the heroic physicality of the Italian.The army had left Peschiera behind when informants arrived to report that the small town of Castelnuovo dared to barricade itself. The spirited inhabitants, reasoning — rightly — that Radetzky could not reach the strong shelter of Verona without passing through their locality, heeded the call of Carlo Alberto, who urged them to obstruct the retreat, and resolved to oppose their breasts to armed force, to offer themselves in holocaust to the Fatherland.

    In those days of heroic enthusiasm, one did not deliberate overmuch upon the probability of success. The greater and more certain the sacrifice, the sharper the spur that drove one to embrace it.

    Gemini Prowatched that devastation'Scempio' means slaughter or carnage, not devastation. Gemini's 'carnage' is semantically accurate.Gemini Proseveral abandoned carts'Vetture' means carriages or vehicles, not carts. 'Carriages' is the correct translation.The imperial artillery soon made short work of Italian patriotism. Castelnuovo, riddled with shot and in flames, was abandoned to the license of the soldiery. We, cadets of the Academy, watched that carnage drawn up near the gate of the old walls, at one end of an elliptical piazza. And as the Croatians, in their blind fury, did not aim with any great precision, we had sought cover behind several abandoned carriages.

    All at once, I saw a young girl burst from a burning house, her hair unbound, barefoot, scarcely covered by a simple shift... evidently someone taken ill, surprised by the fury of the fire and of men. And behold, from that same house, a filthy Croatian came rushing out, weapon in hand, in pursuit of her.

    I thought: "If that cur catches her, he will cut her throat!" And following an irresistible impulse, I was already throwing myself forward to come to her aid, when another brutish soldier, forgetful that in his own country too there were mothers and sisters trembling with dread, leveled his carbine and fired. The young girl fell, and a scarlet streak ran across the white linen that enveloped her.

    Gemini ProA curse, a leap'Imprecazione' is a cognate best rendered as 'imprecation'; 'curse' is too colloquial for Crespi's elevated register.An imprecation, a leap, and I was at her side, trembling with pity and with rage. At the touch of my hands lifting her head, she opened her eyes; but no sooner had she glimpsed the hated uniform than she shuddered and turned her face away... that pallid face on which, standing out more sharply than the deep marks of the end that hung over her, there reigned an expression of utter revulsion.

    Gemini ProI moved my hands over her to find the woundThe Italian 'le toccavo le carni' specifically says 'I touched her flesh'; Claude's paraphrase loses the raw physicality of 'carni' (flesh)."Do not be afraid. I am one of your own; I too am Italian!" and I touched her flesh to find the wound.

    The unhappy girl gestured towards her heart, sighed, and her young limbs were overtaken by the ineffable lassitude of death. I remained to contemplate her. She was of a beauty rendered sublime in my eyes by the aureole of martyrdom. Then it was as though those lips spoke to me in a passionate and vibrant tongue. My emotion grew overpowering. I suffered as though I were receiving into my soul all the sorrows of the Fatherland, and a surge of new resolves broke loose suddenly from the depths. Nor ever, after that day, in moments of danger, did I invoke the sacred ideal of Liberty but that, to renew that surge, there reappeared before me, sharp and clear in my mind, the delicate features of that young girl driven into eternal silence by the lead of the oppressor — unconsciously great, tragic, and beautiful.

    Gemini Prothe juice of our vineyards'Il succo dei nostri grappoli' — 'grappoli' means grape-clusters, not vineyards. Gemini's 'grapes' is literal and correct.Gemini Promy rising race'Risorgente' is best rendered as 'resurgent', which carries the specific resonance of the Risorgimento. 'Rising' is too generic.Night fell, and it was the last night for certain tormentors who had expected to savour much longer the juice of our grapes. Livid lips gave up no secrets. Nor was the matter examined too closely. To what end, in times of inextricable confusion? But I can speak now without fear and without bravado. It was that night that my hands and Achille's came to know their first blood. An act in defense of my resurgent race, an act of Justice!.. The pregnant Lombard mother and the young girl of Castelnuovo did not long remain unavenged!

    Parte Prima

    Capitolo Quarto

    Part One

    Chapter Four

    Riparato e salvo in Verona, il vecchio Maresciallo offerse ai cadetti di entrare, come graduati, nell'esercito regolare, accordando loro il privilegio di scegliere il Reggimento. Nessuno accettò. Fummo internati sino a Gratz e incorporati a quell'Accademia Militare; finché il Ministro della Guerra, accogliendo una nostra istanza, ci permise di far ritorno alle nostre case.

    Giunse il Settembre. Il valoroso esercito piemontese, abbandonato a sé stesso per la defezione dei principi italiani: rintuzzato ne' suoi impeti dalle infermità psichiche del proprio Re, vinto più dalla deficienza della propria organizzazione che dal valore austriaco, era stato costretto a sloggiare dalla Venezia, dai Ducati, dalla Lombardia; a ritirarsi dietro la linea del Ticino. Milano era ricaduta in possesso degli oppressori... Finita, virtualmente, la guerra regia, continuava, secondo l'espressione allora in voga, quella del Popolo.

    Fra le individualità monde da ogni fregio ufficiale che più si distinsero quell'anno, per valore e per interezza d'animo, era un patriota amicissimo della mia famiglia: Pietro Fortunato Calvi...

    Con pochi montanari e con armi quasi primitive, aveva difeso il Cadore dai primi di Maggio ai primi del Giugno, incidendo negli annali della nostra epopea i nomi di Ampezzo, di Termine, di Rivalgo, di Chiusa di Venas, del passo della Morte, di Selva, del Rindimera e la funesta Mauria.

    In questa stupefacente campagna, che certi storici saltano con rapidità anche più stupefacente, Pietro Fortunato Calvi respinse gli attacchi separati dapprima, combinati poi, dati dagli Austriaci alle tre porte naturali del Cadore. Quando alla terza fase della guerra, depose le armi, fu necessità creata dal completo esaurimento dei viveri e delle munizioni. Né in quell'epoca, così facile ad attribuire i rovesci al tradimento, sorse una sola voce a menomare la gloria dell'eroe. Riparò egli, per vie inusitate, a Venezia e, ovunque appariva, era accolto, non come vinto, ma come trionfatore.

    Sulle sue orme, per la via di Portogruaro, l'unica rimasta aperta, entrai a Venezia io pure, unitamente a mio fratello Achille. Il Calvi ci ammise subito a far parte di quella legione dei Cacciatori delle Alpi, il cui nome divenne sinonimo di eroismo e di gloria.

    La fine del 1848 e i primi mesi dell'anno successivo, ebbi così l'opportunità di assistere ad uno spettacolo meraviglioso. Nella mia lunga esistenza non ne conobbi altri più efficaci ad esaltare l'anima umana... Un popolo che, per forza di volontà, ritempra la propria fibra; deterge nei disagi, nelle strette della fame, negli orrori del morbo, nel lavacro del proprio sangue, l'onta delle secolari lascivie, la codarda rassegnazione al servaggio.

    Nella distruzione, i bagliori della vita che si rinnova, della grandezza che si riafferma.

    In quei mesi, oltre alla mia parte delle sofferenze comuni, provai un dolore intimo, intenso. Mio fratello Achille ebbe i santi propositi, ebbe le calde speranze inesorabilmente troncate dal morbo. Giunsi a baciargli la gelida fronte ed a me parve che, al contatto delle mie labbra, nell'inerte cadavere si avvivasse come un rimasuglio d'affetti. Credo che, solo da quel momento, il giovine patriota cessasse totalmente di fremere.

    Personalmente, partecipai ad una sola fazione importante: la sortita verso Mestre che rifornì la città di foraggi e di carni. In quell'occasione vidi, per la prima volta, senza parlargli, un uomo che doveva avere una grande influenza sul mio avvenire: Felice Orsini.

    Due gravi infrazioni alla disciplina mi tolsero prematuramente a quel teatro di sacrifici e di grandezza. Ero stato assegnato alla difesa dei forti di Marghera, lunetta numero tredici. Compito tedioso; poiché Cavedalis, il Ministro della Guerra, col pretesto che le munizioni difettassero, ci aveva imposto di non far fuoco. Assistevamo neghittosamente al febbrile lavoro degli Austriaci che, concentrati a Mestre, scavavano le loro parallele e si preparavano al bombardamento. Quella nostra inazione li aveva tanto inorgogliti, da indurli, un giorno, ad avanzarsi, lungo il canale, sino ad un tiro di fucile dai forti... e da laggiù, ferirono ad un braccio la sentinella della mia lunetta.

    Gli ordini del Ministro e il Ministro stesso sparirono dalla mia mente, per non dire dalla faccia del globo... strappo la miccia dalle mani del cannoniere e il colpo partì. Immediatamente, come se la mia furia si fosse propagata colla celerità dell'elettrico, tutti i cannoni dei forti tuonarono... i pochi nemici che rimasero ritti ebbero l'ali al piede.

    Pietro Fortunato Calvi in persona perorò la mia causa dinanzi al Consiglio di Guerra. Egli addusse, a mia difesa, l'ignoranza degli ordini, l'età, l'amor di Patria, l'impeto irresistibile e, per allora, fui salvo. Ma alla seconda infrazione, le animosità politiche resero la mia situazione insostenibile.

    Il fatale dualismo tra repubblicani e monarchici, che nel 1848-49, fu tanta parte dei nostri rovesci, aveva necessariamente il suo contraccolpo anche a Venezia. Repubblicana dapprima, annessa poi al monarchico Piemonte, essa aveva nuovamente assunto reggimento repubblicano, quando la guerra regia parve irremissibilmente perduta. L'alta borghesia che, sin d'allora, si arrogava l'esclusiva del buon senso pratico, non si acconciava di buona voglia al verdetto popolare e parecchi Ministri la spalleggiavano.

    D'onde, misure ibride che irritavano la moltitudine e la sospingevano frequentemente a radunarsi in piazza San Marco ed a rumoreggiare.

    Nel Marzo del 1849 (il giorno non lo rammento) l'esasperazione, giunta al colmo, pareva in procinto di degenerare in aperta rivolta. A prevenirla, il Cavedalis impartì l'ordine, a Pietro Fortunato Calvi, di disperdere, ad ogni costo, la folla e il Calvi, distaccate parecchie pattuglie della sua legione, fece incombere l'ingrato dovere sui sotto Ufficiali. Senonché, al momento di partire, ci chiamò in disparte e, sicuro d'essere compreso, pronunciò queste testuali parole: "Rammentatevi che a Venezia, più non comandano i Croati."

    Io percorsi a capo della mia pattuglia, buon tratto della città, senza trovare intoppi; ma sbucato appena sulla piazza San Marco, mi trovai di fronte un mare tempestoso. La nostra uniforme verde spigata di bianco e il pennacchio cascante attrassero subito l'attenzione generale ed una voce vibrante gridò: "Vedeteli! Vengono per pigliarci a fucilate!"

    Misi il kepi sulla punta della daga e agitandolo a mo' di saluto, gridai a pieni polmoni: "Noi siamo qui per difendere il Popolo, non per tiranneggiarlo!"

    Addio repressione! La folla si aperse per lasciarci libero il passo e, mutati d'un subito i suoi sentimenti, vociava da mille e mille gole: "Evviva i Cacciatori delle Alpi!" Persi e come assorbiti da quel mare burrascoso, finimmo per diventarne parte integrante.

    Per fortuna, alle nostre armi supplì, coll'autorità e coll'eloquenza, Daniele Manin. Io ho ancora dinanzi agli occhi la sua faccia assomigliante, come goccia a goccia, a quella del Cavour, benché l'animo suo fosse tanto alieno da ogni doppiezza diplomatica. I concetti da lui espressi posso ripetere, oggi ancora, quasi colle sue identiche parole. Esortò a non inacerbire le miserie della Patria, la quale reclamava, nelle sue strettezze, la concordia di tutti i suoi figli. Dividerla, menomare la forza, era delitto.

    Compito urgente, compito supremo quello di debellare lo straniero, di spezzare i ceppi che si apprestavano alla Repubblica. Si penserebbe poi, a rassettare le dissensioni interne; quando il glorioso vessillo sventolerebbe, una volta ancora, su terre e su uomini liberi. Che non avrebbe potuto allora, un popolo cosciente de' propri diritti, ebbro d'entusiasmo, ingagliardito dalla vittoria? Quali raggiri, quale lusinga sarebbe valsa a rapirgli, anche solo in parte, il frutto di tanti sacrifici? "Tornate alle vostre case, ritempratevi alle carezze della madre, della sposa, dei figli trepidanti... povero gran Popolo, così duramente provato alla scuola dell'avversità! Pensate che il cannone può, da un momento all'altro, strapparvi a quelle carezze, reclamare la vostra presenza colà dove si combatte, dove si muore. Nessun Italiano degno di tal nome, si deve rifiutare di presentarsi incontaminato e senza rimorsi, all'estremo sacrificio!"

    Tacque e quel Popolo forte e quel Popolo buono, a mano a mano, si sbandò. Di quanta maggior gloria riuscirebbe anche oggi, la saggia, l'umana parola ai Governanti che tanto leggermente la pospongono all'armi fratricide!

    Il giorno seguente, il colonnello Calvi mi mandò a chiamare. Lo trovai più triste che accigliato. "Il Ministro Cavedalis — mi disse — è su tutte le furie. Venezia è piena di delatori, come ai tempi della Signoria ed egli ha saputo, per filo e per segno, quanto... quanto è accaduto. Esige il nome dei sotto Ufficiali che comandavano le pattuglie; specialmente quello — mi intendi? — quello di un sergente imberbe che pronunciò frasi sovversive e che egli vuole punito. La scappata è contemplata dai regolamenti militari..."

    Mi accinsi a raffazzonare una difesa; ma egli mi interruppe con un gesto. Il superiore non volle e non poteva entrare in quella discussione; ma il patriota stese la destra al patriota e mi diede una stretta che parlò quanto un intero volume.

    "È la seconda del genere — continuò — e non vi sarebbe remissione. Sergente di Rudio, bisogna lasciare Venezia!.. troverai nemici da combattere in ogni lembo d'Italia. Roma ha bisogno di difensori e il Governo della Repubblica, a sollecitazione di quei Triumviri, invia colà i legionari dell'Unione, venuti fra noi dopo il volta faccia di Papa Pio. Venezia ha più bisogno di pane che di militi e il colonnello Ferrara, che guida i legionari, salperà, appunto domani, da Chioggia. Eccoti una lettera per lui... sarai ben accolto. Va, te lo comando!"

    Feci il saluto militare e partii. L'imprevveduto m'aspettava. Il Ministro Cavedalis aveva già inviato a Chioggia l'ordine di arrestare, e ricondurre a Venezia, quanti soldati non appartenessero all'Unione, o non mostrassero un permesso firmato dal comandante della Piazza. Non passeggiai a lungo per le vie di Chioggia.

    La caserma in cui mi rinchiusero fiancheggia una via che mette capo alla marina. Un secondo bastava per lanciarsi dal portone e scantonare per quella via; bastavano quattro salti da scoiattolo a percorrerla e a giungere alla spiaggia. Nel cortile della caserma passeggiavano, alla rinfusa, legionari d'ogni regione e d'ogni uniforme... sul portone spalancato, era di guardia una sentinella, anzi un pisellone che, giudicando così ad occhio e croce, pareva sognasse le buone zuppe del suo paese. Piano, piano, come se altro non mi premesse che lo svagarmi un po' osservando i viandanti, gli giunsi al fianco. Allora agitai le braccia e gli gridai con voce soffocata: "Guarda! Scappa... scappa da quella finestra!"

    Egli guarda a sinistra, io do un balzo a dritta, scantono, sono fuori di tiro. Oh le acque azzurre dell'Adriatico, oh i trabiccoli bruni, dondolanti mollemente sulle onde, leggeri come gusci!.. i trabiccoli lambiti dall'ala dei gabbiani, risonanti d'opere e di canti! Ne avevo di fronte una quarantina già gremiti di legionari, già manovranti per la partenza imminente.

    Mi appiatto in una barchetta che, spinta da due robusti rematori, taglia l'onde celere come una freccia. Ma, mentre mi arrampico sui fianchi di una nave, un ufficiale scorse, dalla spiaggia, l'uniforme verde spigata di bianco. Provvidenziale mi riuscì, in quel frangente, la manovra di spostamento della mobile flottiglia. L'ufficiale aveva indubbiamente fissato, coll'occhio, il trabaccolo sul quale avevo cercato rifugio; ma, nel tragitto, si confuse e salì, per le sue ricerche, colà dove io non ero. Si intestò ad ispezionare una seconda nave, poi una terza e capitò, finalmente, sulla mia... ma già io avevo trovato il modo di indossare un'uniforme dell'Unione. Mi misi in rango cogli altri legionari ed egli, passandomi ad un palmo dal naso, si degnò appena di guardarmi.

    Tuttavia non dovevo metter piede in Roma senza percorrere un largo circuito. Numerosi combattenti avevano, come me, lasciato di sotterfugio Venezia, non per codardia, ma perché attratti, da varie considerazioni, verso il cuore d'Italia. Se non che, giunti a Ravenna, il colonnello Ferrara, accampando non so quali ordini perentori, rifiutò di arruolarci e organizzatici in manipolo, ci fece scortare sino a Bologna, sotto rigida sorveglianza, quasi fossimo prigionieri austriaci. Nostra destinazione definitiva, Venezia. Ma ero cosciente d'aver impugnato l'armi per spezzare catene, non per lasciarmele imporre... decisi di affrontare le vicissitudini di una nuova fuga.

    Come unico graduato, mi era stato affidato l'incarico di provvedere le razioni all'intero manipolo... un bel mattino ne approfittai per dare un addio alla caserma e a San Petronio. Il soldato Angeli, che avevo indotto a seguirmi, mise a mia disposizione qualche spicciolo perché mi procurassi un travestimento; ma il conio veneziano, disgraziatamente, non aveva corso a Bologna ed un espediente estremo si impose: svestire la mia uniforme verde spigata di bianco, che avevo indossata a Ravenna, e barattarla con un abito di frustano. Profondo fu il senso d'amarezza che ne provai; ma finì per consolarmi l'idea che onorata è pure la divisa del lavoratore dei campi, l'onesto e sventurato paria del mio Paese. D'altronde, il cuore anelante la Libertà, il cuore che vince le battaglie me lo portavo sempre, immutato, nel petto.

    Al tocco, quella notte stessa, giungemmo, senza molestie, alle mura dell'antica Ferrara. Le porte chiuse; nella profonda oscurità, sull'altra riva del Po, in lontananza, brillavano i fuochi dell'accampamento austriaco. Ad eccezione della fortezza, la piazza era in potere dei nostri. Batto alla porta.

    — Chi va là?

    — Italiani disertati dall'accampamento austriaco. Desideriamo combattere per la causa dell'indipendenza.

    Siamo ammessi, accolti a braccia aperte, scortati al quartiere generale, tempestati di domande. Le nostre informazioni, racimolate su pei giornali, lungo la strada, furono trovate plausibili... d'altronde ci eravamo spacciati per soldati semplici e nessuno s'aspettava rivelazioni d'alta strategia. Si concluse coll'inoltrarci sino a Cento, dove il Maggiore Mambrini ci arruolava nella Legione Garibaldi.

    Splendeva il più bel sole d'Italia quando, dalle alture di Civita Castellana, vidi risaltare energicamente sull'azzurro la cupola di Michelangelo. La stanchezza svanì come per incanto e le nostre braccia si stesero verso la pianura, colà dove fremeva la metropoli che, della sua forza e della sua sapienza, aveva riempito il mondo. In quei giorni tra i vestigi della sua civiltà antica, tra le superbe creazioni dei tempi di mezzo, simbolo dell'idea nuova, fiammeggiava la camicia rossa.

    Eran gli ultimi giorni d'Aprile. Il 30, le truppe del francese invasore frangevano il proprio impeto contro il nostro petto e il cuore degli Italiani si riapriva alla speranza!

    Source pp. 20–24Scan to view on another deviceHaving found refuge and safety in Verona, the old Marshal offered the cadets entry, as non-commissioned officers, into the regular army, granting them the privilege of choosing their Regiment. No one accepted. We were interned as far as Gratz and incorporated into that Military Academy; until the Minister of War, acceding to our petition, permitted us to return to our homes.

    September came. The valiant Piedmontese army, abandoned to its fate by the defection of the Italian princes, its impetus blunted by the mental infirmities of its own King, vanquished more by the deficiency of its own organization than by Austrian valor, had been compelled to evacuate Venetia, the Duchies, and Lombardy; to fall back behind the line of the Ticino. Milan had fallen once more into the hands of the oppressors... The royal war being virtually ended, there continued, according to the expression then in vogue, that of the People.

    wholeness of spiritEdgren: interezza = integrityAmong the individuals unadorned by any official insignia who most distinguished themselves that year, for valor and for integrity of spirit, was a patriot and a very dear friend of my family: Pietro Fortunato Calvi...

    With a handful of mountaineers and with arms almost primitive, he had defended the Cadore from the first days of May to the first days of June, inscribing upon the annals of our epopee the names of Ampezzo, of Termine, of Rivalgo, of Chiusa di Venas, of the Passo della Morte, of Selva, of the Rindimera, and the ill-fated Mauria.

    In this astonishing campaign, which certain historians pass over with a rapidity even more astonishing, Pietro Fortunato Calvi repulsed the attacks — first separate, then combined — launched by the Austrians against the three natural gateways of the Cadore. When, at the third phase of the war, he laid down his arms, it was a necessity created by the complete exhaustion of provisions and of munitions. Nor in that age, so ready to attribute reverses to treachery, did a single voice arise to diminish the glory of the hero. He made his way, by unfrequented roads, to Venice and, wherever he appeared, was received not as one vanquished, but as a conqueror.

    In his footsteps, by the road through Portogruaro, the only one that remained open, I too entered Venice, together with my brother Achille. Calvi admitted us at once into that legion of the Cacciatori delle Alpi, whose name became synonymous with heroism and with glory.

    the shame of centuries of weakness, the cowardly resignation to servitudeRestores Gemini's 'wantonness'; lascivie = lasciviousness/wantonness, a moral indictment of decadent vice, not the flattened 'weakness'.During the close of 1848 and the first months of the following year, I thus had the opportunity to witness a marvelous spectacle. In my long existence I have known no other more potent in exalting the human soul... A people that, by sheer force of will, retempers its own fiber; that purges, in privation, in the grip of hunger, in the horrors of pestilence, in the baptism of its own blood, the shame of centuries of wantonness, the cowardly resignation to servitude.

    In destruction, the glimmers of life renewing itself, of greatness reaffirming itself.

    In those months, beyond my share of the common sufferings, I endured a grief intimate and intense. My brother Achille had his saintly resolves, his fervent hopes, inexorably cut short by pestilence. I came to kiss his icy brow and it seemed to me that, at the touch of my lips, within the inert cadaver there stirred as it were a last remnant of affection. I believe that only from that moment did the young patriot wholly cease to tremble with life.

    I myself took part in but a single engagement of importance: the sortie towards Mestre which furnished the city with forage and with meat. On that occasion I saw, for the first time, without speaking to him, a man who was destined to have a great influence upon my future: Felice Orsini.

    Two grave infractions of discipline removed me prematurely from that theater of sacrifice and of greatness. I had been assigned to the defense of the forts of Marghera, lunette number thirteen. A tedious duty; for Cavedalis, the Minister of War, under the pretext that munitions were lacking, had imposed upon us the order not to fire. We looked on with sullen idleness at the feverish labors of the Austrians who, concentrated at Mestre, were digging their parallels and preparing for the bombardment. Our inaction had emboldened them so greatly as to induce them, one day, to advance along the canal to within a rifle shot of the forts... and from that position they wounded in the arm the sentry of my lunette.

    The Minister’s orders and the Minister himself vanished from my mind, not to say from the face of the globe... I snatched the linstock from the gunner’s hands and the shot was fired. Instantly, as though my fury had spread with the swiftness of electricity, all the cannon of the forts thundered... the few of the enemy who remained standing took to their heels.

    Pietro Fortunato Calvi in person pleaded my cause before the Council of War. He adduced, in my defense, ignorance of the orders, my youth, love of Country, the irresistible impulse, and for the time being I was spared. But at the second infraction, political animosities rendered my position untenable.

    The fatal dualism between republicans and monarchists, which in 1848–49 accounted for so great a part of our reverses, had of necessity its repercussion at Venice too. Republican at first, then annexed to monarchical Piedmont, the city had once more assumed republican governance when the royal war appeared irretrievably lost. The upper bourgeoisie, which even then arrogated to itself the exclusive possession of practical good sense, did not readily reconcile itself to the popular verdict, and several Ministers lent it their support.

    Hence, hybrid measures that irritated the multitude and drove it frequently to assemble in the Piazza San Marco and to clamor.

    In March of 1849 — the precise day I do not recall — the exasperation, having reached its height, seemed on the point of degenerating into open revolt. To forestall it, Cavedalis issued the order, to Pietro Fortunato Calvi, to disperse the crowd at all costs, and Calvi, having detached several patrols from his legion, laid the thankless duty upon the non-commissioned officers. However, at the moment of our departure, he called us aside and, certain of being understood, pronounced these exact words: “Remember that in Venice it is no longer the Croats who give the orders.”

    I marched at the head of my patrol through a good stretch of the city without encountering any obstruction; but scarcely had I emerged into the Piazza San Marco than I found myself confronted by a tempestuous sea. Our green uniform braided in white and the drooping plume immediately attracted general attention, and a ringing voice cried out: “Look at them! They have come to shoot us down!”

    I placed my kepi upon the point of my dagger and, waving it by way of salute, cried at the top of my voice: “We are here to defend the People, not to tyrannize over them!”

    Farewell, repression! The crowd parted to give us free passage and, its sentiments transformed in an instant, roared from a thousand and a thousand throats: “Long live the Cacciatori delle Alpi!” Lost and as though engulfed by that stormy sea, we ended by becoming an integral part of it.

    Fortunately, where our arms proved unavailing, Daniele Manin supplied what was needed with authority and with eloquence. I have still before my eyes his face, resembling, as one drop of water resembles another, that of Cavour, though his spirit was so wholly alien to every form of diplomatic duplicity. The thoughts he expressed I am able to repeat today still, almost in his very own words. He exhorted them not to aggravate the miseries of the Fatherland, which in its straits called for the concord of all its sons. To divide it, to diminish its strength, was a crime.

    The urgent task, the supreme task, was that of vanquishing the foreigner, of breaking the chains being prepared for the Republic. There would be time afterwards to settle internal dissensions; when the glorious banner would once more wave over free lands and over free men. What might not then be achieved by a people conscious of its own rights, drunk with enthusiasm, strengthened by victory? What scheming, what blandishment would avail to wrest from it, even in part, the fruit of so many sacrifices? “Return to your homes, restore yourselves in the caresses of mother, of wife, of anxious children... poor great People, so harshly tried in the school of adversity! Bethink you that the cannon may, from one moment to the next, tear you from those caresses, summon your presence there where men fight, where men die. No Italian worthy of the name must refuse to present himself unsullied and without remorse to the supreme sacrifice!”

    He fell silent, and that strong People and that good People dispersed, little by little. How much greater a glory would accrue even today from the wise and humane word, to those Governors who so lightly set it aside in favor of fratricidal arms!

    The following day, Colonel Calvi sent for me. I found him more saddened than stern. “The Minister Cavedalis,” he said to me, “is beside himself with rage. Venice is full of informers, as in the days of the Signoria, and he has learned, chapter and verse, what... what has occurred. He demands the names of the non-commissioned officers who commanded the patrols; above all — you understand me? — the name of a beardless sergeant who uttered seditious phrases and whom he wishes to have punished. The escapade is provided for by military regulations...”

    I set about piecing together a defense; but he interrupted me with a gesture. The superior officer would not and could not enter into that discussion; but the patriot extended his right hand to the patriot and gave me a clasp that spoke as much as an entire volume.

    “It is the second of the kind,” he continued, “and there would be no remission. Sergeant di Rudio, you must leave Venice!.. You will find enemies to fight in every corner of Italy. Rome has need of defenders, and the Government of the Republic, at the solicitation of those Triumvirs, is sending thither the legionaries of the Unione, who came among us after the volte-face of Pope Pio. Venice has more need of bread than of soldiers, and Colonel Ferrara, who leads the legionaries, will set sail, precisely tomorrow, from Chioggia. Here is a letter for him... you will be well received. Go — I command it!”

    I rendered the military salute and departed. The unforeseen awaited me. The Minister Cavedalis had already sent to Chioggia the order to arrest, and return to Venice, all soldiers who did not belong to the Unione, or who could not produce a pass signed by the commandant of the Piazza. I did not walk for long through the streets of Chioggia.

    The barracks in which they confined me flanks a street that leads down to the waterfront. A single second sufficed to spring from the gateway and dart round the corner into that street; four squirrel-like bounds sufficed to traverse it and reach the shore. In the courtyard of the barracks there paced about, in mingled confusion, legionaries of every province and in every manner of uniform... at the wide-open gateway stood on guard a sentry, or rather a great lout who, to judge by appearances, seemed to be dreaming of the good soups of his village. Slowly, slowly, as though my sole concern were to amuse myself by watching the passers-by, I drew up alongside him. Then I flailed my arms and cried to him in a muffled voice: “Look! He’s escaping... escaping from that window!”

    He looks to the left, I leap to the right, turn the corner, and am out of range. Oh the blue waters of the Adriatic, oh the brown trabiccoli, rocking gently on the waves, light as shells!.. the trabiccoli brushed by the wings of the gulls, resounding with labors and with songs! I had some forty of them before me, already crowded with legionaries, already maneuvering for the imminent departure.

    I conceal myself in a small boat which, driven by two sturdy oarsmen, cleaves the waves swift as an arrow. But as I clamber up the side of a vessel, an officer on the shore caught sight of the green uniform braided in white. In that critical moment, the shifting maneuvers of the mobile flotilla proved providential to me. The officer had undoubtedly fixed his eye upon the trabaccolo in which I had sought refuge; but in the passage across he became confused and boarded, in his search, the vessel where I was not. He persisted in inspecting a second ship, then a third, and arrived at last upon mine... but by then I had contrived to put on a uniform of the Unione. I fell into rank with the other legionaries and he, passing within a hand’s breadth of my nose, scarcely deigned to glance at me.

    Nevertheless I was not to set foot in Rome without first describing a wide circuit. Many combatants had, like myself, quitted Venice by stealth, not from cowardice, but because drawn, by various considerations, towards the heart of Italy. But upon reaching Ravenna, Colonel Ferrara, citing I know not what peremptory orders, refused to enlist us and, having organized us into a detachment, had us escorted as far as Bologna under strict surveillance, as though we were Austrian prisoners. Our ultimate destination: Venice. But I was conscious of having taken up arms to break chains, not to have them imposed upon myself... I resolved to face the vicissitudes of a new escape.

    As the sole non-commissioned officer, I had been entrusted with the duty of drawing rations for the entire detachment... one fine morning I turned this to account by bidding farewell to the barracks and to San Petronio. The soldier Angeli, whom I had induced to follow me, put a few coppers at my disposal so that I might procure a disguise; but the Venetian coinage, unfortunately, was not current in Bologna, and an extreme expedient imposed itself: to strip off my green uniform braided in white, which I had put on again at Ravenna, and exchange it for a suit of fustian. Deep was the pang of bitterness this occasioned me; but in the end I consoled myself with the thought that the garb of the laborer of the fields — the honest and unfortunate paria of my Country — is also an honorable one. Besides, the heart that yearned for Liberty, the heart that wins battles, I carried always within my breast, unchanged.

    At one o’clock that very night, we arrived, without mishap, at the walls of ancient Ferrara. The gates were shut; in the deep darkness, on the far bank of the Po, in the distance, shone the watch-fires of the Austrian encampment. With the exception of the fortress, the piazza was in our hands. I knock at the gate.

    — Who goes there?

    — Italians deserted from the Austrian encampment. We wish to fight for the cause of independence.

    We are admitted, welcomed with open arms, escorted to general headquarters, plied with questions. Our intelligence, gleaned from the newspapers along the road, was found plausible... besides, we had passed ourselves off as common soldiers, and no one expected revelations of high strategy. The matter concluded with our being conducted as far as Cento, where Major Mambrini enrolled us in the Legione Garibaldi.

    The fairest sun of Italy was shining when, from the heights of Civita Castellana, I saw Michelangelo’s dome stand forth in sharp relief against the blue. The weariness vanished as though by enchantment, and our arms stretched out towards the plain, towards that place where there throbbed the metropolis that had filled the world with its power and its wisdom. In those days, amid the vestiges of its ancient civilization, amid the superb creations of the Middle Ages, the red shirt blazed as the symbol of the new idea.

    It was the last days of April. On the 30th, the troops of the French invader broke their impetus against our breasts, and the hearts of Italians opened once more to hope!

    Parte Prima

    Capitolo Quinto

    Part One

    Chapter Five

    L'ASSEDIO di Roma va indissolubilmente collegato al nome di un uomo nefasto alla Libertà: quello di Luigi Napoleone. I Carbonari, che l'avevano accolto nel loro grembo, lo guardavano con sospetto già nel 1831; poiché, a quell'epoca, si era sparsa la voce che egli avesse tolto di mezzo il fratello (Napoleone Luigi); il quale, per essere nato quattro anni prima, gli sbarrava la via a salire sublime.

    I nostri moti rivoluzionari aprivano allora, o sembrava, nuovi orizzonti all'Italia e i due figli di Ortensia, partecipando a quei moti, cercavano nella Penisola un campo propizio alla loro ambizione; un compenso, fors'anche, alla perdita del trono francese, riscaldato pel momento, da Luigi Filippo. Questo ordine d'idee avvalorava la fosca diceria che, del resto, non potè mai essere approfondita.

    La maggioranza degli storici che trattano dell'epoca, afferma semplicemente che Napoleone Luigi morì di rosolia. Altri si spinge sino ad ammettere che la cosa non è ben certa; ma lascia vagamente supporre che, se delitto venne perpetrato, esso va attribuito ai fanatici di qualche "Vendita".

    Comunque sia, la diffidenza venne attenuata dal tempo e dalle pose umanitarie del principe. D'altra parte, i sovversivi ben comprendevano l'immenso partito che potevano ritrarre da un uomo il cui nome era tutto un programma di guerra all'anticaglia e che non avrebbe potuto uscire dalla sua semioscurità, se non a patto di assumersi la responsabilità intera di quel programma.

    Svanite le speranze di far fortuna in Italia, Luigi Napoleone rivolgeva i suoi occhi alla Francia. Tentò dapprima il colpo di mano di Strasburgo, che fallì principalmente per la sua mancanza di risolutezza; poi quello di Boulogne, che lo condusse dritto dritto alle carceri del forte di Ham.

    I sovversivi di Francia e d'Italia brigarono allora per opporsi alla violenta soppressione di quell'entità politica. La principessa Cristina Belgioioso Triulzio, addentro nelle cose della Giovane Italia, fu indotta a valersi della propria influenza alla Corte di Luigi Filippo, per ottenere un abboccamento col prigioniero.

    Si sa che la parte sostanziale di quell'abboccamento non richiese un lungo preambolo. La gentildonna offerse l'aiuto dei liberali di Francia e d'Italia per effettuare un'evasione e sospingere l'uomo a galleggiare sul burrascoso mare politico. Giunto al potere avrebbe pagato il debito della riconoscenza adoperandosi per la promulgazione di Leggi democratiche e propugnando la nostra indipendenza.

    La titubanza di Luigi Napoleone indusse l'illustre italiana ad accordargli due mesi per riflettere. Nel frattempo, per non trovarsi impreparati agli eventi, gli amici avrebbero pensato ad organizzare il piano d'una fuga.

    I due mesi trascorsero ed un secondo abboccamento ebbe luogo. Il prigioniero lasciò dire; poi espresse la sua profonda simpatia per la nostra Nazione; ma concluse dichiarando che, pel momento, non si sentiva disposto ad agire... sia che diffidasse dell'avvenente e coltissima gentildonna; sia che sperasse aiuto da partiti d'idee più analoghe ai propri istinti ed alle proprie mire segrete. Il piano sembrava abbandonato quando, come folgore, si sparse nei nostri circoli la nuova che Napoleone, cambiando subitamente pensiero, vi aveva dato il proprio consenso e, poco dopo, altrettanto fulminea, giungeva quella del suo travestimento e della sua fuga, dalla Francia al Belgio, dal Belgio all'Inghilterra.

    A Londra, e precisamente al Brunswick Hotel, egli rivede la Belgioioso e suggella, per così dire, il patto: "Avrebbe aiutato l'Italia, non appena assestate le cose di Francia."

    Gli avvenimenti precipitarono... Luigi Filippo venne cacciato dal trono e Napoleone domanda il permesso di rimpatriare. A smuovere l'opposizione parlamentare, sorge Luigi Blanc che, con un discorso solo, cambia indirizzo alla storia di Francia quanto avrebbero potuto farlo dieci battaglie. Napoleone diventa candidato all'Assemblea Nazionale, pel Dipartimento della Senna e mette trionfalmente il piede sul suolo francese.

    Epoca tristemente famosa!.. I partiti che si contendono il predominio del Paese, si avventano l'un l'altro alla gola e scatenano su Parigi l'uragano di sangue che preludia ogni grande mutamento politico. Cavaignac emerge trucemente dalla carneficina e aspira alla Presidenza della Repubblica, contesagli dai vinti che gli oppongono, disperatamente, la candidatura di Ledru Rollin. La risultante tra quelle due correnti, intravista e facilitata dalle nostre associazioni segrete, portava alla vittoria di una terza candidatura; quella di Luigi Napoleone. Il 20 Dicembre del 1848 egli diventa il Magistrato Supremo della gloriosa Nazione.

    Quale il suo primo atto di rilievo?

    Quello di ricompensare i suoi amici adoperandosi, coll'armi e col tradimento, a ristabilire nel cuore della nostra Penisola, il più imbelle, il più nefasto, il più odiato dei Governi... lo sgoverno del Papa!

    L'intento diabolico venne tanto bene dissimulato, da indurre gli Italiani a facilitare lo sbarco delle truppe del Generale Oudinot, apparse improvvisamente nelle acque di Civitavecchia. Essi le accolsero con entusiastiche, fraterne dimostrazioni.

    In Francia, le diffidenze sorsero più prontamente. Le fiere proteste pel sospettato annientamento di una Repubblica sorella, a vantaggio dell'elemento nemico d'ogni libertà, si diffusero dalle aule del Parlamento, giù, tra le moltitudini, assumendo, qua e là, le proporzioni di sommosse vere e proprie. E forse l'energia popolare avrebbe finito per imporre onesti intendimenti, quando si sparse la nuova del rovescio toccato, il 30 aprile, dalle armi dell'invasore.

    Un fremito di torvo patriottismo corse da un capo all'altro della Francia. "Bisognava ripristinare il prestigio della bandiera; rivendicare, ad ogni costo, l'onore nazionale." Come se l'onta di un Popolo derivasse non dal delitto, ma dalla sua mancata perpetrazione! Luigi Napoleone ebbe così, inaspettatamente, a complice volontaria delle sue fosche mire — strano a dirsi! — la più generosa, la più leale tra le entità della vecchia Europa!

    I Francesi, le soldatesche del Re Bomba, gli Spagnuoli, gli Austriaci e l'arti subdole dei preti!.. come avrebbe potuto Roma sottrarsi all'estrema rovina?

    Un uomo solo, Giuseppe Mazzini, previde, in quel tragico momento che, dalla morte stessa poteva, per virtù di eroismo e di martirio, rinnovellarsi il diritto, scaturire la vita. Egli si provò a trasfondere la sua fede in quanti lo attorniavano e gli intellettuali e gli umili ne rimasero come trasfigurati. I "briganti" che difesero Roma scrissero, a note di sangue, la più pura, la più feconda tra le pagine della nostra storia moderna. La ferrea determinatezza che ridiede la sua gloriosa capitale alla Patria nostra si deve, in gran parte, alle loro sconfitte!
