    Gemini Proa sound body of customs officers and soldiers'Guardie di Finanza' is a specific Italian institution; 'Finance guards' preserves the institutional name rather than silently modernising to 'customs officers'.Edgren 1901molestia — molestation (annoyance, vexation)Edgren confirms that 'molestation' in period English meant annoyance/vexation, not the narrower modern sexual sense. This supported retaining Claude's 'suffered no molestation' over Gemini's 'without any trouble'.Edgren 1901intuire — know intuitively (know at a glance)Edgren's gloss 'know at a glance' confirmed that 'divined' (used by both drafts) is apt — capturing the instantaneous, intuitive quality of Trolli's understanding rather than a more deliberate 'understood' or 'guessed'.We parted, with the understanding that we should not lose sight of one another, and that we would quit the city by the Barrière du Trône. But to pass it, guarded as it was by a sound body of Finance guards and soldiers, was rather a ticklish undertaking. Before reaching it, I bought a large loaf and tucked it under my arm. Trolli divined my purpose and did likewise. As workmen returning from their labors we were looked up and down from head to foot, but suffered no molestation.

    Edgren 1901campagna — open country or field, countryEdgren's primary sense 'open country or field' supported using 'open country' rather than the more modern default 'countryside', which would lose the spatial quality of emerging from the city into exposed terrain.And when we reached the open country, the bare and wintry fields, we came together again. Trolli asked me where we should go to lay our heads.

    "Where shall we go? To the devil, but free! Where shall we lay our heads? And what does it matter to you where, so long as it be not within the lunette of the guillotine? Switzerland is far away. But we have known how to cross Spain — we shall cross France as well!"

    We had covered half a dozen miles when we were overtaken by a traveler in a little carriage. Upon seeing us, he drew in the reins and invited us to climb up. I cast a searching glance at him. He was decently dressed and had the look of a good-natured, timid countryman. His nose and his eyes were exceedingly red, and not from the cold alone... he could not be a spy. And besides... he was alone, while we were two, and possessed of resolution. We accepted.

    As soon as the horse had resumed his trot, the good man asked us where we were bound.

    — To Troy.

    — A long walk! I am not going so far; but I will shorten the road for you.

    And he began to give an account of himself and his affairs. He had gone to Paris to sell vegetables, the produce of his own little kitchen-garden and his own labors. And he showed us his calloused hands. The years had been rather lean; but with a little thrift and keeping his eyes open he had never gone without his rabbit and his small wine. And he launched forth, spreading out the long litany of his amours with the gossips of the neighborhood. Of Paris, of the coup d'état, of the horrors of that day — not a phrase, not a word; as though he had left behind him a city perfectly at peace and still. The wine he had swallowed made him talkative, but was not enough to draw him out of a prudent reserve.

    At a certain point there escaped him some remark about certain speculations of his, and he concluded that if they prospered according to his hopes he would have enough to live comfortably upon until the "Lady without a nose" came to cut him down with her scythe. And with that allusion to Death he fell silent, as if absorbed in profound meditation. Ten minutes later he was snoring.

    I thereupon took possession of the reins, and laid on the whip — trot, horse, trot. We passed broad stretches of land, we passed through coppices, we passed through villages... twilight came, night fell... the peasant was snoring still...

    All of a sudden, in the still air, there rang out slowly the brazen toll of a bell. It was eleven o'clock. A little later the wheels of the carriage were grating over cobblestones. And then the horse planted his feet and, for all my whipping, would not budge a step forward or a step backward.

    The strange thing was that this abrupt halt had the power of breaking the peasant's heavy slumber. "Sacré cœur!" he exclaimed, after peering into the darkness. "Where have you brought me? Twelve miles from home!"

    He falls to blaming — mark the justice of the world! — the horse, who might well have remembered where his manger was. But he ends by consoling himself with the thought that we were not in a desert. In the village he had good friends. "Do you see that strip of light? The inn is still open and the host is an honest man. I shall stand you a drink!"

    Half an hour later he was lying down in order to be off betimes. Trolli and I went out into the open air. Warmed within and without, we once more confronted the darkness of the countryside and those yet denser darknesses of the unknown.

    Our subversive Society spread its net over the whole of France, and the threads of that net were knotted again at Troy, so that it was not difficult for us to make our contacts there and to obtain a place on the stage-coach as far as Langres, the locality where the meshes, drawing together, formed the next knot.

    The horses were still stamping under the archway of the inn at Langres when I was already making my way in haste to the house of the Postal Officer. A letter of introduction, a few agreed signs, and he would procure us the means to continue our journey. I pulled the bell and there appeared at once upon the threshold a lady of uncertain years, but not without a certain charm.

    — My husband is not at home, nor do I know when he will return.

    — I am the bearer of a letter which I should like to deliver to him in person...

    The lady's mouth contracted, as though she were holding a slice of lemon between tongue and palate. She became inquisitive: A letter? From whom? Give it here, give it! I will undertake to deliver it... into his own hands.

    — I beg your pardon! I have my orders and...

    Her face had grown pale. She insisted and stretched forth her right hand... her fingers trembled. So much the more did I consider prudence a duty. She went back indoors, slamming the door behind her.

    Gemini Proto interrupt the pleasant process of digestionThe Italian 'la dolce preparazione del chilo' uses the specific physiological term 'chilo' (chyle); Gemini's rendering preserves both the period-scientific vocabulary and the wry humour of the original, whereas Claude's paraphrase flattens it.That evening I was seated in the common-room of the inn, before the fireplace, conversing cheerfully with other guests, when, to delay the sweet preparation of the chyle, there appeared unannounced the shrewish creature with her countenance at once harsh and melancholy. She had two gendarmes at her back.

    The purpose of the visit was made plain at once. The lady demanded the surrender of the mysterious letter. The gendarmes had come to impound it.

    I rose, drew out my pocket-book calmly, and, removing the letter of introduction, called the attention of those present to the fact that it lay in an open envelope. Then, with a bound towards the fireplace, I cast it into the flames.

    Gemini Probursting into unrestrained laughterItalian 'ridere sgangheratamente' conveys loud, unhinged laughter; 'uproarious' (adapted from Gemini's 'laughing uproariously') is more vivid and closer in register than 'unrestrained'.There followed some little confusion. The gendarmes, who considered "the dignity of the uniform" to have been affronted, wished to proceed forthwith; but the order they had received was to seize the document and not to arrest its bearer. "We shall go for more precise instructions." They left, without arresting me, the more readily since the bystanders had been unable to refrain from bursting into uproarious laughter. I was compelled to protest with all my energy. The very Laws of the Republic specified that only sealed letters were liable to seizure; I had therefore, and with perfect right, resisted an abuse of authority.

    And indeed the two gentlemen had placed themselves on false ground. The Law was against those who were supposed to uphold it; and, followed by the lady, discomfited and well-nigh in tears, they departed, and I saw them no more.

    Late that night, however, the Postal Officer appeared, and I had the key to the riddle. His wife, an honest woman and of great merit, had the weakness of being jealous to the point of madness. This passion of hers had of late been inflamed by the mysterious coming and going inherent in our political Society, of whose existence she was unaware. Having fixed in her head the notion that I was bringing her husband the secret missive of some rival, she had made this ill-judged attempt to intercept evidence of guilt which existed only in her imagination.

    Gemini Proall our clandestine network failed usItalian 'ogni influenza segreta ci venne a mancare' says 'every secret influence'; Gemini's rendering is a closer literal match than Claude's interpretive 'clandestine network'.Once the Swiss frontier was crossed, all secret influence failed us, and from that point there began the truly arduous portion of our pilgrimage. Trolli had written to his relatives to send him money to Berne. We had to trudge all the way there: our purses desperately empty, in the heart of winter, with snow lying deep, barely clothed.

    We arrived. But the frost of free Helvetia had nearly crystallized itself, like fringe, about our hearts. As at Barcelona, so now at Berne — there was no money!

    Night fell inexorable. We lay down in the market-place, beneath the stall of a greengrocer. Despite the fast and the bitter cold, we fell into a profound sleep. Blessed is youth!

    But at some point in the night I was startled awake by a peremptory voice cursing in German. A very simple thing had occurred. Trolli was endowed with legs that called to mind a trebuchet; as he slept, he had stretched them out as far as the pavement and was, quite involuntarily, tripping a constable who was passing by. He was dragged out of the hiding-place like a sack of rags and pelted with questions. But he did not understand a word of German and, by way of reply, merely stretched and yawned. The policeman ended by losing patience, and seizing him by the collar shoved him forward with curses and buffets. I heard the heavy double-step fade into the distance; then I drew my jacket closer about my chest and went back to sleep. What good would it have done to intervene?

    Gemini Prowhistling a little tune well known to TrolliItalian 'cabaletta' is a specific operatic term (a fast concluding section of an aria); preserving it maintains the cultural colour and Crespi's milieu. The Claude eval flagged the loss of this term.At daybreak I strolled about the solid prison building, whistling a cabaletta well known to Trolli. I was answered by a brief whistle of recognition, and immediately, framed between the bars of a small window, appeared the face of my Cardinal.

    Gemini Prothe stale air of a schoolroomItalian 'aria mefitica' means noxious/foul air, a specific period term; 'mephitic' preserves the literary register and precise meaning where 'stale' is too mild.Farewell discretion! I waved my cap furiously and called out my greeting with the liveliness of a boy escaping from the mephitic air of a schoolroom. Nor did Trolli respond with less enthusiasm. And there and then he let me know that, in deference to the Law against vagabondage, the Police would have him escorted to the Italian frontier. "Do you promise to follow after me?"

    I had barely time to extend my hand in solemn pledge.

    A warder, hearing the commotion, had seized Trolli by his long dangling legs, and the cardinal face vanished.

    That day I wandered long about the market, and, snatching up a turnip here and there, found a way of quelling the cramps in my stomach. When night fell again I took shelter, like Diogenes, in a barrel. My sufferings were such that, by dawn, I had almost made up my mind to give myself up to the Police.

    But from that very extremity chance offered me, in a most singular fashion, the thread that gave me the means of revival. I had sunk upon a bench in the Public Gardens and was gnawing at the stem of my pipe, to deceive myself... the supply of tobacco had long since been exhausted. The paths were deserted; snow covered the green expanses, and upon it a flock of sparrows was chirping and hopping. Despite the rigors of winter, they had breakfasted.

    Vexed, I seized a small stone, and among those happy creatures there passed, like a thunderbolt, the characteristic protest of empty stomachs. One sparrow gave a cry, turned its little legs in the air, and lay stiff.

    This was no moment for hesitation. In like circumstances, man eats man... I could well bring myself to devour a raw sparrow. I was plucking it when I felt a hand laid upon my shoulder.

    — What are you doing, young man?

    — Taking a snack.

    — You must have the hunger of a hawk. In any case, praise Divine Providence, which has known how to provide for your fast.

    Beside me sat a neat, respectable-looking elderly man; a Protestant minister, in all probability. I pointed out to him that the Lord God had provided for my needs through the slaughter of the innocent, and in a manner too derisory for me to feel my heart overflowing with gratitude.

    He jingled some silver coins in his pocket. After a brief silence: "Will you sell me your pipe for six francs?"

    I pocketed the money; the stranger pocketed the pipe. Then he began: "You see that our Heavenly Father..."

    Gemini ProI sprang up — four long strides —Italian 'Diedi un balzo, quattro balzi' says literally 'I gave one leap, four leaps'; Gemini's count and the word 'bounds' are faithful to the original's rhythmic enumeration, which Claude's 'four long strides' misses.Imagine if I meant to waste my time in philosophizing! I sprang up — one bound, four bounds — and gave myself no rest until I found myself seated where the sweet fragrance of roasting meat was drifting through the air.

    Gemini Prothe double problem of feeding myself and pressing onItalian 'duplice problema' is better served by 'twofold' which has a more formal, period-appropriate register than 'double'.With what remained, I was able to make my way to Lucerne, where the twofold problem of feeding myself and pressing on was to be solved for me by a man against whom I had fought — Pope Mastai Ferretti.

    Switzerland is a marvelous country. United by its beauties, upon those slopes, among those valleys, peoples of diverse origin, tongue and character live together in peace... a miracle of Liberty, a glorious anticipation of a holy Utopia.

    And yet those peoples have failed to show the elevation of spirit needed to assume the full weight of their historic mission. In centuries gone by the heart of Europe beat mightily among the Alps; but in vain! As from the pale mountain ridges the waters rush down to furrow the fertile plains and water and fecundate so great a part of the Continent, so from crags inaccessible to the conqueror should those fair and terrible phalanxes have descended, to accomplish a work of fraternity, to revive the hopes of the oppressed, to extend the dominion of Liberty.

    They did descend; but as a devastating scourge... blind instruments of the wicked, flesh and souls put up for sale!

    Selfishness is fatal to nations because, among other things, the tainted atmosphere of your neighbor, sooner or later, corrupts your own as well, imperils and diminishes your glory. Despotism, defended by Swiss mercenaries, confined within the narrow circle of the mountains the very men who had nurtured it; the Republic remained a puny thing and, in the universal estimation, became a synonym for venality. At the time when I was passing through it, the evil habit was not greatly in force, and already the Federal Government was branding as a criminal both the citizen who was not ashamed to prostitute himself to a foreign Government and the agent who hired him out and made himself his accomplice. Nevertheless Pius IX, who distrusted his own Catholics, maintained secret agents in the Lutheran Cantons, to scrape together, for a goodly sum of baiocchi, the handful of insects he intended, in his heart of hearts, to use to alter the course of the stars, to annihilate the hopes of the new Italy.

    One such agent espied me on the shore of the lake, absorbed in my twofold problem. Attracted perhaps by my soldierly bearing, he opened conversation in the holy name of God and concluded it by formally proposing that I should shoulder a musket in defense of the pontifical throne and the altar.

    My first impulse was to hurl him headfirst into the lake. But a sudden thought restrained me. The Garibaldian tactic!... when you have no ammunition, take it from the enemy. The funds of the Holy See employed to repatriate an adversary of the old order!... my twofold problem more than resolved! And, incidentally, I might do good service to my country's cause as well, by unmasking the intrigue and clipping the nails of the unctuous agent!

    I smiled and, passing myself off as a Swiss from the Canton of Ticino, accepted.

    Gemini Proa room of equivocal characterItalian 'carattere anfibio' literally means 'amphibious character' (belonging to two worlds); 'equivocal' is an interpretation, whereas 'amphibious' preserves Crespi's specific and witty metaphor.I was conducted through winding lanes to a house of mean appearance; I fixed its number well in my memory, mounted a dirty little staircase, entered a room of amphibious character — something between a study and a bedchamber. The contract was duly drawn up and signed, and a few hours later, from the stern of the steamboat as it put rapidly out from shore, I was bidding farewell with broad gestures to my recruiter.

    Gemini ProThe bird was in its cageItalian 'Il merlo' is specifically a blackbird; Gemini's translation preserves this, which also carries Italian slang connotations (a 'merlo' is a gullible person), adding a layer of irony Claude's generic 'bird' loses.Arrived at Flüelen, I presented myself at the Commissioner's office and narrated, from beginning to end, what had befallen me. I was brought back to Lucerne for the identification of the individual. I retraced the winding lanes, found the house of the less than modest appearance, remounted the dirty staircase. The blackbird was in his cage — indeed in bed; the evidence palpable, the Law precise. He was arrested.

    While he was dressing, the Federal Delegate drew out a purse and attempted to count out for me some sum of money or other. A flush of color rose to my face and I burst out: "Italians are ever ready to confront with a tranquil mind any sacrifice, even that of assuming the appearance of a villain. For when the good of the Fatherland is at stake and the lion's leap is not possible, then let the cunning of the fox be welcomed and blessed. But learn this, and let it be your guide: if ever you find one who, having made the sacrifice, stretches out his hand and demands payment, say freely that he was not born beyond your Alps... or, if he was born there, hold it for certain that he is of bastard race, unworthy of the name he flaunts!" I was escorted back to Flüelen, and then, alone, set out for Goeschenen, whence, facing the cold and the storm, I reached the summit of the Saint Gotthard. From up there, something passed across my face like a foretaste of southern warmth; an air saturate with trembling and with sobbing, with hatred and with love.

    And I pressed forward almost at a run, with a heart full to bursting — whether with joy or with pity I could not myself have said. At Varese I was received by Trolli as a dear departed soul miraculously returned to life. And in the hospitality of that family I found ample recompense for my hardships. But in Lombardy the Austrian reaction was raging; and the sight of that hateful uniform rekindled the fever in me... and at that age, with my temperament, it is too arduous a task to put a curb upon one's impetuosity. Friends, privy to what was afoot, opportunely breathed a word of counsel. I must not compromise my hosts, and so I had myself escorted to the frontier.

    Gemini Proseemed to enter into me and make my own soul greaterItalian 'mi compenetrasse' means to pervade or saturate thoroughly; 'permeate' from Gemini captures this sense of deep interpenetration better than the simpler 'enter into'.Having taken leave of my escort and remaining alone, I turned to contemplate the beautiful and unhappy Land which, for the second time, I was compelled to leave. In the majesty of the place and the silence, I passed back in memory over all that had been... the desperate charge of the few, armed with nothing but their right, against the serried masses hideous with bayonets; the gaze of the innocent turned heavenward in vain; the silent tears, the streams of blood. And in the exaltation of that moment my thoughts flew from the foot of the Alps, on and on, to the farthest extremity of Sicily. Everywhere hearts that throbbed, hands that clasped one another in the dark, that in the dark sharpened their blades. Then the great soul of that gentle, vigorous, and unhappy race seemed to permeate me and make my own soul greater. I stretched wide my arms as though I expected to draw into them, lovingly, every one — every one of my companions in bondage. I renewed the inspired oath of Giuseppe Mazzini, and with tears upon my face turned once more upon the road of exile.

    Parte Prima

    Capitolo Undicesimo

    Part One

    Chapter Eleven

    NELL'APRILE del 1852 ero nuovamente a Genova e lavoravo presso certo Ferrari negoziante sarto, quando venni informato che don Bastiano Barozzi, parroco di San Pietro in Campo, viveva rifugiato a Torino. Il Barozzi, durante l'assedio di Venezia, fu cappellano di una Legione composta quasi esclusivamente di studenti di Padova. Era uno di quei preti a cui la sottana non soffocava l'amor di Patria e che, all'ubbidienza passiva, preferivano il seguire gli impulsi della propria coscienza. L'Italia ne vanta buon numero e la Chiesa di Roma avrebbe potuto trarne grande prestigio se, per piacere ai prepotenti, non avesse preferito confessarli e prestare anche mano al loro supplizio.

    Versatissimo nella storia antica, don Bastiano giudicava gli uomini attraverso il prisma del mondo romano buon al livello di Attilio Regolo, degli Scipioni, dei Gracchi avrebbe valuto inalzare l'intera nostra razza. Durante le mie vacanze scolastiche egli, passeggiando lungo l'Ardo e senza darsene l'aria, mi sopannava il cervello di episodi eroici. Penso che gli devo, in non minima parte, il mio culto per la Libertà.

    I preti d'acqua torbida, che non osavano muovergli guerra palese per i suoi principi patriottici, tirarono in ballo i Decretali e se la presero coi suoi mustacchi e col suo pizzo che sapevano di modernista. Egli si ostinò a non farseli radere e ne perdette la Messa. Nelle sue strettezze, fu sovvenuto da mio padre e tanto era bastato perchè don Bastiano gli serbasse una riconoscenza, che rasentava i limiti della venerazione.

    A tale uomo decisi di rivolgermi, nella speranza di rientrare, col suo aiuto, nel vortice dell'azione e pensando che due parole a viva voce valgono più di quattro lunghe lettere, partii alla volta di Torino... a piedi, tanto per non perderne l'abitudine.

    Mia prima cura, quando vi giunsi, fu quella di avviarmi al caffè Nazionale, ritrovo favorito di rifugiati politici. Senonchè, prima d'arrivarvi, scorsi, piantato dinanzi al cartellone di un teatro, un signore colla faccia così vicina allo stampato, che pareva lo leggesse colla punta del naso. L'estrema miopia di don Bastiano Barozzi mi era nota e quell'indizio bastò a rivelarmelo.

    Grande fu la sua meraviglia nel ritrovarmi, smisurata la sua gioia. Egli non seppe contenerla e, lì per lì, senza soggezione alcuna dei viandanti, mi impresse sulle guancie due baci sonori: "To'... questo per conto mio; questo per conto di tuo padre!" Mi domandò poi qual buon vento mi avesse portato a Torino. Ed io:

    "Don Bastiano, Ella sa che i di Rudio non sono poltroni. Che cosa si medita, che cosa si prepara? Sono venuto per non arrugginire e faccio assegnamento su Lei."

    Abbassò gli occhi e non rispose. Ma, dopo breve silenzio, quasi concludendo i suoi pensieri: "Fortunato Calvi è qui. In quattro salti, siamo in casa sua. Vieni!"

    Fortunato Calvi, il glorioso organizzatore dei Cacciatori delle Alpi, il mio Colonnello, l'amico intimo della mia famiglia, mi accolse colle braccia conserte, senza muover passo, con espressione quasi sdegnosa. La piena degli affetti, che stava per prorompere, rimase dolorosamente compressa nel mio petto. Non osai neppure provocare una spiegazione. Ma la spiegazione venne spontanea.

    Il Calvi era stato informato da persona "degna di fede" che io lasciata, per ordine suo, Venezia, invece di recarmi a Chioggia, ero passato, con armi e bagagli, all'Austriaco. Citò persino il nome di tale che aveva giurato d'avermi visto, dietro le trincee, far fuoco contro i miei connazionali.

    La calunnia!.. quale uomo non ne fu, or tanto o quanto, vittima? A poco a poco, il petto si indurisce ai suoi colpi così che, a lungo andare, più non riescono a suscitarmi tempesta alcuna. Conosco audaci che, col loro contegno, quasi la provocano di proposito, per l'acre piacere di mostrarsi superiori. Ma quella era la prima volta che me la trovavo di fronte ed il suo morso mi giunse estremamente crudele.

    Tuttavia mi riuscì facile il dimostrarne l'assurdità.

    Fortunato Calvi, che non aveva minor desiderio di trovarmi innocente, di quello che io avessi di provarglielo, mi stese sorridendo la destra che io strinsi con trasporto febbrile. Mentre don Bastiano, muto testimonio di quella scena e uomo eccezionalmente sensitivo, girava sui tacchi per nascondere una lagrima.

    Le conseguenze di quel colloquio furono, per quanto mi riguarda, di non lieve momento. Giuseppe Mazzini, Luigi Kossuth e Ledru Rollin avevano fondato apertamente, a Londra, un Comitato Rivoluzionario Europeo, al quale si annodarono presto innumerevoli filiali. Organizzava le proprie ogni Popolo che, vinta la nefasta apatia, crittogama degli animi, comprendeva come la rigenerazione scaturisca dall'agitarsi e dal sacrificio. L'Italia ne era un alveare e, per forza di circostanze, il Comitato di Torino aveva, su tutti gli altri nostri, il predominio. Ad esso specialmente spettava l'incitare lo spirito di ribellione serpeggiante nella Penisola e il coordinarne i moti. Suo capo era, nel 1852, il conte Grillanzoni; ma il comando delle eventuali operazioni militari incombeva a Fortunato Calvi.

    Io ebbi dal Calvi e dal Conte Grillanzoni l'incarico di percorrere, come inviato speciale, le principali città del Lombardo-Veneto; dapprima per distribuire manifesti sovversivi e cedole del prestito nazionale mazziniano; poi, in un successivo viaggio, per ispezionare le varie guarnigioni e riferire dati precisi sulla loro forza, sulla loro nazionalità, sul loro umore.

    Semplice il sistema adottato. Si pensò a procurarmi un passaporto a nome di Carlo Moretti, negoziante in sete. Bastava scrivessi: Nella tale o tale altra piazza ho venduto due, tre, quattro balle di seta bianca, o verde, o gialla. Il numero delle balle corrispondeva a quello dei Reggimenti; il colore ne indicava l'uniforme e l'arma. Una studiata allusione alla qualità speciale della merce, o all'indole del cliente, bastava a far comprendere quante altre circostanze riputassi necessario il far rilevare.

    Rammento l'ammonizione fattami dal Calvi, il giorno stesso della mia partenza. Io nascondevo nella cannuccia della pipa, l'elenco dei vari Sotto Comitati e sorridevo. Il Calvi, sospettò che quel sorriso provenisse da leggerezza e disse severamente: "Non illuderti giovanotto! Una lieve imprudenza, un semplice incidente fortuito e saresti impiccato!" Nella forca non incappai, ma le passai, più di una volta, rasente.

    La mia prima tappa fu a Stradella, ove strinsi la mano a Nino Bixio (sopravvissuto alla ferita riportata a villa Corsini) e a quel Winkler già capo, a Venezia, di una legione ungherese, che doveva, pochi anni dopo, chiudere l'eroica esistenza, combattendo sulle rive del Volturno. Il contatto di uomini di simile tempra, mi fu causa di piacevole eccitamento. Ma passato, coll'aiuto di un contrabbandiere, l'azzurro Ticino e inoltratomi appena nei domini dell'Austria, parve che una mano di ferro mi serrasse alla gola. L'oppressore aveva, dopo il 1849, acuito il torpido ingegno per trasformare quelle terre in una bolgia d'inferno.

    Regola generale, l'Austria considerava le Provincie fiaccate dalle sue armi, e l'altre riconquistate dall'armi alleate della Russia, come un covo di briganti. Sfogare su di esse un odio barbarico, gratificare delle più atroci vendette pareva, nelle sfere auliche, alta sapienza politica. Persuaso di compiere una missione affidatagli da Dio, Francesco Giuseppe non si riteneva neppure vincolato dalla propria parola e dalle stipulate capitolazioni. Il generale Klapka, l'eroico difensore di Komorn, aveva ottenuto per la guarnigione, eccellenti condizioni di resa. Vennero tutte violate. Un codice d'onore esiste persino tra i ladri; ma col pretesto della ragion di Stato, non ne esisteva alcuno per Francesco Giuseppe.

    Dei trentaquattro generali ungheresi, intelligenze elette, fibre eroiche, che avevano costretto i soldati imperiali a mostrare ripetutamente le spalle, tre furono condannati al carcere perpetuo, dodici cristianamente fucilati, o impiccati. E lo scempio sarebbe stato maggiore, se gli altri non si fossero salvati colla fuga e coll'esilio. Per parecchi mesi uno stormo di carnefici vestiti, quasi a maggior scherno, col colore della speranza, si affaccendarono in Ungheria ad ammucchiare cadaveri di patrioti. Tutto questo par cancellato, ormai, dalla memoria degli uomini che urlano per le strade gli evviva al vecchio vituperevole. Eppure non si cessa mai dall'imprecare contro i generosi che, accolti nel proprio animo gli spasimi delle vittime, arrischiano la vita per somministrare ai despoti un pizzico della loro stessa medicina.

    In Italia, a dir vero, Francesco Giuseppe ebbe gli artigli smussati da Vittorio Emanuele II che, salito al trono tra la glaciale diffidenza dei suoi sudditi, seppe piegarsi alle terribili necessità incombenti, senza compromettere l'avvenire. Egli non volle firmare il trattato di pace coll'Austria, quando essa non si assumesse l'obbligo di accordare piena amnistia ai centomila Italiani che, dal Lombardo-Veneto, si erano rifugiati in Piemonte, sia per muoverle guerra, sia per sottrarsi alle sue sevizie.

    Vittorio Emanuele II disponeva, benché vinto, di una forza non trascurabile e l'Imperatore mantenne il patto. Senonchè il suo "perdono" era tacitamente condizionato alla completa sottomissione al giogo austriaco. Mancavano appigli? Sequestri, imposizioni arbitrarie, catene e forca non venivano centellinati dalla paterna sollecitudine di Francesco Giuseppe ed il bastone era applicato sul dorso anche di coloro, uomini o donne, che, senza ribellarsi, manifestavano sensi virili.

    Povere giovanette italiane, dall'animo aperto ad ogni cosa bella e gentile; e voi madri, aristocratiche o popolane, che passaste, con comandata lentezza, tra due file di sgherri, calanti, a tutta forza, il bastone sulle nude membra, prestatemi voi la voce che basti ad una degna apoteosi dell'apostolica belva!

    E di proposito accenno a Francesco Giuseppe e non all'Austria; perchè quando la somma del potere è concentrata in un uomo, e sul suo capo, per debito di giustizia, va concentrata, del pari, la somma dell'odio e dell'infamia.

    Frammisti agli Italiani trovai, nelle mie peregrinazioni, parecchi ufficiali ungheresi degradati perchè avevano amato la Patria e l'avevano difesa. Vessati con feroce pertinacia, avevano, ciò nulla meno, il coraggio di ribellarsi ai comandi odiosi impartiti a danno di un Popolo affratellato dalla sventura. E fu, tra loro, chi, disperando di tempi migliori, mise fine, col suicidio, ad una vita diventata insopportabile.

    Fortunatamente, quasi a deludere i prevaricatori, Natura ha disposto le sue cose in modo che la morte stessa è la grande seminatrice della vita e la stessa tirannide la grande fecondatrice d'ogni libertà!

    Malgrado le disfatte e il sangue sottrattole e le persecuzioni, la vita pulsava, nella Patria nostra, sotterranea, ma intensa. Italiani che si organizzavano, che cospiravano, o che palesavano la febbrile impazienza di farlo, ne trovai a dovizia in ogni centro. La deduzione logica tratta dalla orribile situazione, era che bisognava tornare daccapo e far meglio e vincere ad ogni costo.

    Source pp. 53–56Scan to view on another deviceIN APRIL of 1852 I was once again in Genoa, working for a certain Ferrari, a merchant tailor, when I was informed that Don Bastiano Barozzi, parish priest of San Pietro in Campo, was living as a refugee in Turin. Barozzi, during the siege of Venice, had served as chaplain to a Legion composed almost exclusively of students from Padua. He was one of those priests in whom the cassock did not stifle the love of the Fatherland and who, to passive obedience, preferred to follow the impulses of their own conscience. Italy boasts a good number of such men, and the Church of Rome might have drawn great prestige from them, had she not preferred, in order to please the powerful, to hear their confessions and even lend a hand in their torment.

    Deeply versed in ancient history, Don Bastiano judged men through the prism of the Roman world; to the level of Attilio Regolo, of the Scipios, of the Gracchi he would have wished to raise our entire race. During my school holidays, while walking with me along the Ardo and without seeming to make anything of it, he would steep my mind in heroic episodes. I believe that I owe to him, in no small part, my devotion to Liberty.

    The priests of murky waters, who dared not wage open war upon him for his patriotic principles, dragged in the Decretals and took exception to his mustache and his goatee, which smacked of the modernist. He stubbornly refused to have them shaved off, and for it he lost the right to say Mass. In his straitened circumstances, he was aided by my father, and that alone had been sufficient for Don Bastiano to cherish towards him a gratitude that bordered on veneration.

    It was to this man that I resolved to turn, in the hope of re-entering, with his help, the vortex of action, and thinking that two words spoken face to face are worth more than four long letters, I set off for Turin… on foot, just so as not to lose the habit.

    My first care, upon arriving there, was to make my way to the Caffè Nazionale, the favored haunt of political refugees. Before I reached it, however, I caught sight of a gentleman standing planted before the playbill of a theater, his face so close to the print that he seemed to be reading it with the tip of his nose. Don Bastiano Barozzi's extreme short-sightedness was known to me, and that single clue was enough to reveal him.

    Great was his astonishment at finding me; boundless his joy. He could not contain it and, there and then, without the least regard for the passers-by, pressed two resounding kisses upon my cheeks: "Here... this on my account; this on your father's account!" He then asked me what lucky wind had brought me to Turin. And I:

    "Don Bastiano, you know that the di Rudios are not cowards. What is being contemplated, what is being prepared? I have come so as not to grow rusty, and I am counting upon you."

    He lowered his eyes and did not answer. But, after a brief silence, as though concluding his own thoughts: "Fortunato Calvi is here. In a few leaps, we can be at his house. Come!"

    Fortunato Calvi, the glorious organizer of the Cacciatori delle Alpi, my Colonel, the intimate friend of my family, received me with folded arms, without moving a step, with an expression that was almost one of disdain. The flood of affection that was on the point of bursting forth remained painfully compressed in my breast. I did not even dare to provoke an explanation. But the explanation came of its own accord.

    Calvi had been informed by a person "worthy of belief" that I, having left Venice upon his orders, instead of proceeding to Chioggia, had gone over, with arms and baggage, to the Austrian. He even cited the name of one who had sworn to have seen me, behind the trenches, firing upon my own countrymen.

    Calumny! — what man has not been, to some degree or another, its victim? Little by little, the breast hardens to its blows so that in time they no longer avail to stir any storm within me. I know bold men who, by their very bearing, almost provoke it of set purpose, for the acrid pleasure of showing themselves superior. But that was the first time I had found myself face to face with it, and its bite was extremely cruel to me.

    Nevertheless, I found it easy to demonstrate its absurdity.

    Fortunato Calvi, who had no less a desire to find me innocent than I had to prove it to him, smilingly extended his right hand, which I clasped with feverish emotion. While Don Bastiano, mute witness to that scene and a man of exceptionally sensitive feeling, turned upon his heel to conceal a tear.

    The consequences of that conversation were, as far as I was concerned, of no slight moment. Giuseppe Mazzini, Luigi Kossuth and Ledru Rollin had openly founded, in London, a European Revolutionary Committee, to which innumerable branches were soon attached. Each People that, having vanquished that nefarious apathy — the cryptogam of souls — understood how regeneration springs from agitation and sacrifice, organized its own. Italy was a hive of them, and by force of circumstance the Committee of Turin held predominance over all the others. Upon it fell, above all, the task of inciting the spirit of rebellion that snaked through the Peninsula and of co-ordinating its movements. Its head, in 1852, was Count Grillanzoni; but the command of eventual military operations fell to Fortunato Calvi.

    From Calvi and Count Grillanzoni I received the commission of traversing, as special envoy, the principal cities of Lombardy-Venetia; first to distribute subversive manifestos and bonds of the Mazzinian national loan; then, on a subsequent journey, to inspect the various garrisons and report precise particulars of their strength, their nationality, and their temper.

    The system adopted was simple. A passport was procured for me in the name of Carlo Moretti, a silk merchant. It sufficed for me to write: In such-and-such a place I have sold two, three, four bales of white, or green, or yellow silk. The number of bales corresponded to the number of Regiments; the color indicated their uniform and arm of service. A studied allusion to the particular quality of the merchandise, or to the character of the customer, sufficed to convey whatever other circumstances I deemed it necessary to bring to notice.

    I recall the admonition Calvi gave me on the very day of my departure. I was concealing in the stem of my pipe the list of the various Sub-Committees, and I was smiling. Calvi suspected that smile to be the fruit of levity and said sternly: "Do not delude yourself, young man! A slight imprudence, a simple fortuitous incident, and you would be hanged!" I did not fall into the gallows' hands, but I passed within very close reach of them more than once.

    My first halt was at Stradella, where I shook hands with Nino Bixio — who had survived the wound sustained at Villa Corsini — and with that Winkler who had been, at Venice, the commander of a Hungarian legion, and who was to close, a few years later, his heroic existence fighting on the banks of the Volturno. Contact with men of such mettle was a source of pleasant excitement for me. But having crossed, with the help of a smuggler, the blue Ticino and ventured but a little way into the domains of Austria, it seemed as if an iron hand were closing around my throat. The oppressor had, after 1849, sharpened his sluggish wits to transform those lands into a pit of hell.

    As a general rule, Austria regarded the Provinces subdued by her own arms, and the others reconquered by the allied arms of Russia, as a den of brigands. To vent upon them a barbaric hatred, to gratify the most atrocious vengeances — this appeared, in courtly spheres, the height of political wisdom. Persuaded that he was carrying out a mission entrusted to him by God, Francesco Giuseppe did not even consider himself bound by his own word or by the capitulations he had stipulated. General Klapka, the heroic defender of Komorn, had obtained excellent terms of surrender for the garrison. Every one of them was violated. A code of honor exists even among thieves; but under the pretext of reasons of State, none such existed for Francesco Giuseppe.

    Of the thirty-four Hungarian generals — chosen minds, heroic fibers — who had repeatedly compelled the Imperial troops to show their backs, three were condemned to perpetual imprisonment, twelve were with Christian charity shot or hanged. And the carnage would have been greater, had the others not saved themselves by flight and exile. For several months a swarm of executioners, clad — as though in crowning mockery — in the color of hope, busied themselves in Hungary heaping up the corpses of patriots. All this now seems blotted from the memory of those who shout in the streets their cheers for the contemptible old man. And yet one never ceases to curse the generous souls who, having taken into their own hearts the anguish of the victims, hazard their lives to administer to despots a measure of their own medicine.

    In Italy, it is true, Francesco Giuseppe had his talons blunted by Vittorio Emanuele II who, having ascended the throne amid the glacial distrust of his subjects, knew how to bow to the terrible necessities pressing upon him without compromising the future. He refused to sign the peace treaty with Austria unless she undertook the obligation of granting full amnesty to the hundred thousand Italians who, from Lombardy-Venetia, had taken refuge in Piedmont, whether to make war upon her or to escape her persecutions.

    Vittorio Emanuele II disposed, even in defeat, of a force not to be lightly reckoned with, and the Emperor kept the pact. Nevertheless, his "pardon" was tacitly conditional upon complete submission to the Austrian yoke. Were pretexts ever lacking? Sequestrations, arbitrary impositions, chains and the gallows were not doled out sparingly by the paternal solicitude of Francesco Giuseppe, and the rod was applied to the backs even of those, men or women, who, without rebelling, gave expression to manly sentiments.

    Poor young Italian women, your souls open to every fair and gentle thing; and you mothers, aristocratic or of the people, who were made to pass, with commanded slowness, between two files of hirelings bringing down their cudgels with full force upon your bare limbs — lend me, you, the voice sufficient for a worthy apotheosis of the apostolic beast!

    And I name Francesco Giuseppe deliberately, and not Austria; because when the sum of power is concentrated in one man, upon his head, by equal right of justice, must likewise be concentrated the sum of hatred and of infamy.

    Mingled among the Italians, I encountered in my wanderings a good number of Hungarian officers, degraded because they had loved their Fatherland and defended it. Harassed with ferocious persistence, they nonetheless had the courage to rebel against the odious commands issued to the detriment of a People bound to them by common misfortune. And there were those among them who, despairing of better times, put an end, by suicide, to a life that had become unendurable.

    Fortunately, as though to confound the oppressors, Nature has so disposed her affairs that death itself is the great sower of life, and tyranny itself the great fecundator of every liberty!

    and the bloodletting, andRestores Gemini's "the blood drawn from it"; the clitic -le in "sottrattole" means the blood was drawn FROM the Fatherland, a relational sense the generic "bloodletting" drops.Despite the defeats, and the blood drawn from it, and the persecutions, life pulsed in our Fatherland — underground, but intense. Italians who were organizing, who were conspiring, or who betrayed the feverish impatience to do so, I found in abundance in every center. The logical conclusion drawn from the dreadful situation was that one must begin again from the beginning, do better, and conquer at any cost.

    Parte Prima

    Capitolo Dodicesimo

    Part One

    Chapter Twelve

    Della mia escursione che si spinse, per ben due volte, sino alla remota Udine, narrerò, in succinto, qualche episodio.

    Delusa la polizia a Milano, a Como, a Bergamo, a Brescia, mi avvicinavo a Mantova, con una grande sicurezza d'animo... mi affidava la certezza di non esservi conosciuto da anima viva. Ma proprio mentre passavo il dazio, violento come un colpo d'ariete, mi sentii lanciare in pieno petto, il mio nome e cognome.

    Lì per lì, mi ritenni scoperto e perduto. Ma l'uomo che mi stava di fronte aveva il volto sorridente e la sua destra, stesa, invitava la mia. Era lo stesso capo delle guardie. Dove l'avessi conosciuto, ora non rammento... era un patriota addentro nelle cose segrete.

    Quando seppe che non ero a Mantova per cambiare aria, si rabbuiò. Proprio il giorno innanzi, la polizia aveva praticato vari arresti e se ne ignorava il perchè... egli intravide un nesso tra quei rigori inaspettati ed il mio arrivo. Mi consigliò di riparare immediatamente a casa sua e di rimanervi rinchiuso. Egli si sarebbe industriato di scoprire terreno e, a sera, mangiando un boccone, si delibererebbe sul da farsi.

    Provocata dalla congiura, in cui primeggia la mite figura del prete Tazzoli, si iniziava proprio allora, a Mantova, la serie delle raffinate torture che sospinse a morte inumana il manipolo dei martiri di Belfiore. Ma, rientrando a tarda sera, il mio ospite non seppe recarmi se non notizie vaghe, e con esse un soffio del terrore che incombeva sulla città. Non si era imbattuto in fratello che si sentisse sicuro di poter passare la notte nel proprio letto.

    Mi coricai in soffitta, alla meglio, senza svestirmi... preparato a tentare, al minimo allarme, la fuga su pei tetti.

    Di buon mattino, interrogando a lungo il mio ospite, seppi che, tra la guarnigione, era un ufficiale tirato su nell'Accademia Militare di Milano e condiscepolo mio: i legami della prima giovinezza non si sgualciscono per passare degli anni. Mezz'ora dopo, ero in Castello e davo e ricevevo un abbraccio fraterno, o giù di lì. Ero sotto la protezione dell'aquila bicipite.

    Per spiegare la mia presenza, mi dissi avviato a Belluno per assistere papà, aggravato dagli anni... avevo fatto una punta sino a Mantova, appositamente per rivedere l'amico; per rievocare, in sua compagnia, l'età della felice spensieratezza. Come per tacita intesa, ci guardammo dal toccare argomenti d'indole politica; ma nella mezza baldoria organizzata per farmi festa, non persi di vista lo scopo del mio viaggio ed ebbi, a bocconi ed a spizzico, quante informazioni cercavo. Due ore prima del tramonto, l'ufficiale stesso cortesemente si offerse di accompagnarmi, in carrozza, sin oltre le mura. E, sotto l'egida sua, passai il dazio, senza molestie, salutato militarmente.

    Senonché sfuggito ad un pericolo probabilmente immaginario, dovetti affrontarne uno anche troppo reale, a Verona.

    Avevo preso alloggio allo "Scudo di Francia" e, serrato appena nella mia camera, feci istintivamente un po' d'ispezione. Era situata al terzo piano; l'unica finestra dava su un cortile. Di fronte avevo la parete posteriore d'un altro albergo, quello della "Regina d'Inghilterra." Piccando il viso in giù, scorsi una larga tettoia che declinava verso il centro del cortile. Un tanfo di stalla saliva sin lassù; udii lo scalpitare d'un cavallo.

    Sedetti al tavolino, presso il caminetto acceso e incominciai a scrivere il mio rapporto su Mantova. D'improvviso, risuonarono nel corridoio passi cadenzati. Soffiai sulla candela e gettai al fuoco la mia lettera che, carbonizzata, volò su per la cappa.

    Fu battuto all'uscio ed una voce ossequiosa, quella di un inserviente: "Signor Moretti, favorisca!" Dopo breve silenzio, un'altra voce burbera ed imperiosa: "In nome della Legge, aprite!"

    Avevo scavalcato il parapetto e vi stavo aggrappato a braccia tese, per diminuire la distanza che mi separava dalla tettoia. I gendarmi incominciarono a battere l'uscio col calcio del fucile... allentai le mani e mi lasciai precipitare, rasente il muro. Il colpo fu rude, ma il disastro toccò alle tegole. Lungo esse strisciai e giunto all'orlo, approfittai d'una trave sporgente e di quel tanto di ginnastica acconsentitami dalle membra indolenzite, per porre la grondaia fra me ed i birri. Accovacciato là sotto, udii l'uscio della mia camera cedere con uno schianto... poi un calpestìo precipitoso, poi... silenzio! poi una voce vibrante: "Sergente, scommetto che è saltato dalla finestra!" E il sergente, che la sapeva lunga: "Pezzo d'asino! Non si salta da un terzo piano!"

    Credo che neppur s'affacciassero al davanzale. Il cortile era deserto. Dall'uscio socchiuso della stalla, si proiettava una striscia di luce. Essa mi rivelò un mucchio di letame. Vi saltai...

    Poco dopo, entravo risolutamente nella cucina della "Regina d'Inghilterra:" sguatteri ed inservienti, affaccendati, non alzarono neppure il capo. Un cuoco, la grossa epa avvolta nel grembiale bianco, il bianco berretto sul capo, soffiava, in quel momento, sul suo mestolo... Mi guardò stupefatto; ma non si era riavuto dalla sorpresa e già infilavo l'uscio della sala da pranzo. I camerieri mi ritennero probabilmente per l'amico di qualche sguattero. In pochi secondi mi trovai all'aperto.

    Dove passare la notte? La Polizia, messa in maggiore sospetto dalla mia scomparsa, non mi avrebbe permesso di trovare riposo in qualsiasi albergo. Gironzolai alla deriva, sinché capitai di fronte alla Posta. Vi stavano allineate alcune diligenze, collo sportello spalancato... in una d'esse, mi gettai sdraiato e vi stetti accovacciato sino al sopraggiungere dell'alba. Più volte, fra il sonno e la veglia, udii passarmi ai piedi la ronda. Se avessi russato, con tutta probabilità, non sarei qui a raccontare quella mia avventura.

    Un pericolo più indiavolato ancora mi procurai, per eccesso di zelo, al mio ritorno a Milano. Per consiglio di un tal Valentini, studente in chimica, alloggiavo, durante il mio soggiorno nella capitale lombarda, in casa della sua innamorata; la quale campava coi proventi di una sua bottega da lattivendola e tenendo pensione. Alla mensa intervenivano parecchie guardie di finanza ed un sergente ungherese, ostinato tormentatore di pianoforte. Mi pareva d'essere come trincerato nello stesso campo nemico.

    La lattivendola, donna in carne, con una faccia da panettone fresco, era di sentimenti italianissimi tanto che, nella mia visita precedente, le avevo consegnato, perchè lo distruggesse, un pacco di manifestini rivoluzionari, diventato superfluo. Ora ella mi annuncia d'averlo conservato, intatto, in fondo ad un cassettone. Me ne rallegrai... avrei potuto, prima di rientrare nel Piemonte, dare, con una buona affissione, l'ultimo tocco all'opera mia.

    Ne parlai al Comitato Rivoluzionario che, irritatissimo, in quei giorni, per le pessime notizie giuntegli da Mantova, approvò la mia determinazione. E tra quei patrioti, trovai anche chi, spontaneo, si offerse di prestarmi aiuto... un operaio tarchiato, con una larga cicatrice sul volto. Non mi disse il suo nome; era tappezziere, come il povero Antonio Sciesa.

    Una notte fredda, piovosa, deserte le vie. Incominciammo dalla località detta il Carrobbio; io su un marciapiede, egli sull'altro... appiccicavamo il nostro proclama con quelle piccole ostie che i Milanesi chiamano "obiadìtt". Eravamo diretti verso il Duomo e già vedevo biancheggiare la facciata del magnifico tempio, quando dall'altro lato della piazza, e precisamente sotto il Coperto dei Figini, risuonò, nel silenzio, il terribile ammonimento: "Scappa, scappa!"

    Vidi la figura tarchiata del tappezziere fuggire a gambe levate, verso piazza Mercanti e una ronda di poliziotti, sbucata probabilmente da qualche andito oscuro, lo inseguiva vociando. Se non era la sua fraterna sollecitudine, forse me la sarei cavata senza dare nell'occhio; ma lanciandomi quel suo grido, egli aveva messo sull'avvisato anche i birri; buon numero dei quali si avanzò a passo di corsa, alla mia volta.

    Non c'erano alternative. Gettai i manifestini e via a precipizio per un dedalo di arterie anguste, finché trafelato, mi trovai, erta di fronte, la linea delle mura. Salii la rampa, carponi; ma cogli inseguitori alle calcagna e un dazio, con rispettivo corpo di guardia, a destra ed a sinistra, potevo considerarmi in trappola. Temporaneo riparo ebbi dietro uno di quei grossi ippocastani e, per un momento, mi lusingai che, incerti della direzione da me presa, i maledetti ritornassero scornati sui loro passi; o, saliti sul bastione, pigliassero tutti a destra, lasciandomi libero di squagliarmela silenziosamente a sinistra. Ma non furono tanto gonzi.

    Giunti lassù, essi si divisero in due gruppi che, voltandosi le spalle, procedettero contemporaneamente d'ambo i lati, osservando con cautela dietro ogni albero. Come a Verona mi trovai nella terribile alternativa o di assoggettarmi, più tardi, ad un piccolo salto che, in virtù del capestro, non avrebbe rimedio, o d'affrontare i pericoli immediati di un gran salto, che pure mi lasciava qualche percentuale di scampo. Mi decisi pel gran salto.

    Quanto tempo rimanessi stordito là in fondo, a dir vero, non seppi mai. Fortunatamente avevo battuto su terreno molle e forse, a rimettermi ritto, valse la pioggia che cadeva come se me la rovesciassero addosso. Mi incamminai, penosamente e tutto inzuppato, verso Pavia.

    In quale stato vi giunsi? Non è difficile l'immaginarlo. Tre giorni sotto coperte ed ero di bel nuovo in assetto. Il fratello della Giovine Italia che mi aveva amorosamente assistito, avrebbe voluto che mi fermassi più a lungo; ma la terra di salvazione era troppo vicina perchè non mi sentissi la febbre di rimettervi il piede. Mi accompagnò egli sin presso il ponte che attraversa il Ticino e che congiunge la riva lombarda alla piemontese e ci separammo.

    Ed ecco due, tre, una folla di spiritati correre rumorosamente all'imboccatura del ponte. Dalle frasi che si scambiavano ad alta voce, compresi che uno sconosciuto, qualche contrabbandiere, o qualche "testa calda" inseguito da una pattuglia, si era gettato dal parapetto. Lo avevano colpito al volo con una schioppettata nella schiena ed ora si industriavano di pescarlo.

    Girai al largo e trovato, collo spacciarmi per disertore, il modo di farmi traghettare, giungevo sul far della mezzanotte a Stradella. Casa Winkler era tuttora illuminata. Vi entrai e mi trovai dinanzi un gruppo di patrioti che mi accolsero come se piovessi dalle nuvole. Un messaggio segreto, giunto da Pavia, aveva recato la nuova che ero stato scoperto, inseguito, mortalmente ferito mentre cercavo scampo saltando nel Ticino. Fortunato Calvi già n'era telegraficamente informato.

    Ripartii immediatamente e trovai il Calvi ancora a letto. Non appena si rimise dalla sorpresa, il suo primo pensiero fu per don Bastiano Barozzi, a cui aveva già dato appuntamento, per le nove, al caffé del Pantheon. Il buon prete vi entrò pallido e quasi stravolto. La sua miopia gli impedì di scorgermi e il Calvi gli mosse incontro. Vedendolo esclamò: "Ah quale sciagura! Che dirà il mio benefattore quando saprà che l'ho ricompensato spingendo suo figlio alla perdizione?"

    — E che diresti tu, se Carlo entrasse qui dentro, sano e sorridente?

    La faccia del Barozzi ebbe moti convulsi. Ad un tratto, il groppo che lo strozzava si sciolse ed egli diede in uno scoppio di pianto.

    Allora l'ateo saltò al collo del sacerdote. Il che tende a dimostrare che l'ipotesi di Dio non è necessaria agli uomini per amarsi fraternamente!

    Source pp. 57–60Scan to view on another deviceOf my excursion, which extended, on two separate occasions, as far as the remote Udine, I shall recount in brief some few episodes.

    Gemini Promy full name was hurled squarely at my chestThe Italian 'mi sentii lanciare' is reflexive — 'I felt [it] hurled at me' — not a passive 'was hurled'. Gemini's 'I felt' restores the first-person sensation of the original.Edgren 1901dazio — custom (duty; toll); custom-placeEdgren's 'custom-place' confirmed that 'dazio' denotes the physical checkpoint, not just the duty owed. This supported 'customs-post' (a period-appropriate compound) over the Gemini draft's 'custom-house' or a modern 'toll-booth'.Having baffled the police at Milan, at Como, at Bergamo, at Brescia, I was drawing near to Mantova with a great confidence of spirit... I was reassured by the certainty of not being known there by a living soul. But at the very moment I was passing through the customs-post, violent as a battering-ram's blow, I felt my full name hurled squarely at my chest.

    In that instant I believed myself discovered and lost. But the man who stood before me wore a smiling countenance, and his right hand, extended, invited mine. It was none other than the chief of the guards himself. Where I had made his acquaintance I can no longer recall... he was a patriot deep in secret affairs.

    When he learned that I was not in Mantova for a change of air, his face darkened. The very day before, the police had carried out a number of arrests, the reason for which remained unknown... he glimpsed a connection between those unexpected severities and my arrival. He advised me to take refuge immediately in his house and remain shut within it. He would endeavor to sound out the ground, and in the evening, over a bite to eat, we should deliberate on what was to be done.

    Edgren 1901mite — mild (clement, merciful, gentle)A modern default for 'mite' might be 'mild' or 'meek'. Edgren's gloss includes 'gentle', which fits the collocation 'gentle figure' more naturally than 'mild figure' and avoids the connotation of passivity in 'meek'.Provoked by the conspiracy in which the gentle figure of the priest Tazzoli plays a leading part, there was beginning at that very moment, in Mantova, the series of refined tortures that drove to an inhuman death the little band of the martyrs of Belfiore. But, returning late in the evening, my host could bring me nothing save vague tidings, and with them a breath of the terror that hung over the city. He had not encountered a single brother who felt secure of being able to pass the night in his own bed.

    I lay down in the attic as best I could, without undressing... ready to attempt, at the slightest alarm, a flight across the rooftops.

    Early the following morning, after a long conversation with my host, I learned that among the garrison there was an officer who had been educated at the Military Academy of Milan and had been a fellow-pupil of mine: the bonds of earliest youth do not grow worn with the passing of years. Half an hour later I was in the Castello, exchanging a fraternal embrace, or something very like it. I was under the protection of the double-headed eagle.

    Gemini Prosaluted with military honoursThe Italian 'salutato militarmente' means he received a salute; 'military honours' inflates the sense beyond what 'militarmente' conveys.To explain my presence, I gave it out that I was bound for Belluno to attend upon my father, weighed down by years... I had made a detour to Mantova expressly to see my old friend again; to evoke, in his company, the age of happy carelessness. As by tacit understanding, we each took care not to touch upon subjects of a political nature; yet in the half-festivity that was organized in my honor, I never lost sight of the purpose of my journey, and gleaned, in snatches and by degrees, all the information I was seeking. Two hours before sunset, the officer himself courteously offered to accompany me, by carriage, beyond the city walls. And under his protection I passed through the customs-post without hindrance, receiving a military salute.

    However, having escaped one danger that was in all likelihood imaginary, I was compelled to face one all too real at Verona.

    I had taken lodgings at the "Scudo di Francia," and scarcely had I shut myself into my room than I made, by instinct, a small inspection of it. It was situated on the third floor; the single window gave onto a courtyard. Opposite me lay the rear wall of another hotel, the "Regina d'Inghilterra." Craning my face downward, I made out a broad penthouse roof that sloped toward the center of the courtyard. A smell of the stable rose up to that height; I heard the stamping of a horse.

    I sat down at the little table beside the lit fireplace and began to write my report on Mantova. All at once, measured footsteps resounded in the corridor. I blew out the candle and cast my letter into the fire, where it turned to ash and flew up through the flue.

    There came a knock at the door and an obsequious voice, that of a servant: "Signor Moretti, if you please!" After a brief silence, another voice, gruff and imperious: "In the name of the Law, open up!"

    I had already swung myself over the parapet and was clinging there with arms fully extended, so as to diminish the distance between myself and the penthouse roof. The gendarmes began to beat the door with the butts of their rifles... I loosened my grip and let myself drop, close against the wall. The shock was severe, but the damage fell to the roof-tiles. Along them I slid, and reaching the edge, availed myself of a projecting beam and of such gymnastics as my aching limbs permitted, in order to place the gutter between myself and the constables. Crouching beneath it, I heard the door of my room give way with a crash... then a hasty trampling... then silence... then a ringing voice: "Sergeant, I'll wager he jumped from the window!" And the sergeant, who knew better: "Fool! One doesn't jump from a third floor!"

    I doubt they even leaned over the sill. The courtyard was deserted. From the half-open stable door a strip of light was cast. It revealed to me a dung-heap. I leapt into it...

    Gemini Prohis great belly wrapped in a white apron'Epa' specifically means paunch (a protruding gut), not just belly. Gemini's 'paunch' is the more precise anatomical register.A little later I walked resolutely into the kitchen of the "Regina d'Inghilterra:" scullions and servants, busy about their work, did not so much as raise their heads. A cook, his great paunch wrapped in a white apron, a white cap on his head, was blowing on his ladle at that very moment... He stared at me in stupefaction; but he had not yet recovered from his surprise when I was already making for the door of the dining room. The waiters took me in all probability for some scullion's friend. In a few seconds I found myself in the open air.

    Where to pass the night? The police, rendered more suspicious still by my disappearance, would not have permitted me to find rest in any hotel. I wandered adrift until I found myself in front of the Post. Several stage-coaches stood there in a row with their doors flung wide... into one of them I threw myself at full length and crouched there until the approach of dawn. More than once, between sleep and waking, I heard the night-watch pass beneath my feet. Had I snored, in all probability I should not be here to tell that adventure of mine.

    Gemini ProSeveral customs officers attended her table'Guardie di finanza' are finance guards (fiscal police), not customs officers. They are a distinct corps; Gemini's literal rendering is historically accurate.A yet more devilish peril I brought upon myself, through excess of zeal, on my return to Milan. On the advice of a certain Valentini, a student of chemistry, I had lodged, during my stays in the Lombard capital, at the home of his sweetheart; a woman who lived from the proceeds of a little dairy-shop she kept, and by taking in boarders. Several finance guards attended her table, as did a Hungarian sergeant, a relentless tormentor of the pianoforte. I felt as though I were entrenched within the very enemy's camp.

    The dairywoman, a fleshy creature with a face like a fresh panettone, was of the most ardently Italian sympathies, so much so that on my previous visit I had entrusted to her, for destruction, a bundle of revolutionary pamphlets that had become superfluous. Now she informed me that she had preserved it intact, at the bottom of a chest of drawers. I rejoiced at the news... before returning to Piedmont I might, with a good posting of bills, put the finishing touch to my work.

    Alternative drafthe was a decorator, like the poor Antonio SciesaNeither draft was used. Claude had 'decorator', Gemini had 'tapestry-maker'. The Italian 'tappezziere' most accurately means upholsterer — and Antonio Sciesa is historically documented as an upholsterer. Both drafts were imprecise.I spoke of it to the Revolutionary Committee, which, exasperated in those days by the wretched news arrived from Mantova, approved my resolution. And among those patriots I found one who, of his own accord, offered to lend me his assistance... a stocky workman, with a broad scar upon his face. He told me not his name; he was an upholsterer, like the poor Antonio Sciesa.

    Gemini Prothe façade of the magnificent church gleaming white before meThe Italian is 'magnifico tempio'. Crespi deliberately chose the classicising 'tempio' over 'chiesa'; 'temple' preserves that rhetorical elevation.A cold, rainy night, the streets deserted. We began at the locality known as the Carrobbio; I on one pavement, he on the other... we pasted up our proclamation with those small wafers which the Milanese call obiadìtt. We were heading toward the Duomo, and already I could see the façade of the magnificent temple gleaming white before me, when from the far side of the square, and precisely beneath the Coperto dei Figini, there rang out in the silence that terrible warning: "Run, run!"

    I saw the stocky figure of the upholsterer flee at full speed toward the Piazza Mercanti, and a patrol of policemen, bursting forth from some dark alleyway, gave chase with shouts. Had it not been for his fraternal solicitude, I might perhaps have slipped away without attracting notice; but by launching that cry of his, he had put the constables on the alert as well; and a goodly number of them advanced toward me at a run.

    There were no alternatives. I flung down the pamphlets and fled headlong through a labyrinth of narrow streets, until, breathless, I found myself confronted with the line of the city walls rearing up before me. I climbed the ramp on all fours; but with my pursuers at my heels and a customs-post, with its respective guard-house, to right and to left, I might consider myself trapped. Momentary shelter I found behind one of those great horse-chestnut trees, and for an instant I flattered myself that, uncertain of the direction I had taken, the wretches would turn back, crestfallen, upon their steps; or that, having gained the rampart, they would all veer to the right, leaving me free to steal away silently to the left. But they were not so simple-minded.

    Having reached the top, they divided into two groups which, turning their backs to one another, advanced simultaneously on either side, peering with caution behind every tree. As at Verona, I found myself in the terrible alternative of submitting, sooner or later, to a small jump from which, by virtue of the noose, there would be no recovery, or of braving the immediate dangers of a great jump, which still left me some measure of a chance. I resolved upon the great jump.

    How long I lay stunned down there at the bottom, in truth I never knew. Fortunately I had fallen upon soft ground, and perhaps it was the rain, which fell as though poured over me bodily, that served to bring me back to my feet. I set out, painfully and soaked to the bone, toward Pavia.

    Gemini ProI was once again in order'Di bel nuovo in assetto' has a colloquial jauntiness that 'in order' misses. Gemini's 'in fine fettle' captures the idiomatic tone better.In what condition did I arrive there? It is not difficult to imagine. Three days under covers and I was once again in fine fettle. The brother of the Giovine Italia who had tended me with such care was desirous that I should remain longer; but the land of salvation was too near for me not to feel the fever to set foot on it once more. He accompanied me as far as the bridge that crosses the Ticino and joins the Lombard bank to the Piedmontese, and there we parted.

    Edgren 1901colpo — blow (stroke): colpo di fucile, gunshotThe Italian 'schioppettata' (from 'schioppo', musket) needed a period-appropriate English equivalent. Edgren's entry for the related 'colpo di fucile' = 'gunshot' confirmed this as the natural 1900-era English, avoiding a modern 'rifle shot' or 'bullet'.And then — two, three, a crowd of wild-eyed people came running noisily to the mouth of the bridge. From the phrases they exchanged at full voice, I understood that an unknown man, some smuggler or some hot-head pursued by a patrol, had thrown himself from the parapet. They had struck him mid-flight with a gunshot in the back, and were now endeavoring to fish him out.

    I skirted a wide berth, and, having found a way to get myself ferried across by passing myself off as a deserter, I arrived, as midnight was approaching, at Stradella. The Casa Winkler was still lit. I entered and found before me a group of patriots who received me as though I had fallen from the clouds. A secret message, arrived from Pavia, had brought the news that I had been discovered, pursued, and mortally wounded while seeking escape by leaping into the Ticino. Fortunato Calvi had already been informed of it by telegraph.

    I departed immediately and found Calvi still in bed. Barely recovered from his surprise, his first thought was for don Bastiano Barozzi, with whom he had already arranged to meet at nine o'clock at the Caffè del Pantheon. The good priest entered it pale and almost beside himself. His short-sightedness prevented him from seeing me, and Calvi went to meet him. Catching sight of Calvi, he exclaimed: "Ah, what a calamity! What will my benefactor say when he learns that I repaid him by driving his son to ruin?"

    — And what would you say, if Carlo were to walk in here, sound and smiling?

    The face of Barozzi underwent convulsive movements. All at once, the knot that was strangling him loosened, and he burst into a flood of tears.

    Then the atheist threw himself upon the neck of the priest. Which tends to demonstrate that the hypothesis of God is not necessary to men in order that they may love one another as brothers!

    Parte Prima

    Capitolo Tredicesimo

    Part One

    Chapter Thirteen

    NON conosco moto insurrezionale italiano che sia valso a Giuseppe Mazzini tante calunnie, tante amarezze, tante defezioni quanto quello del 1853.

    La gente così detta positiva, di quel positivismo miope che consiste nel lasciare il mondo correre indisturbato magari alla propria rovina, sosteneva che un Popolo di recente dissanguato e rotto, non poteva rispondere animosamente ad un appello alle armi; sobbarcarsi volonteroso a nuovi sacrifici. E sentenziava, con tutta serietà, che le rivoluzioni si fanno quando spontaneamente maturano, non quando si vuole.

    Massima singolare che, sviscerata, condurrebbe a quest'altra: "Per acquistare la propria indipendenza, per rivendicare i propri diritti, nulla di meglio dello sdraiarsi in poltrona, lasciando al Sole la cura di maturare gli eventi, come matura le zucche."

    I moti organizzati da Giuseppe Mazzini non furono mai né la rivoluzione vera, né la guerra; ma essi indubbiamente le prepararono e le resero necessarie.

    D'altra parte, quelli del Febbraio 1853 non vennero iniziati dal grande agitatore; ma appunto da quel Popolo che, se si prestasse fede agli uomini seri, di null'altro sarebbe stato curante se non di acconciarsi un giaciglio nei fatti compiuti e nella propria miseria.

    E lo ha pur detto il Mazzini e lo ha scritto e lo ha provato. Ma anche oggigiorno abbondano i superuomini, lustro e decoro dell'Italia nostra; i quali, non potendo confutare le dichiarazioni di quel grande che non ha mai mentito, le saltano maestrevolmente di piè pari e continuano ad esaltare i portenti della poltrona, a persistere nelle vecchie calunnie.

    La "Fratellanza", società segreta mirante all'insurrezione, era già fortemente organizzata, a Milano, tre anni dopo che ogni speranza d'Italia pareva avvizzita. Composta da alcune migliaia d'operai, era essa suddivisa in varie sezioni contrassegnate dalle lettere dell'alfabeto. La sua esistenza era assolutamente ignorata dalla Polizia austriaca, dal Mazzini, dagli intellettuali, dalla classe aristocratica, che pure aveva comune con essa l'odio del giogo straniero.

    Il suo organizzatore principale, il tintore Assi, si mise in comunicazione col Mazzini quando stimò che la "Fratellanza" avesse forza sufficiente ad agire. L'Assi chiese armi, danari e capi atti a condurre a buon fine un piano d'attacco.

    Il Mazzini accolse la nuova come una prova che la propaganda delle sue idee aveva sviluppato buoni germi anche tra la moltitudine popolare; ma non prestò piena fede alle assicurazioni dell'Assi sull'importanza numerica e sulla serietà d'intenti della nuova organizzazione, sinché il maggiore Eugenio Brizzi da Jesi ed altri informatori, da lui inviati segretamente a Milano, non glie le ebbero confermate in tutto e per tutto.

    Tuttavia, egli consigliava di temporeggiare, quando nel Dicembre del 1852, l'Austria dando nuovo sfogo alla propria ferocia, coronava di forche gli spalti di Belfiore e, scelta una vittima tra ogni classe di cittadini, ve l'appendeva.

    Di fronte alla truce provocazione, parve agli operai della "Fratellanza" delitto il discutere più a lungo. Essi notificarono al Mazzini d'aver deciso di agire, anche senza il suo aiuto e senza il suo consenso. Dovere imprescindibile del Mazzini divenne quello di assecondare l'inevitabile, di adoperarsi perchè la generosa iniziativa, non rimanesse isolata e sterile.

    Gli ultimi del Dicembre 1852, per incarico avuto direttamente dal Conte Grillanzoni, io partivo per Milano e la mezzanotte del giorno in cui arrivai, dopo breve attesa sotto l'orologio di piazza Mercanti, consegnavo un plico di istruzioni ad un uomo che, sbucato dall'ombra, mi aveva fatto il segnale convenuto.

    Il piano definitivo dell'insurrezione perveniva così nelle mani dello stesso maggiore Brizzi incaricato di curarne l'esecuzione. Esso era dovuto nelle sue linee principali, a Pietro Fortunato Calvi. Sua base fondamentale il principio che le armi dovevano essere tolte di sorpresa al nemico, disorganizzato dall'uccisione dei suoi ufficiali.

    Per concessione regolamentare, questi dormivano, generalmente, non in quartiere ma in alloggi privati. Si era preso nota dei loro indirizzi e conosciuti, naturalmente, erano anche i ritrovi dove, di solito, si radunavano.

    La compagine militare austriaca era, tra l'altro, insediata, entro le mura di Milano, al Palazzo del Comando Generale, a quello della Gran Guardia ed al Castello.

    Il primo veniva guardato, più che difeso, da un picchetto di venticinque soldati. Le loro armi stavano allineate sulla fuciliera, sotto l'andito del portone, d'innanzi al quale passeggiava incessantemente una vigile sentinella. Gli altri soldati ingannavano l'ore stirando le membra nel cortile, fumando nelle camerate. Alle cinque del pomeriggio, il Governatore, i Generali e lo Stato Maggiore pranzavano in quel palazzo.

    Il ridurre, con un colpo di mano, la sentinella all'impotenza, non era sorpresa di grande difficoltà. Come la cordicella tira la fune e la fune trascina la grossa gomena, quel primo tenue successo avrebbe dato in potere degli insorti una trentina di fucili, il picchetto inerme, i capi della guarnigione.

    Qualcosa di simile, ma con mezzi più poderosi, doveva essere tentato alla Gran Guardia, ove avevano stanza cento sgherri giù di lì; ma specialmente al Castello che racchiudeva, nell'armeria, dodici mila fucili. I due armaioli che, giornalmente li ispezionavano, erano dei nostri. Essi si impegnarono a lasciare le porte socchiuse perchè, l'abbatterle, non ci derubasse un tempo prezioso.

    Nei locali della Gran Guardia, gli insorti dovevano trincerarsi e stabilire il proprio quartier generale per dare un centro alla rivoluzione. E, da quel centro, vestite delle uniformi tolte d'addosso ai morti ed ai prigionieri, dovevano divergere piccole pattuglie che, battendo il rullo, avrebbero indotto gli ufficiali ad uscire dalle loro dimore. Sulla soglia troverebbero chi si sarebbe incaricato di suggellare le loro labbra e per sempre.

    Si scelse, a dar nel nemico, il 6 Febbraio, giorno di baldoria carnevalesca e di paga pei soldati. I loro quartieri ne sarebbero rimasti quasi deserti. La sorpresa avrebbe avuto così maggiore probabilità di riuscita; agio maggiore i nostri di precipitarsi nei caffé, nelle osterie; di rinnovare, e per intento più sublime, il lavacro di sangue dei Vespri.

    A spianare la via alla vittoria, Giuseppe Mazzini aveva sollecitato ed ottenuto dal Kossuth, in procinto di partire per gli Stati Uniti d'America, un proclama rivoluzionario diretto ai suoi connazionali acquartierati specialmente nella caserma di San Francesco e già si avevano affidamenti che, al minimo indizio d'insurrezione seria, i figli mal domi della prode Ungheria avrebbero fatto causa comune cogli oppressi fratelli italiani.

    Come coronamento dell'opera, le barricate! L'impresario dell'illuminazione, per proteggere chi le innalzava, si era impegnato a lasciare Milano nel buio. E per difenderle, Giuseppe Mazzini fece penetrare nella città certi proiettili di nuova invenzione, da lanciare sugli assalitori a mo' di bombe.

    Aggiungasi che gli studenti di Pavia, segretamente interpellati, si erano impegnati ad accorrere in aiuto della capitale; segnale convenuto, una fiammata sulla guglia del Duomo. Li avrebbe comandati l'Acerbi... ed altro non chiesero se non l'armi. Il Mazzini, per la via del Po, le aveva provvedute.

    Né a questo si limitava la preparazione dei moti simultanei, oltre le mura. In Piemonte spiava, impaziente d'azione, la turba dei rifugiati politici. Conveniva aprirle una via ampia e facile per calare, organizzata, all'offesa. Giuseppe Mazzini ne affidò l'incarico a Fortunato Calvi ch'i meditò di impossessarsi del Lago Maggiore, con un piano animoso che spiegherò tra breve.
