Si sarebbe accontentato di qualsiasi lavoro; ma eran tempi di crisi e i disoccupati formavano esercito. Su pei giornali le colonne "Help wanted" erano cospicue per la loro assenza; quelle "Situation wanted" si misuravano a iarde. Girava sollecitando qua e là; ma per colmo di disgrazia, aveva l'aspetto signorile. I più umani si accontentavano di rispondere con qualche facezia. Altri, sbirciatolo appena, gli grugniva il laconico "Go on!" che all'orecchio italiano sembra saturo di tanto dispregio. Camminava sinché le gambe lo reggevano; poi si appigliava alla risorsa dei senza risorse... sedere su una panchetta, in qualche parco e aspettare la tarda notte... penosissimo riposo!

    Il sollievo gli giunse inaspettato, una notte per vie misteriose, quando già da quarant'ott'ore non masticava boccone. Aveva rincasato colle solite precauzioni. Non accese la lampada e, prima di svestirsi, per impulso d'abitudine, rimboccò le coltri. Qualcosa di pesante rotolò sul letto. Poco dopo scioglieva un pacco legato con gran cura e una deliziosa fragranza gli solleticava le nari. Aveva d'innanzi, a sua piena disposizione, una meraviglia di vivande rosolate. E addentò con forza canina, senza riflettere, senza pensare. Solo quando ebbe forbita la bocca egli, meravigliandosi d'aver aspettato tanto, si domandò "D'onde mi è piovuta tutta questa abbondanza? Chi mai si è preso cura di me?"

    Il suo primo sospetto cadde sulla signora Mancherini e sue due gentili creature che ella, a guisa della Provvidenza, proteggeva sotto le sue grandi ali. Ma pensò, non senza logica, che la Mancherini avrebbe apparecchiato regolarmente e che un pacco legato con cura doveva venire da lontano. Nessuno dei vecchi amici sapeva delle sue critiche circostanze. Dunque che mistero era quello?

    Per dirla in breve, si era già disteso sotto le coltri e il mondo esteriore già dileguava e la stessa coscienza del proprio essere si era abbuiata; ma una esigua parte del suo cervello lavorava automaticamente ancora... Il silenzio, il vuoto sconfinato e tenebroso. Ad ora, ad ora vi lampeggiavano, insolute, le due domande: "D'onde quest'abbondanza? Chi si prende cura di me?"

    E poiché la vita e l'universo non esistono, all'atto pratico, se non per quel tanto che si riflettono nella nostra mente, noi possiamo arguire a quali minimi termini essi possano ridursi nel cranio magari anche di un eroe!

    Source pp. 121–127Scan to view on another deviceEdgren 1901debito — DUE (proper); DEBT; DUTYClaude rendered 'ha il suo debito posto' as 'has its proper place'; Gemini as 'has its due place'. Edgren's primary gloss 'DUE' plus the secondary sense of DUTY/obligation prompted the more dynamic 'claims its rightful place', foregrounding the sense that this page is owed its position — not merely occupying itAS an introduction to this second part, a page essentially of love claims its rightful place. What I here relate I had, in part, from the Count di Rudio himself, and in part from his wife. Nor was I without the aid of certain intimate letters, yellowed by time, yet still exhaling, fresh as ever, the fragrance of the past.

    intellectsEdgren: ingegno = 'natural talent (genius)'; 'intellects' is less precise than the dictionary's primary definitionEdgren 1901sentire — feel (perceive, be sensible of); smell; taste; judge (esteem); haveItalian 'sente l'anima di Giuseppe Mazzini' — Claude chose 'breathes', Gemini 'bears'. Edgren's range (feel, perceive, sense, have) showed that 'sente' here means something closer to 'holds within itself' or 'conveys'. 'Carries' was chosen as more neutral and accurate than 'breathes' (too physiological) or 'bears' (too heavy)Edgren 1901capolino — (far capolino) peep in, peep through — diminutive of capo (head)Both drafts rendered 'fa capolino' as simply 'peeps in/through'. The Edgren context (diminutive of 'capo' = head) reinforced that 'far capolino' specifically evokes poking one's head through an opening, prompting the addition of 'through the doorway' to recover the spatial concreteness lost in a bare 'peeps in'The little politics that peeps in through the doorway carries the soul of Giuseppe Mazzini. For what, after all, is di Rudio, if not a reflection of that great conspirator? Setting aside the disproportion of their genius, the only substantial difference between the two seems to me this: the one held that without faith in God neither a spirit of sacrifice nor an enduring honesty is possible; the other managed to sacrifice himself and to live honestly, having set aside that mighty hypothesis altogether.

    In London, di Rudio made his way to the innkeeper already known to us, at whose establishment Fumagalli had preceded him. Tressoldi introduced him to the chorus-master of Drury Lane, a Milanese who engaged him as a chorister in the Italian Opera company. Thus, as in other days from flowers, di Rudio drew his sustenance from music — that is, music sung by others; for, on the whole, he was obliged to content himself with opening his mouth wide and waving his arms. The important thing was to pocket five shillings each evening, and in this he succeeded marvelously well, as any seasoned chorister could have done.

    Gemini ProIt should be added that he was, in effect, paid to listen toGemini's 'Let it be added' renders Italian 'Aggiungasi' (a formal impersonal imperative) more faithfully than Claude's 'It should be added'; dropping 'in effect' tightens the sentenceLet it be added that he was paid to listen to Madame Glassier and to the tenor Armandi; while, to be transported into ecstasy by the high notes of this Bolognese cobbler, the wealthy paid relatively fabulous sums.

    Gemini Prothe newspaper noticesItalian 'annunci' means advertisements/announcements; Gemini's 'advertisements' is the standard modern English equivalent and more precise than Claude's 'notices'When the season ended, the Opera Company drifted back, one by one, beneath the fair skies of Italy; but di Rudio remained beneath the leaden skies of London. He persisted in reading the newspaper advertisements and at last found work at the Wapping Docks, a locality situated at the eastern extremity of the metropolis. The work consisted of handling rushes and fashioning coverings for bottles, making use, for this purpose, of a newly invented machine, the contrivance of a Frenchman.

    The excessive distance of the workshop obliged him to forgo the cordial hospitality of the good Tressoldi. The necessity of bread had, in any case, already thinned out that pleasant company. To follow the manifold threads of their lives, to trace worthily the stubborn, exhausting struggle sustained by our exiles in order to keep afloat, would require a volume unto itself.

    Gemini Prowaists compressed by the beltItalian 'ventri compressi dalla cintola' — 'ventri' means bellies, not waists; Gemini correctly identified the body part. 'Pinched' was substituted for 'compressed' to sharpen the imageWe should encounter feet peering out between sole and upper, bearing witness to the long and fruitless hunt for employment; bellies pinched by the belt, betraying the enormous disproportion between appetite and the means of satisfying it. I should have to tell of nocturnal hunts across the rooftops and the mewing of innocent cats destined for the pot; to describe the cramped lairs shared between half a dozen human creatures, each instinctively reclaiming, in sleep, their portion of the blanket — a blanket sometimes pieced together with the art of a mosaicist, compounded of newspapers gathered from the benches in the public gardens.

    The drama would not fail to chill us with its cold breath... virile constitutions corroded by hunger; sensitive natures ruined beyond all hope of recovery; poems vanished silently amidst the clamor of a thieving, pleasure-seeking world; the death-rattles of the consumptive; corpses cut to pieces upon the cold marble of the mortuary slab... acts of heroism without witnesses, unrecorded, yet no less worthy of history than those which unfolded in the light of great occasions.

    intellectsEdgren: ingegno = 'natural talent (genius)'; 'intellects' is less precise than the dictionary's primary definitionGemini Prothe generating flame of divine conceptionsItalian 'fiamma genitrice' — 'genitrice' is adjectival (generative/generatrix); Gemini's 'generative' is the correct English adjective form, where Claude's 'generating' reads as a participleAgainst this backdrop, rendered now in strokes of lamentation, now of comedy, now of the sublime, stands out the pensive figure of Giuseppe Mazzini who, to feed the generative flame of divine conceptions, of genius and of virtue, goes knocking at the moneylender's door and pledges... his own boots.

    Nor should we want for hearty laughter hurled, like defiance, in the face of misery... frequent dreams of glory; sentimental love affairs piercing to the heart through threadbare elbows; long fasts forgotten in the reading of Byron... and likewise those occupations dripping with grease that know nothing of poverty. Angelo Fumagalli, to cite one example, made an excellent living manufacturing sausages.

    The Count di Rudio had found lodging at Baldwin's Garden, in the house of one Mancherini, a figurine-maker from Lucca. Fumagalli and a certain Leoni were the only exiles with whom he maintained, in those days, relations of close friendship.

    He had come to know Leoni at Tressoldi's inn, and they had grown intimate through a characteristic incident which I am pleased to recall.

    Gemini Profull liberty of flight to the birds of prey chained in their native gaolsGemini's word order ('granted to the birds... full freedom of flight') better mirrors the Italian sentence structure; 'freedom' is more natural in the collocation 'freedom of flight' than 'liberty'Austria and the petty tyrants of Italy, who had been given so many headaches by the exiles, devised a new means of defense. They granted to the birds of prey chained in the prisons of their homelands full freedom of flight, on the condition that they should pass themselves off as political refugees and insinuate themselves among the honest company.

    The calculation was that a change of sky would not change their ways, and that the public, implicating the good in the blame provoked by the wicked, would cease to pity the exiles and to lend moral support to their noble aspirations.

    Gemini Proa swaggering inciter who, backed by his associatesItalian 'aizzatore' = instigator/agitator; Gemini's 'instigator' is more standard than Claude's 'inciter'. 'Confederates' was chosen independently for its period colouringA veritable flock of such gentlemen descended upon Tressoldi's inn. They were led by one Bonacina, a Milanese, a swaggering instigator who, backed by his confederates and emboldened by the innkeeper's soft character, soon turned that congenial establishment into a sinister den of ruffians.

    Tressoldi's comical despair, and the fact that he had caught the scent of the troublemakers' special mission, induced di Rudio to resort to an extreme expedient. He found willing souls in Leoni, a kind of athlete, and in other well-built young men; then, having wrested from Tressoldi the promise of the strictest neutrality, he awaited a favorable occasion.

    Gemini Proturn everything topsy-turvyItalian 'mandar tutto a soqquadro'; 'upside down' is the more natural and less quaint collocation; 'topsy-turvy' risks sounding archOne evening Bonacina and four or five fellows of the same stamp were demanding to drink at no cost and threatening to turn everything upside down if they were not served instanter. Di Rudio stepped forward and ordered them to clear the room... and never to set foot in it again.

    This beardless boy smells of good health!Restores Gemini's idiomatic rendering; 'gli puzza la salute' means to be tired of living / asking for a beating, not a literal 'smells of good health'."This beardless boy must be tired of living!" replied Bonacina, making as if to deal him a backhanded blow. It was the signal for a furious brawl... Fumagalli had his head broken; but the ruffians, overwhelmed and badly mauled, ended by beating a retreat, leaving their leader to make the best of things as he could. Alone, with one eye closed and the other barely open, his mouth bloody, Bonacina backed away to the window and, swearing he would have his revenge, leaped out and vanished into the darkness of the street.

    Not a single grim face, that evening, at the sign of Tressoldi's! But the affair had, for di Rudio, an unexpected sequel. One foggy night, returning from the Opera, with his head full of trills and high notes, turning a corner into a deserted lane, he found himself face to face with Bonacina, heavily muffled, fist raised and in that fist a large knife.

    "Ah, ah!" said the scoundrel mockingly. "Now we shall see whether your liver is sound!" But instead of delivering the blow, he drew back... he had caught the gleam of a pistol barrel.

    Then di Rudio was seized by a singular impulse: to add, to the silent eloquence of the weapon, that of a brief homily. He recalled to Bonacina the heroism and greatness of his fellow Milanese, in such striking contrast to his own conduct. And probing that heart, to rekindle beneath the ashes the gentle sentiments of childhood, he asked him, among other things, whether he did not believe that his mother was ashamed to have brought into the world, nursed and raised a son who was surely betraying her finest hopes; who mocked the sufferings of his fellow citizens; who spat upon her gray hairs; who renounced the Fatherland.

    Bonacina, hard and contemptuous at first, ended by absorbing, head bowed, his dose of medicine. Then, flinging away the knife with a sudden movement, he fled headlong, sobbing like a child. The love of the Fatherland had known how to kindle some glimmer even in that base and debased soul!

    Gemini Proconcentrated sunbeamsItalian 'raggi concentrati' literally = concentrated rays; 'sunshine' (closer to Gemini's 'sunbeams' family) captures the warmth metaphor better as a mass noun paralleling 'the sun of Italy' in the next clauseThe Count di Rudio, from his lodging at Baldwin's Garden, went every evening to exchange a few words with Fumagalli and Leoni. After their customary long walk, they pooled what few coins they had and allowed themselves a bottle... of concentrated sunshine. Such was the name they gave to the product of the Lombard vineyards; the only available means by which they could warm themselves at the sun of Italy.

    The first to appear rarely at the accustomed gathering was di Rudio.

    Apropos of these absences, Leoni asked him one evening, with an expression that revealed the unspoken meaning, whether he was lodging in a house of young ladies. "Without a doubt!" replied the Count. "At the Mancherinis' there are two. One is called Sara and is the daughter of the household; the other goes by the name of Elisa and is their niece. Her parents live in some corner of the County of Surrey and have sent her to London, if I was correctly informed, to train in the art of artificial flowers."

    Leoni pressed to know more, and the Count di Rudio, who had picked up the musical jargon at Drury Lane, replied:

    "Miss Sara has a soul tuned in the minor key, soft and caressing; Miss Elisa, on the other hand, has vibrations in the major, impetuous, conquering."

    And Leoni, looking fixedly at Fumagalli and winking at him: "Let us recite the de profundis. The Moretto is ill and... we shall soon lose him!"

    The following evening and the next and many more besides, di Rudio was nowhere to be seen. His two friends grew anxious and resolved to go to Baldwin's Garden to investigate. They found the door ajar. To ring the bell, or to step boldly forward without asking leave? While they stood in two minds, they heard a sweet, gentle voice saying: "In my garden there grows a pear..."

    A masculine voice, that of di Rudio, interrupted: "I have already told you that the names of trees are masculine, and the names of fruits feminine. Excepting dattero, limone, ananassa..."

    — Except dattero, limone, ananassa and fico! — observed another woman's voice, more vibrant than the first.

    Leoni drew Fumagalli away. Then he said: "I foresaw it. The fellow is done for!"

    Signora Mancherini, a Booth by birth, a distant relation of the founder of the Salvation Army, was English through and through; but her husband's occupation had dusted her, not to say plastered her, with art more or less classical. She spoke with evident ostentation of Venuses and Dianas. And with the Gods of Olympus she had overburdened every piece of furniture, every shelf, every projecting surface of her little apartment. She was also accustomed to dressing her hair in a fashion that recalled times long since vanished.

    Her daughter Sara was a fair flower of eighteen years. In the judgment of the Count di Rudio, she took a little from her father and a little from her mother... a blend of English and Italian. But the northern disposition, active and assertive, had, by an apparent contradiction of nature, ended by remaining as if atrophied, while the gentle and submissive disposition of the Italian woman, developing and all but bursting forth, stamped the whole of her character in a very marked degree. She did not seek to shine; she did not imperiously demand love; she preferred to love without being loved in return to being loved without loving in return. Not yet having reached the age at which feeling, fully developed, knows what the tempests of passion are, she was content with the tepid breath of friendship, and lavished upon her cousin Elisa the tender devotion which she would later reserve for a husband; in her cousin she rejoiced, suffered at her vexations, and exulted in her joys. Happy in her own half-light, she asked for nothing more, provided that Elisa should triumph.

    however rare and restrained as befitted every young English womanRemoves the invented 'as befitted' (a notion of propriety absent from source and both drafts); restores Claude's faithful 'rare and sparing in every English young lady'.This latter, meanwhile, as if to make it so deliberately, was endowed with a temperament that tended to accentuate Sara's disposition. Of the English people it is generally said that they have strong teeth and that once they succeed in seizing their prey they never let it go. Beneath Miss Elisa's fair hair, beneath the rosy skin, behind the blue eyes that even today call forth a sense of sweetness, there nestled a tenacity of purpose and a courage by no means common. These qualities, finding occasion to manifest themselves, appeared as a paradox and astonished the superficial observer; but not one who, accustomed to analysis and deduction, had noted the significance of that broad, firmly drawn chin and the energy of her gesture, however rare and sparing in every English young lady.

    The Count di Rudio told me that, as a girl, she possessed a certain suppleness of character and intellect; but she could never have been brought, even had she wished it, to the point of self-negation. To love strong souls; to open to them, as a corolla to the kisses of the sun, the most hidden gradations of her heart; but in order to be stronger, greater together as two. To conclude a treaty of alliance — that yes, with all her ardor, and to remain faithful to it unto the last; to sign even an honorable surrender, never!

    Di Rudio had resolved to teach Italian to the two young women at the urging of Signora Mancherini. Why refuse? To study our language becomes fatally synonymous with loving our Fatherland, and our friends are always something of enemies to our enemies. The sympathy of the English was greatly coveted by the Italians, and even the arts of peace may serve to win a battle. Besides, every small stone has its value in great undertakings!

    And so it came about that the Count di Rudio, every evening, after having labored prosaically among rushes, applied himself to instilling the sweetest and the strongest of modern tongues into the charming heads of two young women born and raised upon the foggy banks of the Thames; and perhaps the Gods of Olympus, from the heights of their shelves, smiled down upon the scene.

    Accustomed to the blunt directness of military life, the Count di Rudio proved a rather brusque master, and the hour of the lesson assumed, so to speak, the rigidity of a geometrical problem. But no sooner had that hour passed than Signora Mancherini set her steaming tea service upon the table and the atmosphere changed as if by enchantment... books and notebooks vanished, and animated conversation flowed without interruption, punctuated and, as it were, annotated by sips of the warm beverage. Signora Mancherini, who was stout, filled the large armchair entirely with her own person. Sara sat, by a habit consonant with her inclinations, upon a low footstool, resting her forearm upon Miss Elisa's lap. The Count di Rudio, lean, nervous, dark, amidst so much triumph of fair complexions and rosy hues — like a grain of pepper — sat at first calm and composed; then, little by little, he grew all gestures and starts, and his countenance, most mobile, flashed and sparkled.

    Before long, through his doing, the titanic figures who had striven for our Risorgimento were embroidered upon the fibers of those souls, all the more clearly for the fact that the fabric receiving them was sensitive and unversed in powerful emotions. And they were transformed by it: they felt, suffered, hoped and willed in the Italian manner.

    What was bound to happen, happened. The heroes were dead or far away; the Count di Rudio was close at hand, young, attractive. He too had suffered for the sacred cause. In the eyes of the two young women he ended by growing to heroic stature, and they blended about that head his own prestige and that of others.

    Angelo Fumagalli had a singular expression to characterize that situation: "Venice," he would say, "had the Moor; Belluno, being smaller, has only the Moretto. But the Moor was loved by a single Desdemona; that rascal di Rudio has two. Perhaps... and why not?... with not a shadow of malice, mind you!... perhaps he has three!"

    The only one who seemed unaware of this was the Count di Rudio himself. Love knows no tropics; hearts burn at the Pole as furiously as at the Equator. It is the manner of expressing affection that varies with latitude. The Italian impetuosity is unknown to the young English woman, who perhaps senses instinctively how discordant it would sound in that world of subdued tones. Free and mistress of herself, she adorns herself, when she loves, with a composed dignity... the distinguishing mark of the free. Hence her unjust reputation for coldness. That charm glided over the spirit of the Count, absorbed and all but saturated with a wholly different passion.

    True it is that in the long hours spent at the workshop, while his practiced hands carried out the habitual labor mechanically, the mind of di Rudio strayed toward the two young women. Often the daydream absorbed him so completely that the noise of the tools and the faint odor of the rushes were, as it were, suppressed. In those moments — so he affirmed to me — a mysterious fragrance of youth and health penetrated his lungs and gave him a subtle intoxication. Sometimes he had caught himself yearning for the evening... But was it not in the evening that, having scraped away the scales of vulgarity, he conversed with his great ones, upon great things? The explanation seemed more than sufficient to him, and he did not trouble to enquire any further.

    Unexpectedly, his employer, a Frenchman, withdrew from the business. A new proprietor, new methods: the foreign labor was dismissed, and the Count di Rudio found himself once more without immediate resources, very nearly destitute. For a time he went forward on the small savings he had put aside; then he found himself reduced to utter penury. The day he turned out his pockets and searched his every pocket within, he reflected, not without dismay, that he already owed a month's rent to Signora Mancherini. For several evenings past, at the lesson, he had appeared pale and listless. Miss Elisa asked him whether he was unwell. "Not precisely," he replied, "but I must say something that weighs upon me. I have most pressing obligations. Tomorrow evening I shall be engaged elsewhere; and I do not know when we shall be able to resume our lessons."

    The festival of rosy and azure hues was over... the via crucis of the hero reduced to the level of the pariah began, the one to whom society denies salt and fire. He went out early in the morning, and returning at a very late hour, he managed to slip along the corridor, avoiding unpleasant encounters — he had come to call them such. He was of such a temper that he would have preferred to face the mouth of a cannon to the face of a creditor.

    Unfortunately, one cannot leave hunger on the threshold of one's own house, nor hurl it into the street like an awkward burden. He hurled instead to the moneylender his few superfluous possessions, one by one. He had left behind his old friends, and to approach them now, when he was in extreme need, seemed to him a deliberate courting of rebuff. He would have been glad of any work; but these were times of crisis and the unemployed formed an army. In the newspapers the "Help Wanted" columns were conspicuous by their absence; those of "Situation Wanted" were measured by the yard. He went about soliciting here and there; but to compound his misfortune, he had the appearance of a gentleman. The more humane contented themselves with answering with some pleasantry. Others, having barely glanced at him, grunted the laconic "Go on!" which to Italian ears seems saturated with so much contempt. He walked as long as his legs would support him; then he resorted to the recourse of those without recourse... sitting upon a bench in some park and waiting for the late night... a painful repose.

    Relief came to him unexpectedly, one night by mysterious ways, when he had not tasted a morsel in forty-eight hours. He had returned home with his customary precautions. He did not light the lamp, and before undressing, from the force of habit, turned down the bedclothes. Something heavy rolled upon the bed. Shortly afterwards he was unwrapping a parcel tied with great care, and a delicious fragrance tickled his nostrils. Before him, entirely at his disposal, was a marvel of roasted viands. And he bit into them with canine voracity, without reflection, without thought. Only when he had wiped his mouth did he, wondering that he had waited so long, ask himself: "Whence has all this abundance rained down upon me? Who can have taken care of me?"

    His first suspicion fell upon Signora Mancherini and her two gentle creatures whom she, in the manner of Providence, sheltered beneath her ample wings. But he reflected, not without logic, that Signora Mancherini would have laid out a proper table, and that a parcel tied with care must have come from a distance. None of his old friends knew of his critical circumstances.

    What mystery, then, was this?

    To put it briefly, he had already stretched himself beneath the bedclothes and the outer world was already receding, and even the consciousness of his own being had grown dim; but a small part of his brain still worked on mechanically... Silence; the limitless and tenebrous void. Now and then, within it, the two questions flashed unresolved: "Whence this abundance? Who is taking care of me?"

    And since life and the universe, in practical terms, exist only insofar as they are reflected in our minds, we may gauge to what minimal terms they may be reduced even within the skull of a hero!

    Parte Seconda

    Capitolo Secondo

    Part Two

    Chapter Two

    LA GRATA sorpresa notturna continuò, con matematica regolarità... un pacchetto ben legato e ben fornito, ogni ventiquattro ore. Il conte di Rudio decise d'andare al fondo della cosa, e, un bel mattino, rassettata come di consueto la camera, si rannicchiò nel vano della finestra, dietro il cortinaggio, risoluto a rimanere appostato e silenzioso magari il giorno intero. Di lassù dominava, per buon tratto, il marciapiede a destra ed a sinistra ed egli pensava, a ragione, che le polpette non gli potevano giungere per l'aria a volo. Avrebbe scoperto il suo misterioso benefattore.

    Un pò prima del mezzogiorno, mentre appoggiato al davanzale fotografava coll'occhio il bruno via vai che si agitava confusamente ai suoi piedi, un subito destarsi delle vibrazioni vitali, un dolce tepore, un senso di luce penetrante, lo scossero. Egli si volse di scatto... non era più solo!

    Avvolta in un bianco accappatoio, coi capelli in parte annodati, in parte fluenti, stava ritta al di qua della soglia, la signorina Elisa. Le dita della destra tenevano, per la cordicella, il pacco rigeneratore e gli occhi azzurri erravano in un indicibile turbamento.

    "Signor Carlo!... mormorò. Ignoravo che fosse qui!" Subitamente si rinfrancò e soggiunse con calma: "Hanno portato questo pacco per lei!"

    — Quando? Per la porta non è entrata anima viva.

    — Stamane per tempo. Se avessi saputo che era a casa, glie l'avrei subito consegnato.

    — Stamane, ha detto?

    — Per tempo. Un signore ben vestito, italiano all'aspetto, all'accento. Gli ho chiesto il nome; ma mi ha risposto che, per ragioni tutte sue particolari, non desiderava di darsi a conoscere. Non mi parve lecito l'insistere. D'altronde egli parlava tanto male l'inglese ed io so così poco d'italiano!...

    Non aggiunse altro e rimase ad occhi bassi, in attesa di qualche frase, di una parola allusiva, non foss'altro, al bel tempo. E come la parola assolutamente non venne, fece nervosamente arco delle sopracciglia e rialzò gli occhi. La blanda interrogazione che vi brillava, si smarrì presto in un'espressione di penosa meraviglia. Il conte di Rudio pareva assorto nel pensiero di cose lontane... la sua mente correva presso l'italiano ben vestito che, senz'essere uno stregone, conosceva tanto bene i segreti del suo stomaco. E si irritava del mistero che, malgrado il tedioso appostamento, non era riuscito a risolvere. Quando s'accorse d'essere osservato... silenziosamente, dignitosamente, si spolverò, colla destra, la giacchetta imbiancata dal lungo contatto col davanzale.

    La situazione diventava imbarazzante quando, nel corridoio, risonò un calpestio di passi precipitosi ed ecco sopraggiungere la signorina Sara. Ella sorreggeva, serrandolo al seno, un gran vaso di rose.

    Lo depose presso la finestra e dopo qualche secondo di muta contemplazione: "Ci sta a meraviglia!" Si rivolse all'Elisa e soggiunse: "L'idea è tua e a te spetta il dare una spiegazione!" Ma non glie ne lasciò il tempo e fissando il conte: "Una sera ha detto che la rosa è l'emblema della sua famiglia e che, anche per questo, è da Lei preferita sopra ogni altro fiore. Elisa ebbe un'ispirazione. «Procuriamone un cespo, forse l'avrà caro.» Veda che rigoglio! Per non darle fastidi continueremo ad innaffiarlo noi. Elisa mi faceva osservare che le rammenterà la mamma e la Patria... perchè l'Italia è il paese delle rose. Non è così?"

    Qui le parve, o finse, che qualcuno la chiamasse e corse via. La signorina Elisa salutò con un cenno del capo e svelta e leggera, come un'ombra, scomparve.

    Il conte di Rudio se ne stette a lungo immobile, fissando l'occhio nel vuoto, come se qualcosa delle due giovinette vi fosse rimasto. Da quel giorno esse occuparono maggior parte delle sue ore fantasiose. Si insinuarono, non chiamate, ne' suoi pensieri e non appena vi apparivano erano accolte con amorevolezza più che fraterna. Gli occhi tristi, che non avevano mai visto altro se non che la tortura dell'innocente ed il trionfo della canaglia, si rinfrancarono in quella interna contemplazione. E per la prima volta, il Conte sentì profondamente che, al disopra del mondo astioso, degli uomini che si rodono scambievolmente, della insaziabile cupidigia, della spietata prepotenza, della melma varia e nauseabonda in cui si affonda e si imbraca il nostro vivere civile, sta sospesa nel cielo azzurro, che ne è saturo, una forza serena che trasforma, che redime, che crea, che ci può far buoni e felici... l'Amore!

    Source pp. 128–129Scan to view on another deviceGemini Prohe tucked himself into the window recess behind the curtain'Crouched' more precisely renders 'si rannicchiò' (to curl up, huddle) and better conveys the physical posture of someone hiding in a window recess to spy on the street below.THE AGREEABLE nocturnal surprise continued with mathematical regularity... a well-tied and well-stocked parcel, every twenty-four hours. The Count di Rudio resolved to get to the bottom of the matter, and one fine morning, having set his room in order as usual, he crouched in the window recess behind the curtain, determined to remain posted there in silence were it to take the whole of the day. From that vantage above he commanded a good stretch of the pavement to right and left, and he reasoned, not without justice, that the morsels could hardly reach him through the air on the wing. He would discover his mysterious benefactor.

    Gemini Prothe dark to-and-fro that stirred in confused motion at his feet'Confusedly' is more concise and idiomatic than the periphrastic 'in confused motion', and directly mirrors the Italian adverb 'confusamente'.Gemini ProHe turned with a jerk'With a start' better captures the involuntary, surprise-driven nature of 'di scatto' in this context; 'with a jerk' sounds too mechanical and physical.A little before midday, while leaning against the sill and photographing with his eye the dark to-and-fro that stirred confusedly at his feet, a sudden awakening of vital vibrations, a gentle warmth, a sense of penetrating light, startled him. He turned with a start... he was no longer alone!

    Edgren 1901accappatoio — accappatoio [cappa], M.: mantle; combing-cloth.The primary draft retained the Italian word in italics with a gloss ('a white *accappatoio* — a combing-cloth'); the Gemini draft used the modern default 'dressing-gown'. The final translation dropped the Italian word and used 'combing-cloth' directly, following Edgren's period-appropriate definition rather than the anachronistic modern equivalent. This keeps the register consistent with the 1901 setting.Wrapped in a white combing-cloth, her hair in part knotted, in part flowing, Miss Elisa stood upright on the near side of the threshold. The fingers of her right hand held by its cord the restorative packet, and her blue eyes wandered in an unspeakable perturbation.

    "Signor Carlo!..." she murmured. "I did not know you were here!" She gathered herself at once and added with composure: "Someone brought this parcel for you!"

    — When? Not a living soul has entered by the door.

    — Early this morning. Had I known you were at home, I should have brought it to you at once.

    — This morning, you say?

    — Early. A well-dressed gentleman, Italian in appearance and in accent. I asked his name; but he answered that, for reasons entirely his own, he did not wish to make himself known. It did not seem proper to insist. Besides, he spoke English so very ill, and I know so little Italian!...

    She added nothing more and remained with eyes downcast, waiting for some phrase, for a word — were it only an allusion — to the fine weather. And as the word absolutely did not come, she nervously arched her brows and raised her eyes again. The mild enquiry that glimmered in them was soon lost in an expression of pained wonder. The Count di Rudio appeared absorbed in thought of distant things... his mind was running upon the well-dressed Italian who, without being a sorcerer, knew the secrets of his stomach so passing well. And he chafed at the mystery which, despite his tedious vigil, he had not succeeded in resolving. When he became aware that he was being observed... silently, with dignity, he brushed from his jacket with his right hand the whitish dust acquired by its long contact with the sill.

    The situation was growing awkward when, in the corridor, there sounded the tread of hurrying steps, and there appeared Miss Sara. She was carrying, clasped to her bosom, a great vase of roses.

    She set it down by the window and after a few seconds of mute contemplation: "It looks wonderfully well there!" She turned to Elisa and added: "The idea is yours and it falls to you to give the explanation!" But she allowed her no time for it, and fixing her eyes upon the Count: "One evening you said that the rose is the emblem of your family and that, for that reason too, it is your favorite flower above all others. Elisa had an inspiration. ‘Let us procure a bush; perhaps he will hold it dear.’ See what luxuriance! So as not to trouble you, we shall continue to water it ourselves. Elisa observed to me that it will put you in mind of your mother and your Fatherland... for Italy is the land of roses. Is it not so?"

    Here she thought — or feigned — that someone was calling her, and ran off. Miss Elisa took her leave with a nod of the head, and swift and light as a shadow, vanished.

    The Count di Rudio remained a long while motionless, fixing his gaze upon the void, as though something of the two young women had lingered there. From that day they occupied the greater part of his dreaming hours. They insinuated themselves, unbidden, into his thoughts, and no sooner did they appear there than they were received with a tenderness more than brotherly. His sad eyes, which had never beheld aught save the torment of the innocent and the triumph of the base, found renewal in that inward contemplation. And for the first time, the Count felt profoundly that, above the world of spite, of men who gnaw one another, of insatiable cupidity, of pitiless arrogance, of the manifold and nauseating mire in which our civil life sinks and wallows, there floats suspended in the blue sky — of which it is the very substance — a serene force that transforms, that redeems, that creates, that can make us good and happy... Love!

    Parte Seconda

    Capitolo Terzo

    Part Two

    Chapter Three

    AMORE!... per la signorina Sara o per la signorina Elisa? Il problema, per quanto semplice, gli suscitò nell'animo uno sgomento singolare, a cui succedette una non meno singolare irritazione. "Perchè affannarsi a risolverlo — ne concluse — dal momento che l'accasarmi mi è conteso da altri ideali?" Il conte di Rudio agiva inconsciamente, ed in un campo diverso, come di fronte alle idee nuove agisce l'uomo nel cui cervello si abbarbica il pregiudizio. Il quale intuisce la ragionevolezza di quelle idee, ma tant'è! chiude ostinatamente gli occhi al vero e la certezza stessa che il suo idolo si infrange, lo fa campione più strenuo delle vecchie fole e della menzogna.

    Ed ora, ecco come finì la lunga siccità di scarsella del conte di Rudio.

    Dal giorno in cui il colpo di Stato aveva trionfato in modo definitivo, Vittor Hugo, presa la via dell'esilio, si era stabilito nell'isola di Jersey. Egli vi pubblicava "L'Homme" un periodico dalle cui pagine sfolgorava, coll'intensità prestatele dal genio, la condanna della nefasta politica imperiale. L'eroe del 2 Dicembre, sensibilissimo alle punture della critica, come ogni delinquente che aspiri alla gloria, se ne lagnava destramente presso il Gabinetto di Londra che, con pari destrezza, se ne schermiva. Ma quando le complicazioni della politica europea indicarono, all'Inghilterra, in Napoleone III il sovrano adatto a prestarle mano forte nella meditata impresa di fiaccare l'orgoglio dello Czar, l'assecondare i capricci di quell'uomo si impose come atto di saggia diplomazia. E la libertà del pensiero e la tradizionale larga ospitalità del popolo inglese vennero, almeno in parte, ristrette e sacrificate agli interessi veri, o supposti, della Nazione.

    Vittor Hugo era attorniato da vari brillanti ingegni, ai quali si unì il Pianciani. Quel nido di intellettuali venne improvvisamente scompigliato. E parve tale sacrilegio che persino i reazionari francesi non ardirono applaudire.

    Il Pianciani decise di riparare una volta ancora a Londra. Prima di lasciare l'isola, egli spedì, colle altre carte, assicurandolo per diecimila sterline, il manoscritto della sua "Storia dei Papi". Le carte pervennero a destinazione, il manoscritto scomparve. E corse voce che i Gesuiti se ne fossero impossessati, probabilmente per gettarlo alle fiamme. Aggiunse colore di verità ad una tal voce, il fatto che la Compagnia d'Assicurazione sborsò immediatamente il vistoso indennizzo, senza iniziare la minima inchiesta, senza profferir verbo.

    Il Pianciani e il conte di Rudio si abboccarono e il gentiluomo romano che, colla sua cordialità espansiva, coi suoi impulsi generosi, si faceva perdonare la deformità del viso (il vaiolo gli aveva spuntato il naso) accolse il gentiluomo bellunese da paro a paro. Nel congedarsi, e quasi di sghembo, la conversazione cadde sulle strettezze finanziarie di quest'ultimo.

    "Non potevi giungermi più a proposito! — disse il Pianciani. — Mi sono accinto a ritentare la "Storia dei Papi". Vuoi essere il mio amanuense?"

    L'uomo avvezzo agli ardimenti, alle fatiche, ai pericoli dell'azione, venne confinato su una poltrona e fece sua virtù di quanto aveva sempre riputato difetto esiziale, la pazienza. In compenso veniva a contatto cogli uomini luminosi per genio, per carattere, per sofferenze virilmente sopportate che frequentavano la casa del Pianciani: ribelli perseguitati, calunniati, senza tetto, senza pane, non vinti... grandi nei loro amori, nei loro odi, nelle loro utopie. Nobile e fiera compagnia!

    I più avevano un'idea fissa che quasi li improntava. Essi tentano di convincere, di attrarre nella loro orbita il giovine amanuense.

    Il Pianciani intravvede nei torbidi addensati in Oriente la probabile salvezza d'Italia. Liberolle, lo storico, sogna l'unione delle genti latine, ma col predominio della Francia. Vittor Hugo non sa darsi pace per la dabbenaggine della sua Patria abbarbagliata dall'equivoco splendore della Corte di Napoleone il Piccolo. Egli ha perduto ogni fede nella virtù di un Popolo insensibile ai grandi ideali, pronto a fucilare e a farsi fucilare pel fascino bugiardo delle frasi fatue. Ed ogni sua irruenza chiude invariabilmente coll'amara sentenza: "Il popolo è un gran fanciullo che vuol essere ingannato!"

    E tra quelle figure moderne passano, ora miti e sorridenti, ora corruschi di luce sinistra, i fantasmi della Storia. Sono diciannove secoli di glorie e di sozzure che, sfilando sulle brevi carte, rinnovano nel cuore del conte, i fremiti, i dolori, l'ira delle generazioni che dormono polvere.

    Tuttavia nella mente, fatta come turgida da quel vasto intreccio, sorgono, ad ora ad ora, due figure semplici e sorridenti che lo agitano più profondamente del genio; che disvogliano d'ogni fregio imperatori e pontefici, che li ricacciano nella loro volgarità. Esse gli appariscono costantemente adorne di rose, il bel fiore emblema della sua famiglia ed un senso di sereno lo pervade e resta: un senso che attenua il lezzo delle orgie vaticane; che toglie orrore alle maledizioni che, da ogni lembo d'Italia, da ogni lembo d'Europa, si levarono mai sempre contro i ladroni che trafficarono la Patria, l'umanità, il Cristo.

    Il Conte di Rudio ora non le discute più; esse sono diventate come parte di lui stesso. Non già che le sue idee siano, a questo proposito, nette e ben determinate. L'amore, non dimentico delle usate arti, dissimula tuttora la propria vigoria al riparo di altri affetti più anticamente radicati ed in pieno sviluppo. Ma egli è persuaso, ormai, d'aver trovato sul suo sentiero il bello, il buono, il casto stupendamente incarnati. Vederle, assorbirne le irradiazioni, elevarsi... sentirsi meno infelice! Egli si abbandonò alla nuovissima sensazione senz'alcun sospetto. Quando la bufera rivoluzionaria lo riprenderà, la festa del sentimento verrà bruscamente troncata; ma egli porterà nei cimenti maggior tesoro di care rimembranze: i nobili affetti prestano l'ali a raggiungere le ardue vette!

    Ed anela, con ansietà sempre più intensa, il giorno in cui, ottenuto il compenso delle sue fatiche, potrà presentarsi a fronte alta alla signora Mancherini, pagare da galantuomo i propri debiti, riprendere il corso delle interrotte lezioni; abbeverarsi nuovamente a quel bello, a quel buono, a quel casto, cattivando sempre più le sue giovani amiche al culto dei martiri e degli eroi!

    Il giorno venne. Con che passo leggero percorse la lunga via, con quale slancio salì le scale! Mamma Mancherini fattasi innanzi gli intercettò la soglia colla sua ampia e rigogliosa persona. Ond'egli, allungando immediatamente la destra... e le sterline, si profuse a chieder scusa del ritardo involontario. Ma la signora lo guardò con aria sospettosa. Accorsero le due giovinette e il conte di Rudio seppe, con sua grande meraviglia, che l'affitto era stato pagato ad ogni fin di mese. La signorina Elisa tirò ancora in ballo lo straniero misterioso e Sara affermò di non aver mai consegnato al signor conte le debite ricevute, perchè il signor conte da lungo tempo non si lasciava più vedere. Ma la pingue Mancherini non parve troppo soddisfatta e se ne andò accigliata, borbottando: "Oh che pasticcio, che pasticcio!"

    In quei giorni la guerra di Crimea tirava innanzi zoppicando. Sin dal primo manifestarsi dei torbidi che condussero a quella guerra, Giuseppe Mazzini aveva proposto di fare appello al sentimento unitario della Germania; di assecondare le aspirazioni nazionali dell'Italia e dell'Ungheria; di far scorrere un soffio di vita nuova tra i Balcani; di formare, per dirla in breve, attorno all'Impero moscovita, un grande baluardo di Stati liberi che avrebbe compressa e paralizzata la Russia.

    Questo concetto non dispiaceva al Gabinetto di Torino. Ma Napoleone III e l'Inghilterra miravano a non provocare le ostilità dell'Austria; a indurla, anzi, a partecipare attivamente alla loro alleanza. E fu appunto perchè l'Austria non si sarebbe mai decisa ad entrare in campagna finché le durava sul collo il terribile vespaio dell'Italia, che si accettò di buon grado, che si sollecitò, anzi, la cooperazione di Casa Savoia. L'esercito piemontese era il solo nucleo fortemente organizzato; il solo ritenuto capace di precipitare gli avvenimenti nella nostra Penisola.

    L'Austria non si lasciò smuovere ed evitò così agli Italiani l'umiliazione di vedere gli eroi della nostra indipendenza, combattere d'accanto alle orde croate, per mantenere sul Continente europeo "una triste proiezione dell'Asia".

    E la meditata grandiosa sollevazione di popoli si ridusse, all'atto pratico, ad un semplice, lungo, tedioso assedio. L'Inghilterra, scossa nel suo prestigio militare, a corto di soldati, si decise ad aprire gli arruolamenti, per organizzare qualche legione straniera.

    Non appena l'apposito decreto venne promulgato, il di Rudio si vide capitare dinanzi il Fumagalli ed il Leoni. Questi aveva deciso di partire per la Crimea e veniva per una visita di congedo. "E se sarà per l'ultima volta — disse, stringendo la destra al conte — orbene, per l'ultima volta, addio!"

    Si recarono a passare le poche ore che mancavano alla partenza, nella "Public House" di una certa Shaw, moglie di un nostro connazionale. Era appunto il luogo di convegno delle reclute. L'ambiente di un'eleganza da parata, era già eccezionalmente animato... un'animazione confusa, piena di scatti e di risate. Militari e borghesi, a crocchi, si battevano sulla spalla; si scambiavano nomignoli ed aggettivi grassi e cordiali. Nei cantucci appartati, qualche figura gentile, in cappellino, colla faccia pallida e lacrimosa. Fiori e ramoscelli verdi su ogni petto; spire azzurrognole di sigarette, quasi da ogni bocca.

    Ma il chiasso e la confusione non si addicevano all'animo del di Rudio; tanto più che la vista di tanti Italiani, in procinto di versare il proprio sangue per una causa che egli riteneva estranea alla Patria, gli rammentava i tristi tempi dei soldati di ventura. E quando il Leoni, tra il serio ed il faceto, gli disse: "Orsù, Moretto! Deciditi ed imbarcati con noi!" egli, con ruvidezza soverchia, gli domandò: "Che male ti hanno fatto i Russi, perchè tu debba offrire in olocausto la tua giovinezza, nell'intento di aumentare fra loro, il numero delle vedove e degli orfani?"

    E il Leoni: "In Crimea sventolano i colori italiani!"

    — Qualsiasi bandiera è in irrisione, quando non stia come simbolo di verità e di giustizia!

    La conversazione prendeva cattiva piega. Fortunatamente un coscritto milanese, ripigliando il filo di una discussione precedente, manifestò il proprio rammarico per non aver pensato a portar seco "I Sepolcri" di Ugo Foscolo, che tanto avrebbero contribuito ad accorciargli le ore della traversata.

    — A casa ne ho un esemplare — gli disse il di Rudio. — L'accetti. È un'edizione economica, coi margini pieni di scarabocchi ma non ne guastano il succo.

    In quattro salti fu al Baldwin's Garden. Salì le scale ed entrò nella sua camera, lasciando l'uscio socchiuso. Già buttava sottosopra il cassettone, quando dall'altro lato del corridoio, gli giunsero nettamente gemiti e singhiozzi. Che è questo?

    Fattosi sulla soglia, si trovò di fronte la signorina Sara che, arrossendo fugacemente e senza attendere d'essere interrogata: "Elisa piange perchè è persuasa che Ella parta stanotte per la Crimea. Amore me la strugge. E s'è anche compromessa agli occhi della mamma. Fu quel pasticcio delle ricevute... se ne rammenta? Mamma le ha cavato tutto dal cuore e il meno che ora le tocchi è della grillina. Lo straniero misterioso è là che si dispera. Fu l'Elisa che provvide coi propri risparmi... a tutto, a tutto!"

    Il conte di Rudio strinse, girò e rigirò e capovolse il grande poeta; poi cacciandoselo rabbiosamente in fondo alla tasca: "Dica alla signorina Elisa che i di Rudio non sono soldati di ventura. Per la Libertà... sempre! mercenari di despoti stranieri... mai!"

    E scese le scale a precipizio. Ma la tempesta che le parole della signorina Sara avevano suscitato, lo seguì sulla via; entrò, per lo spiraglio dell'uscio, nella "Public House" della signora Shaw, gli vorticò tutta la sera attorno al capo... tanto che i discorsi e la letteratura e la politica e il chiasso e la scena farraginosa dell'imbarco e gli addii giunsero al suo cervello, non come realtà vivida e ben delineata, ma quasi pallida rievocazione di immagini emergenti confusamente da tra le nebbie di tempi trascorsi.

    Una seconda sorpresa lo attendeva. Rincasando verso il tocco scorse, seduta sul pianerottolo, una figura di donna in veste bianca... nell'oscurità gli sembrava fosforescente. Un istante di stupefazione lo prese... e quella si alzò e, retrocedendo, parve svanisse. L'avrebbe ritenuta per un fantasma, se un uscio non avesse cigolato. La signorina Elisa aveva passata tanta parte della notte vegliando, in lagrime forse, per accertare che il mare e la guerra non glie lo avevano rapito.

    E il di Rudio, nel tumulto che tentava di sopraffarlo, si aggrappò disperatamente a questo concetto: "Prima del pomeriggio, disilluderla e fuggire!" Con quella risoluzione si coricò; in quella i pensieri della notte, ispirati al doloroso ricordo di tanti martiri e vivi e morti, lo intestardirono. Quando si presentò, rigido e sicuro di sé stesso, alle due giovinette, l'Elisa lo accolse dicendo: "Si sente male? È pallido stamane!"

    — Mi sembra che siate pallidi entrambi! osservò la cugina. E si offerse di preparare una tazza di latte caldo... anche la mamma, rientrando, l'avrebbe gradita. Quando fu sulla soglia si volse e sorridendo: "Non commettete sciocchezze. Ogni istante perduto è sottratto alla vostra felicità e, a furia di nascondervi, potreste anche finire col non trovarvi più. Vi amate, siete nati l'uno per l'altro, che ci vuol tanto?" E fuggì via. Il momento decisivo era giunto. L'Elisa attorcigliava nervosamente un suo nastro e il di Rudio l'osservava rimanendo a rispettosa distanza. Incominciò col ringraziarla per tutto quanto aveva fatto per lui. Inutile ogni diniego... Sara gli aveva tutto confidato. Il guaio era che egli non poteva dimostrarle la sua riconoscenza se non a parole.

    E tirò via riassumendo le tristi vicissitudini a cui deve sottostare chi propugna una causa santa e perseguitata. Con quale animo avrebbe osato offrirle la destra, trascinarla nella sua vita tumultuosa, nella sua miseria? Dove trovarle un nido che non venisse, quando che sia, contaminato e distrutto dalla rabbia degli uomini? Sarebbe saggio, umano, onesto il riserbare un ambiente funestato dallo spavento delle congiure, dalle insidie dell'armi, dallo stridore delle catene, dal terrore del sangue ai figli nati a perpetuare le miti virtù materne? L'infanzia anela i baci delle bocche sorridenti; vuole rispecchiare nell'anima candida, occhi sereni. E che altro poteva dirle; quale felicità prometterle, senza ricorrere a frasi menzognere? Aveva scelto, tra due mali, il minore; aveva deciso di allontanarsi... per sempre. Supplicava di interpretare la sua risoluzione con quella correttezza di sentimenti che glie l'aveva suggerita; di conservare buona memoria di lui.

    Qui e qui soltanto, la signorina Elisa alzò la faccia e lo fissò negli occhi. Coll'acutezza delle anime semplici, seppe scandagliare un'anima. Si espresse da creatura cosciente di poter disporre del proprio destino, coll'eloquenza pacata e casta che a taluno de' miei lettori, avvezzi ad esaltare "l'opulenza dell'anca e i fremiti della carne", potrebbe sembrare dissotterrata da qualche museo d'archeologia... Altri tempi, altri uomini, altri amori!

    "Ad un'anima come quella del di Rudio ella poteva muovere incontro senza sospetto e senza artifici: avrebbe preferito il vedersi definitivamente respinta al divenire sua per virtù simulate. Vi sono donne italiane, non lo ignorava, che hanno compresso le loro serene aspirazioni, imposto al cuore la solitudine ed il silenzio per non diventare spose e madri in tempi calamitosi. Essa ammirava la loro austerità, ma non si sentiva d'approvarla. I tristi allevano liberamente i tristi e i buoni dovrebbero sottrarsi al dovere di crescere chi perpetui le oneste tradizioni? L'ambiente, che non si mostrava favorevole a coltivare, in teneri cuori, le miti virtù che egli le supponeva, avrebbe favorito lo sviluppo delle maschie virtù paterne. Il temprare caratteri virili è missione che raffina l'orgoglio di una madre. O forse la credeva, come le sue antiche romane, atta solo a presiedere al buon ordine della cucina ed a filar lana? Lo strazio dei cuori che trasudan sangue, il terribile sforzo dei muscoli che si gonfiano per spezzar catene, il tormentoso lavorìo degli intelletti che cercano ovunque l'equità e la luce, avrebbero dovuto lasciarla indifferente? Aveva una profonda simpatia pel Paese che egli le aveva dipinto così bello, così grande, così sventurato. L'amore che portava alla Patria non la ingelosiva... distoglierlo dalle sue mire, rintuzzare i suoi impeti generosi!? Precisamente il contrario! Avrebbero lavorato assieme, e con lena raddoppiata, pel trionfo della Libertà. Egli aveva acceso una fiamma che la faceva maggiore di sé stessa... si provasse a spegnerla, se glie ne bastava l'animo, se ne aveva la forza!"

    Il conte pensava: "Benedetta la bocca rosea che vince l'eloquenza di tanti martiri e vivi e morti!" Tuttavia insistette:

    "No, Elisa! Il cospiratore è solitario anche in seno alla famiglia. Egli deve, talvolta, dimenticare d'avere una madre e moglie e figli. Se la sua ora predestinata arriva, chiude le orecchie alla loro preghiera, non vede le lagrime e parte... parte senza confidare i suoi segreti neppure all'aria che respira. Se quell'ora viene—"

    — Un ultimo abbraccio, uno schianto del cuore, fors'anche: ma nessuna domanda indiscreta. Non sono io certa che tu nulla puoi compiere di cui io debba arrossire!

    Quando la signorina Sara rientrò colle chicchere e col latte, l'innamorato che poco prima voleva fuggire lontano, lontano, aveva un ginocchio a terra. Strette, nelle sue, le mani d'Elisa, le baciava, le baciava... Il 9 Dicembre 1855 veniva legalizzata una fiamma sulla quale la sventura si affrettò a soffiare ma invano! Le forti vicissitudini di oltre mezzo secolo, non che spegnerla, dovevano più e più sempre ringagliardirla!

    Source pp. 130–135Scan to view on another deviceLOVE!... for Miss Sara or for Miss Elisa? The problem, however simple it might appear, stirred within him a singular dismay, followed by a no less singular irritation. “Why torment oneself to resolve it,” he concluded, “when the settling down into marriage is denied me by other ideals?” The Count di Rudio was acting unconsciously, and in a different arena, much as a man acts before new ideas when prejudice has taken root in his brain. Such a man intuits the reasonableness of those ideas, yet — so it is! — he obstinately closes his eyes to the truth, and the very certainty that his idol is crumbling makes him a yet more strenuous champion of old fables and falsehood.

    And now — here is how the Count di Rudio’s long drought of purse came at last to an end.

    From the day on which the coup d’état had triumphed definitively, Victor Hugo, having taken the road of exile, had settled on the island of Jersey. There he published L’Homme, a periodical whose pages blazed forth, with all the intensity lent by genius, the condemnation of the baleful imperial politics. The hero of the Second of December, keenly sensitive to the pricks of criticism, as every criminal who aspires to glory must be, contrived to lodge his complaint adroitly with the Cabinet in London, which, with equal adroitness, fended it off. But when the complications of European politics pointed out to England, in Napoleon III, the sovereign best suited to lend her a strong hand in the meditated enterprise of breaking the Czar’s pride, to humor the whims of that man presented itself as an act of wise diplomacy. And the freedom of thought and the traditional broad hospitality of the English people were, at least in part, curtailed and sacrificed to the true, or supposed, interests of the Nation.

    Victor Hugo was surrounded by various brilliant minds, to whose company Pianciani now attached himself. That nest of intellectuals was suddenly thrown into confusion. And the thing seemed such a sacrilege that even the French reactionaries dared not applaud.

    Pianciani resolved to repair once again to London. Before leaving the island, he dispatched, together with his other papers, the manuscript of his Storia dei Papi, insured for ten thousand pounds sterling. The papers arrived at their destination; the manuscript vanished. And rumor ran that the Jesuits had taken possession of it, most probably to cast it into the flames. A certain color of truth was lent to this rumor by the fact that the Insurance Company disbursed the considerable indemnity immediately, without initiating the least inquiry, without uttering a single word.

    Pianciani and the Count di Rudio met and conferred, and that Roman gentleman, who by his expansive warmth and his generous impulses made men forgive the disfigurement of his face — smallpox had blunted his nose — received the gentleman from Belluno as an equal among equals. At parting, and almost by the way, the conversation fell upon the latter’s financial straits.

    “You could not have come to me at a better moment!” said Pianciani. “I have set myself to attempt the Storia dei Papi anew. Will you be my amanuensis?”

    The man accustomed to the boldness, the labors, the perils of action was confined to an armchair, and made a virtue of what he had always accounted a fatal defect — patience. In recompense, he was brought into contact with those men luminous in genius, in character, in sufferings borne with virile fortitude who frequented the house of Pianciani: rebels, persecuted, slandered, without roof, without bread, unvanquished — great in their loves, their hatreds, their utopias. Noble and proud company!

    Most of them had one fixed idea that seemed almost to stamp them. They endeavored to convince, to draw the young amanuensis into their orbit.

    Gemini Prothe foolishness of his PatriaStandardises the translation of 'Patria' to 'Fatherland' throughout, fixing the inconsistency flagged in the Claude evaluationPianciani discerned in the troubles massing in the East the probable salvation of Italy. Liberolle, the historian, dreamed of the union of the Latin peoples, but under the predominance of France. Victor Hugo could not make his peace with the foolishness of his Fatherland, dazzled by the equivocal splendor of the Court of Napoleon the Little. He had lost all faith in the virtue of a People insensible to great ideals, ready to shoot and to be shot for the lying enchantment of fatuous phrases. And his every outburst closed invariably with the bitter sentence: “The people is a great child that wishes to be deceived!”

    Gemini Prothe brigands who trafficked in their PatriaSame Patria/Fatherland consistency fix as aboveAnd amid those modern figures there passed, now mild and smiling, now flashing with sinister light, the phantoms of History. Nineteen centuries of glories and of squalors went filing across those brief pages, renewing in the Count’s heart the shuddering, the grief, the wrath of the generations that sleep as dust. Yet in his mind, grown as though swollen with that vast entanglement, there arose, from hour to hour, two simple and smiling figures that stirred him more profoundly than genius; that stripped emperors and pontiffs of every ornament and thrust them back into their vulgarity. They appeared to him constantly adorned with roses, the fair flower that is the emblem of his family, and a sense of serenity pervaded and abided with him: a sense that tempered the stench of Vatican orgies; that stripped the horror from the maledictions which, from every corner of Italy, from every corner of Europe, had ever and always been raised against the brigands who trafficked in their Fatherland, in humanity, in Christ.

    The Count di Rudio no longer debated them; they had become as part of himself. Not that his ideas were, in this regard, clear and well defined. Love, not forgetful of its accustomed arts, still dissembled its own vigor behind the shelter of other affections more anciently rooted and in full flower. But he was persuaded, by now, of having found upon his path the beautiful, the good, the chaste, wondrously made flesh. To behold them, to drink in their radiance, to be elevated — to feel himself less unhappy! He surrendered to the wholly novel sensation without the least misgiving. When the revolutionary tempest seized him again, the festival of feeling would be brusquely cut short; but he would carry into the greater trials a richer treasury of dear remembrances — noble affections lend wings to reach the arduous heights!

    And he yearned, with an ever more intense anxiety, for the day when, having received the reward of his labors, he might present himself with head held high before Signora Mancherini, pay his debts like a man of honor, resume the course of the interrupted lessons; drink again from that font of the beautiful, the good, the chaste, drawing his two young friends ever more deeply into the cult of martyrs and heroes!

    Gemini ProMiss Elisa contrived once more to invoke the mysterious stranger'brought up' is a more natural rendering of 'tirò ancora in ballo' (literally 'dragged into the dance again'); 'contrived to invoke' over-translatesThe day came. With what light a step he covered the long road, with what eagerness he mounted the stair! Mamma Mancherini stepped forward and intercepted the threshold with her ample and flourishing person. Whereupon he, extending his right hand without delay — and the sterling — poured forth his apologies for the involuntary delay. But the lady looked at him with a suspicious air. The two young girls came running in, and the Count di Rudio learned, to his great astonishment, that the rent had been paid at the end of every month. Miss Elisa brought up once more the mysterious stranger, and Sara affirmed that she had never delivered the proper receipts to the Signor Conte, because the Signor Conte had not let himself be seen for a great while. But the portly Mancherini appeared not altogether satisfied, and went off with a frown, muttering: “Oh, what a muddle, what a muddle!” In those days the Crimean War was dragging on, limping forward. From the very first manifestation of the troubles that led to that war, Giuseppe Mazzini had proposed making an appeal to the unitary sentiment of Germany; of seconding the national aspirations of Italy and Hungary; of sending a breath of new life through the Balkans; of forming, to put it briefly, around the Muscovite Empire, a great rampart of free States that would have compressed and paralyzed Russia.

    This conception was not displeasing to the Cabinet of Turin. But Napoleon III and England were aiming to provoke no hostility from Austria, and indeed to induce her to participate actively in their alliance. And it was precisely because Austria would never have resolved to enter the campaign so long as the terrible hornets’ nest of Italy hung over her neck, that the co-operation of the House of Savoy was accepted with good grace, was in fact solicited. The Piedmontese army was the only strongly organized nucleus; the only one deemed capable of precipitating events in our Peninsula.

    Austria would not be moved and thus spared the Italians the humiliation of seeing the heroes of our independence fight side by side with the Croatian hordes, to maintain upon the European Continent “a sorry projection of Asia.”

    And the meditated great uprising of peoples was reduced, in practice, to a simple, long, and tedious siege. England, shaken in her military prestige, short of soldiers, resolved to open enlistments, to organize some foreign legions.

    No sooner was the appropriate decree promulgated than di Rudio found Fumagalli and Leoni presented before him. The latter had decided to depart for the Crimea and had come to pay a farewell visit. “And if it be for the last time,” he said, pressing the Count’s hand, “then for the last time — farewell!”

    They went to pass the few remaining hours before departure at the Public House of a certain Shaw, wife of a fellow-countryman of theirs. It was precisely the gathering-place of the recruits. The setting, with a parade-ground elegance, was already exceptionally animated — a confused animation, full of starts and bursts of laughter. Military men and civilians, in clusters, were slapping one another on the shoulder, exchanging nicknames and hearty, good-natured epithets. In secluded corners, a few gentle figures in bonnets, with pale and tear-stained faces. Flowers and green sprigs on every breast; bluish spirals of cigarette smoke from almost every mouth. But the noise and confusion did not suit di Rudio’s temper; the more so because the sight of so many Italians on the point of shedding their blood for a cause he held foreign to the Fatherland put him in mind of the woeful days of the soldati di ventura — soldiers of fortune. And when Leoni, half in earnest, half in jest, said to him: “Come now, Moretto! Make up your mind and embark with us!” he answered, with excessive bluntness: “What harm have the Russians done you, that you should offer your youth as a holocaust, with the intention of swelling their number of widows and orphans?”

    And Leoni: “Italian colors are flying in the Crimea!”

    — Any flag is a mockery when it does not stand as a symbol of truth and justice!

    The conversation was taking a bad turn. Fortunately a Milanese conscript, picking up the thread of a previous discussion, expressed his regret at not having thought to bring with him I Sepolcri by Ugo Foscolo, which would have contributed so greatly to shortening the hours of the crossing.

    — I have a copy at home, di Rudio told him. — Accept it. It is an inexpensive edition, with the margins full of scribbles, but they do no harm to the substance.

    In four bounds he was at Baldwin’s Garden. He mounted the stairs and entered his room, leaving the door ajar. He was already turning the chest of drawers upside down when, from the other side of the corridor, there reached him distinctly the sounds of moaning and sobbing. What could this mean?

    Gemini ProThe mysterious stranger was herself. It was Elisa who provided out of her own savingsCorrects a factual mistranslation. Italian 'Lo straniero misterioso è là che si dispera' means 'The mysterious stranger is there, despairing' — Sara is pointing toward Elisa in the other room. Claude's 'was herself' invents an identification not in the source text.Going to the threshold, he found himself face to face with Miss Sara, who, flushing briefly and without waiting to be questioned: “Elisa is weeping because she is persuaded that you are leaving tonight for the Crimea. Love is consuming her. And she has also compromised herself in Mamma’s eyes. It was that business of the receipts — do you remember? Mamma has drawn everything from her heart, and the least that awaits her now is a thorough scolding. The mysterious stranger is there, in despair. It was Elisa who provided out of her own savings — for everything, everything!”

    Gemini ProThe Count di Rudio turned the great poet over and over in his handsRestores the verb 'strinse' (gripped/squeezed) which Claude omitted. The Italian has four actions — strinse, girò e rigirò e capovolse — and the grip conveys di Rudio's emotional agitation. Gemini had 'squeezed'; 'gripped' better suits the physical gesture with a book.The Count di Rudio gripped the great poet, turned him over and over in his hands, then thrust him furiously into the bottom of his pocket: “Tell Miss Elisa that the di Rudios are not soldiers of fortune. For Liberty — always! Mercenaries of foreign despots — never!” And he descended the stairs at a run. But the tempest that Miss Sara’s words had roused followed him into the street; entered, through the crack of the door, into Mrs. Shaw’s Public House, and swirled about his head the whole evening — so much so that the talk and the literature and the politics and the noise and the chaotic scene of the embarkation and the farewells all reached his brain not as vivid and clearly defined reality, but almost as a pale evocation of images emerging confusedly from among the mists of times gone by.

    A second surprise awaited him. Making his way home toward one o’clock, he glimpsed, seated on the landing, the figure of a woman in a white dress — in the darkness she seemed to glow with a faint phosphorescence. A moment of stupefaction seized him... and she rose and, retreating, seemed to vanish. He would have taken her for a phantom, had not a door creaked. Miss Elisa had passed a great part of the night keeping watch, in tears perhaps, to assure herself that the sea and the war had not taken him from her.

    And di Rudio, in the tumult that threatened to overwhelm him, clung desperately to this resolve: “Before afternoon, undeceive her and flee!” With that resolution he went to bed; thereupon the thoughts of the night, inspired by the painful remembrance of so many martyrs both living and dead, made him yet more stubborn. When he presented himself, rigid and self-possessed, before the two young girls, Elisa greeted him with: “Are you unwell? You are pale this morning!”

    — It seems to me that you are both pale! the cousin observed. And she offered to prepare a cup of warm milk — Mamma too would be glad of some when she came in. As she reached the threshold she turned and, smiling: “Do not be foolish. Every moment lost is stolen from your happiness, and by forever hiding from each other you might end by never finding each other again. You love each other, you were born one for the other — what more is needed?” And she was gone. The decisive moment had come. Elisa was nervously twisting one of her ribbons, and di Rudio watched her, keeping at a respectful distance. He began by thanking her for all that she had done for him. Any denial was useless — Sara had confided everything to him. The misfortune was that he could demonstrate his gratitude only in words.

    And he went on to recount the sad vicissitudes to which one must submit who champions a holy and persecuted cause. With what conscience could he have dared to offer her his hand, to drag her into his tumultuous life, into his poverty? Where find for her a nest that would not, sooner or later, be tainted and destroyed by the fury of men? Would it be wise, humane, honest to reserve an environment haunted by the terror of conspiracies, the snares of arms, the clank of chains, the dread of blood, for children born to perpetuate the gentle virtues of their mother? Infancy yearns for the kisses of smiling lips; it needs to mirror in its candid soul eyes that are serene. And what else could he say to her; what happiness could he promise, without resorting to mendacious phrases? He had chosen, between two evils, the lesser; he had resolved to withdraw — for ever. He implored her to interpret his resolution with that rectitude of feeling that had suggested it to him; to preserve a kindly memory of him.

    Here, and here alone, Miss Elisa raised her face and looked him in the eyes. With the acuteness of simple souls, she knew how to sound a soul. She expressed herself as a creature conscious of the power to dispose of her own destiny, with that calm and chaste eloquence which to some of my readers, accustomed to exalting “the opulence of the hip and the quiverings of the flesh,” might seem exhumed from some museum of archaeology... Other times, other men, other loves!

    Gemini Prolike the ancient Roman women of oldItalian 'come le sue antiche romane' — 'sue' (his) is significant: Elisa means the Roman women of his heritage. Claude's 'of old' is redundant with 'ancient' and loses the possessive.Gemini ProThe love he bore his Patria did not make her jealousSame Patria/Fatherland consistency fix applied throughout Elisa's speech“Toward a soul such as that of di Rudio she could advance without suspicion and without artifice: she would have preferred to see herself definitively rejected over becoming his through simulated virtue. There were Italian women — she was not ignorant of this — who had suppressed their serene aspirations, imposed solitude and silence upon their hearts, so as not to become wives and mothers in calamitous times. She admired their austerity, but did not feel that she could approve of it. The wicked rear the wicked freely, and should the good withdraw themselves from the duty of rearing those who shall perpetuate honest traditions? The surroundings that seemed unfavorable to cultivating, in tender hearts, the gentle virtues he supposed her to possess, would favor the development of the virile paternal virtues. To temper virile characters is a mission that refines a mother’s pride. Or did he perhaps take her, like his ancient Roman women, as fit only to preside over the good order of the kitchen and to spin wool? The anguish of hearts that sweat blood, the terrible effort of muscles that swell to burst their chains, the tormented labor of intellects that seek everywhere for equity and light — were these things to have left her indifferent? She had a deep sympathy for the country he had painted for her so beautiful, so great, so unfortunate. The love he bore his Fatherland did not make her jealous — to distract him from his aims, to blunt his generous impulses? Precisely the contrary! They would work together, and with redoubled energy, for the triumph of Liberty. He had kindled a flame that made her greater than herself — let him try to extinguish it, if his heart were equal to it, if he had the strength!”

    The Count thought: “Blessed the rosy mouth that vanquishes the eloquence of so many martyrs, living and dead!” Nevertheless he persisted:

    “No, Elisa! The conspirator is alone even in the bosom of his family. He must at times forget that he has a mother, a wife, children. If his predestined hour arrives, he stops his ears to their prayer, he sees not the tears and he departs — departs without confiding his secrets even to the air he breathes. If that hour comes —”

    — One last embrace, a wrench of the heart, perhaps — but no indiscreet question. Am I not certain that you can do nothing of which I need be ashamed!

    When Miss Sara returned with the cups and the milk, the lover who but a short while before had wished to flee far, far away, had one knee on the ground. Holding Elisa’s hands clasped within his, he was kissing them, kissing them again. On the ninth of December, 1855, a flame was made lawful, upon which misfortune hastened to breathe — in vain! The hard vicissitudes of more than half a century, far from extinguishing it, were only to make it burn ever more brightly!

    Parte Seconda

    Capitolo Quarto

    Part Two

    Chapter Four

    LA parola al Conte di Rudio: L'anno stesso che mi accasai fui coinvolto in una malaugurata faccenda che fece molto chiasso. Gli esuli che non avevano dovizie di baiocchi, frequentavano certa trattoria di Rupert Street, ove per poco prezzo potevano assaporare qualche piatto cucinato all'usanza nostra. Più di una volta vi ero capitato anch'io e vi avevo stretto conoscenza con un Rossi mantovano, giovanotto che si acquistò presto le mie simpatie per il suo carattere espansivo ed allegro. Il Rossi era soverchiamente corrivo in fatto di relazioni amorose, ma altri vizi in lui non seppi riscontrare e quando il romagnolo Foschini mi avvertì, in tutta confidenza, di stare alla larga da quel "brutto ceffo" perchè era una spia del Governo francese, io gli osservai che il prestare leggermente fede alle calunnie insinuate, con tutta probabilità, per fini loschi, dai nemici del nostro partito, era un prestarsi puerilmente al loro giuoco.

    Il Foschini, imbronciato, mi voltò le spalle ed io... strinsi col Rossi maggiore intimità... un pochino anche per l'istinto, in me prepotente, di voler veder chiaro, sino al fondo delle cose. Ebbi a convincermi che il Foschini era realmente in errore. Tale convinzione, a dir vero, non è oggigiorno tanto radicata. Un Rossi, come seppi vari anni dopo, era stato sinistramente implicato nelle congiure di Mantova. Tuttavia il cognome Rossi è molto comune nell'Alta Italia ed io non ebbi mai il modo di accertare se la mia conoscenza di Londra e la spia del Governo austriaco siano l'identica persona. Per chi si interessi d'indagare queste minuzie, dirò che il Rossi di

    Londra aveva un fratello prete... circostanza che potrebbe servire di guida a stabilire la verità!

    Una sera, ad ora già inoltrata, mi trovavo nell'osteria di via Rupert in compagnia del "brutto ceffo". C'era anche il Foschini, il quale sedeva tutto solo, in disparte; ma dall'occhiatacce che ci aveva rivolte e da certi monosillabi biascicati, avevo potuto arguire che si correva il rischio di navigare in acque torbide.

    Gli avventori diradarono e rimanemmo quasi soli. Ad un certo punto, il Foschini si alzò e, passando stecchito d'accanto al Rossi, lo urtò nel gomito e gli disse: "Spia!"
